The seed feature is for interactive / dev use, not the normative test sequence. Use an empty spec (no examples) for the smoke test fixture.

## Files
- `src/protocol/rest/seed.rs`

## Done when
- Boot with a spec containing schema-level examples seeds those rows into the backend
- No examples present → listener starts without error and logs the "no examples" message
- Seed failures (type mismatch in example) → startup warning logged, boot continues, twin is live
- Resource with no PK → all examples inserted, startup warning logged, no panic
- Duplicate PK example → first-seen wins, second skipped with debug log
- `seed_from_spec` is called AFTER backend is initialized with empty tables (integration test verifies that a GET after boot returns seeded data)

title: REST report + canary assertions
description:
Depends on bd-12o.4 (listener must be complete so request recording is real). Wave 5 — runs in parallel with bd-12o.9.

## Changes

### Session recording (`src/protocol/rest/session_log.rs`)
The listener records each request into a `RestSessionLog` that accumulates across all requests for the session lifetime:
- Method + path + matched route (or "unmatched" if no RouteEntry matched)
- Response status code
- Duration in milliseconds
- Whether the request triggered a constraint violation, and which type (unique, not_null, check, type_coercion)
- Whether the request was a refusal (unsupported_shape, not_found, validation_error)

### Report extension (`src/report.rs`)
The REST report is a SEPARATE artifact from the existing Postgres `twinning.v0` report. Do NOT add a `rest_session` block to the existing Postgres report schema — that would create a mixed-protocol artifact. Instead:

The `--report` flag on `twinning rest` writes a `twinning.rest-report.v0` artifact (separate from `twinning.v0`):
```json
{
  "version": "twinning.rest-report.v0",
  "outcome": "PASS" | "FAIL" | "REFUSAL",
  "spec": {
    "source": "api.yaml",
    "hash": "sha256:...",
    "resource_count": 5,
    "route_count": 12,
    "security_schemes_bypassed": ["BearerAuth"]
  },
  "session": {
    "request_count": 12,
    "endpoints_exercised": ["POST /files", "GET /files/{id}"],
    "endpoints_not_exercised": ["DELETE /files/{id}"],
    "constraint_violations": { "unique": 1, "not_null": 0, "type_coercion": 2 },
    "refusals": { "unsupported_shape": 0, "not_found": 1 }
  },
  "canary": {
    "total": 4, "passed": 3, "failed": 1,
    "failures": [{ "assertion": "endpoint_called", "expected": "DELETE /files/{id}", "detail": "never called" }]
  },
  "next_step": "..."
}
```

### Canary manifest (REST variant)
A `RestCanaryManifest` is an optional JSON file the operator can pass via `--canary <FILE>`. It declares behavioral expectations evaluated against the session log at report time:
- `endpoint_called`: assert method+path was exercised at least once
- `status_returned`: assert a specific status code was returned for a route
- `constraint_triggered`: assert a constraint violation type was triggered at least once
- `refusal_issued`: assert a refusal code was returned

`outcome` at the report level is `PASS` if all canary assertions pass (or if no canary is provided), `FAIL` if any assertion fails.

### Report outcome when no canary provided
If no `--canary` file is given, outcome is `PASS` unless the twin encountered a process-level error. The `canary` block is omitted from the report.

Add `schemas/twinning.rest-report.v0.schema.json`.

## Files
- `src/protocol/rest/session_log.rs`
- `src/protocol/rest/canary.rs`
- `src/report.rs` (additive only — add REST report serialization without touching Postgres report structs)
- `schemas/twinning.rest-report.v0.schema.json`

## Done when
- Session log is populated after a request sequence
- REST report JSON is emitted separately from the Postgres report and is valid against its schema
- Canary assertions evaluate correctly against a known session log (unit test: one passing assertion, one failing assertion)
- `--canary` flag is accepted by `RestArgs` (requires update to bd-12o.1 — add this flag to `RestArgs` if not already present)

## RestSessionLog placement — separate Arc
The `RestSessionLog` must NOT be inside the `BaseSnapshotBackend` mutex — mixing session recording with data storage creates a single bottleneck and makes the log inaccessible after backend shutdown. Use a separate `Arc<Mutex<RestSessionLog>>` passed alongside the backend through the listener's shared state:
```rust
struct RestSharedState {
    backend: Arc<std::sync::Mutex<BaseSnapshotBackend>>,
    session_log: Arc<std::sync::Mutex<RestSessionLog>>,
}
```
Both fields use `std::sync::Mutex` — NOT `tokio::sync::Mutex`. The kernel code that accesses the backend is synchronous; `tokio::sync::Mutex` would require `.await` to lock and cannot be used in sync contexts without `blocking_lock()`. Consistent with bd-12o.4's mutex type decision. The listener (bd-12o.4) holds `Arc<RestSharedState>` as its axum extension. Both fields are locked independently.

## Spec hash — produced by bd-12o.2
The `twinning.rest-report.v0` schema includes `spec.hash: "sha256:..."`. The spec parser (bd-12o.2) hashes the raw spec file bytes (not the parsed struct) and attaches the hash to `RestCatalog.spec_hash: String`. The report writer reads it from there.

title: Integration smoke test — metadata API v2
description:
Depends on bd-12o.4 (listener complete) and bd-12o.8 (report + canary complete). Wave 5 — the end-to-end gate for the whole epic.

## Changes

### Fixture
Drop a minimal OpenAPI spec into `tests/fixtures/rest/minimal-api.yaml`. Use a hand-crafted minimal spec rather than the live cmdrvl metadata API spec to avoid external dependencies in CI. The minimal spec must exercise the same parsing paths the real spec uses:
- At least two resource schemas: one flat (e.g., `File` with `id`, `name`, `size` fields), one with a `$ref` (e.g., `Upload` that `$ref`s `File`)
- At least one `allOf` composition in a schema
- Full CRUD paths for the primary resource (`File`): `GET /files`, `POST /files`, `GET /files/{id}`, `PATCH /files/{id}`, `DELETE /files/{id}`
- One security scheme declared (e.g., `BearerAuth`) — not enforced, but present to test the bypass logging path

### Fixture sanity check
Document at the top of the test file:
- Primary resource chosen: `File` (non-composite PK field `id`, required fields `id` and `name`)
- Required field to omit in step 7: `name`
- Field to trigger type mismatch in step 8: send a string for `size` (declared `integer`)

### Test sequence (`tests/rest/metadata_api_smoke.rs`)
Boot the REST twin with the fixture spec on an ephemeral port (port 0 — OS-assigned):
1. `POST /files` with `{"id": 1, "name": "foo.txt", "size": 1024}` → assert 201, body contains `id: 1`
2. `GET /files/1` → assert 200, body matches posted data
3. `PATCH /files/1` with `{"name": "bar.txt"}` → assert 200, `name` field updated
4. `GET /files/1` → assert 200, `name == "bar.txt"`
5. `DELETE /files/1` → assert 204, no body
6. `GET /files/1` → assert 404
7. `POST /files` missing required `name` field → assert 422
8. `POST /files` with `{"id": 2, "name": "baz.txt", "size": "not-an-int"}` → assert 400 (type mismatch)

### Report assertions
After the sequence, shut down the twin and read the `twinning.rest-report.v0` artifact:
- `request_count == 8`
- All exercised paths appear in `session.endpoints_exercised`
- At least one refusal recorded in `session.refusals`
- Report is valid JSON and contains required top-level fields (`version`, `outcome`, `spec`, `session`)

### Doctor check
Also run `twinning doctor health` and assert it returns a valid JSON health response (smoke-test that the REST module did not break the shared doctor path).

## Files
- `tests/rest/metadata_api_smoke.rs`
- `tests/rest/run_mode.rs`
- `tests/fixtures/rest/minimal-api.yaml`

## Done when
- `cargo test rest` passes end to end on CI
- All 8 request steps return the expected status codes
- Report assertions all pass
- Doctor health check still works

## Test helper pattern — `start_test_server` signature
`start_test_server` is a **synchronous** function — it spawns the listener thread internally and returns once the server is listening:
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn smoke_test() {
    let config = RestConfig { spec_path: fixture_path(), host: "127.0.0.1".into(), port: 0, serve: true, ..Default::default() };
    let (addr, shutdown_tx) = twinning::protocol::rest::listener::start_test_server(config);
    // addr is the actual bound address (OS-assigned port)
    let resp = reqwest::get(format!("http://{addr}/files")).await.unwrap();
    shutdown_tx.send(()).unwrap();
}
```
The function signature is `start_test_server(config: RestConfig) -> (SocketAddr, std::sync::mpsc::Sender<()>)` — no `.await`, no tokio oneshot. The test itself is `#[tokio::test]` because HTTP client calls need async, but `start_test_server` is not async. Shutdown is via `std::sync::mpsc::Sender<()>`.

If bd-12o.4 does not export this signature exactly, bd-12o.9 cannot be implemented. Verify the signature matches before writing test code.

## `--run` lifecycle test (`tests/rest/run_mode.rs`)
Add a test for the one-shot mode:
- Start the twin with `--run "curl -s http://127.0.0.1:{port}/files"`
- Assert the child command exits 0
- Assert the report file is written and contains `request_count: 1`
- Assert `twinning rest` exits 0
This is the only test verifying the one-shot lifecycle end-to-end.

title: REST child singleton routes marked ok fail with two path parameters
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Twilio Fax OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/twilio.com/twilio_fax_v1/1.29.1/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/twilio.com/twilio_fax_v1/1.29.1/openapi.json -o /tmp/twilio_fax.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/twilio_fax.json --port 18139
python3 /tmp/twilio_fax_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18139
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18139/v1/Faxes/FX11111111111111111111111111111111/Media/ME22222222222222222222222222222222'
curl -i -X DELETE 'http://127.0.0.1:18139/v1/Faxes/FX11111111111111111111111111111111/Media/ME22222222222222222222222222222222'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST twin v0 requires exactly one path parameter for resource `fax.v1.fax.fax_medias`"}
```

Expected response/output:
The startup routing table marks these child singleton media item routes as `ok`, so runtime should either execute them with the child lookup key (`Sid`) plus parent scope (`FaxSid`) and return 404/object/204, or the classifier should mark them as a precise unsupported nested/child resource shape. It should not advertise the route as executable and then fail normalization because it sees two path parameters.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /v1/Faxes/{FaxSid}/Media/{Sid} status: status=501, expected=[200, 404]
FAIL GET /v1/Faxes/{FaxSid}/Media/{Sid} response shape: status=501, body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', 'detail': 'REST twin v0 requires exactly one path parameter for resource `fax.v1.fax.fax_medias`'}
FAIL DELETE /v1/Faxes/{FaxSid}/Media/{Sid} status: status=501, expected=[204, 404]
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/topology.rs` can classify the child singleton path as schema-first/medium/ok, but `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs::pk_predicate()` has a hard requirement that singleton routes carry exactly one path parameter. Child singleton routes naturally have parent and child path parameters (`FaxSid`, `Sid`). The classifier and normalizer disagree about whether this route shape is executable in v0. A fix should either derive the child lookup predicate from the terminal path param while treating the parent param as scope, or classify this relationship as an explicit unsupported nested route before normalization.

title: bd-mcp.3: MCP HTTP listener — Axum server wiring auth, dispatch, startup report, and CLI
description:
Create `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/listener.rs` and wire the complete `twinning mcp` subcommand. This bead reuses the REST twin's Axum/Tokio infrastructure and auth module directly.

## McpConfig

```rust
pub struct McpConfig {
    pub source: McpCatalogInput,  // LiveServer(command) | Manifest(path)
    pub host: String,             // default 127.0.0.1
    pub port: u16,                // default 9878
    pub auth_mode: RestAuthMode,  // reuse from super::super::auth — Bypass | Shape
    pub run_command: Option<String>,
    pub report_path: Option<PathBuf>,
    pub json: bool,
}
```

## McpSharedState

```rust
struct McpSharedState {
    catalog: Arc<McpCatalog>,
    auth_mode: RestAuthMode,
    session_log: Arc<Mutex<McpSessionLog>>,
}
```

McpSessionLog: track request count, method counts, refusal count, tool call outcomes (stubbable hits vs unsupported_shape). Same lightweight pattern as RestSessionLog.

## Axum handler

Single route: `POST /` (also accept `POST /message` and `POST /mcp` as aliases).

Handler flow:
1. Read body bytes (same MAX_REQUEST_BYTES = 1MB limit as REST)
2. Check auth: call `check_auth(&headers, &catalog.security_schemes, auth_mode)` — reusing `crates/twinning-rest/src/auth.rs::check_auth` directly. If auth missing/malformed: return 401 with JSON-RPC error envelope (-32600, "unauthorized").
3. Parse body as JsonRpcRequest. If parse fails: return 400 with JSON-RPC error -32700.
4. Call `dispatch(request, &catalog)` from dispatcher.rs.
5. If None (notification): return 204 No Content.
6. Serialize response and return 200 application/json.

Note: MCP error responses still return HTTP 200 (JSON-RPC error semantics, not HTTP error semantics) — this is per MCP spec.

## Server lifecycle

Follow listener.rs pattern exactly:
- `fn start_server(config: &McpConfig) -> RefusalResult<RunningMcpServer>`
- `fn shutdown_and_join(server: RunningMcpServer) -> RefusalResult<Arc<McpSharedState>>`
- `pub fn run(config: McpConfig) -> Result<Execution, Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`
  - if run_command is None: start server, wait for signal, shutdown, emit report
  - if run_command is Some: start server, run child with BASE_URL env, shutdown, emit report

## McpReport (startup report)

```json
{
  "version": "twinning.mcp-report.v0",
  "outcome": "PASS",
  "catalog": {
    "source": "live_server | manifest",
    "server_name": "...",
    "server_version": "...",
    "catalog_hash": "...",
    "tool_count": N,
    "resource_count": N,
    "prompt_count": N,
    "tools_stubbable": N,
    "tools_unsupported": N
  },
  "session": {
    "request_count": N,
    "refusal_count": N,
    "tool_calls": { "stubbable": N, "unsupported": N }
  },
  "next_step": "Inspect tool executability. Tools with no output schema return unsupported_shape — add outputSchema to the MCP server definition to enable stub execution."
}
```

Also emit a human-readable startup table to stderr (non-json mode):
```
MCP twin ready — 12 tools (8 stubbable, 4 unsupported), 3 resources, 2 prompts
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:9878
```

## CLI wiring

Add to `src/cli.rs` (gated by `#[cfg(feature = "mcp")]`):
```
twinning mcp --server <COMMAND>    # introspect live server
twinning mcp --manifest <FILE>     # load static catalog
  [--host <HOST>]                  # default 127.0.0.1
  [--port <PORT>]                  # default 9878
  [--auth-mode bypass|shape]       # default shape
  [--run <COMMAND>]                # run child with BASE_URL env
  [--report <FILE>]                # write McpReport JSON
  [--json]                         # machine-readable output
```

Add to `src/lib.rs`:
```rust
#[cfg(feature = "mcp")]
if let Some(cli::Command::Mcp(args)) = &cli.command {
    let execution = twinning_rest::mcp::listener::run(args.into())?;
    print!("{}", execution.stdout);
    return Ok(execution.exit_code);
}
```

## Files touched
- `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/listener.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/mod.rs` (add listener, report)
- `src/cli.rs` (add Mcp variant gated by mcp feature)
- `src/lib.rs` (add mcp dispatch block)
- `src/config.rs` (add mcp_config_from_cli if needed)

title: REST GET /Files/{FileId} refuses FileId path lookup alias
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Xero Files OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml -o /tmp/xero_files.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/xero_files.yaml --serve --port 18160
python3 /tmp/xero_files_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18160
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18160/Files/4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c
curl -i -X PUT http://127.0.0.1:18160/Files/4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"id\":\"4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c\",\"name\":\"File2.jpg\",\"manufacturer\":\"x\",\"releaseDate\":\"2026-01-01\",\"Id\":\"4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c\",\"Name\":\"File2.jpg\"}"
curl -i -X DELETE http://127.0.0.1:18160/Files/4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c
```

Actual response pattern:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"resource `fileobjects` has no primary key for path lookup"}
```

Expected response/output:
The route is classified `ok` at startup and `FileId` is the declared path identifier for `FileObject`. Empty state may return structured 404, and updates/deletes may return 404/no-op depending on kernel semantics, but these routes should not refuse with unsupported_shape merely because the response schema is named `FileObject` while the path parameter is `FileId`.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /Files/{FileId} path lookup: status=501 body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', 'detail': 'resource `fileobjects` has no primary key for path lookup'}
FAIL PUT /Files/{FileId} path lookup: status=501 body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', 'detail': 'resource `fileobjects` has no primary key for path lookup'}
FAIL DELETE /Files/{FileId} path lookup: status=501 body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', 'detail': 'resource `fileobjects` has no primary key for path lookup'}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs::pk_predicate()` falls into the no-primary-key branch for `fileobjects`. `src/protocol/rest/spec.rs::primary_key_for_resource()` only recognizes lower-case `id` or `<schema>_id`, but Xero uses property `Id`. Then `resource_column_for_path_param()` cannot map `FileId` to column `Id` because its prefix stripping is based on schema/resource prefixes (`file_object`, `fileobjects`) rather than the literal path resource prefix `File`. The normalizer should either infer case-insensitive `Id` as a primary key or map `FileId` to `Id` for this path lookup.

title: MCP seed JSONL contract export and v1 import refusal
description: Implement the smallest MCP seed JSONL v1 surface without pretending MCP has relational rows. V1 exports a deterministic LLM-fillable seed contract or seedability report from the MCP catalog, but seed-data import is explicitly refused for now. No fake data generation in twinning.
design: MCP catalog entries are tools, resources, and prompts, not tables. The v1 contract should list each entry with seedable=false and a reason unless the current code already has a durable fixture state path. Add CLI/config handling for seed flags if needed, but importing seed data should return a structured unsupported_shape refusal. This keeps MCP honest while preserving a contract artifact an external agent can inspect. Actual resource/tool fixture-state import moves to a backlog bead.
acceptance_criteria: Golden contract export for a tiny MCP manifest is stable and lists tools, resources, and prompts with seedability reasons. Importing a seed-data JSONL file returns a structured unsupported_shape refusal through both HTTP and stdio paths if both expose the flag. Existing schema-backed tool stubs continue to behave exactly as before. No in-memory fixture store or generated tool-output override is added in this v1 bead.

title: REST spec seeding rejects OpenAPI byte-format examples as invalid bytes
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public 1Password Connect OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/1password.local/connect/1.5.7/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/1password.local/connect/1.5.7/openapi.json -o /tmp/onepassword_connect.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/onepassword_connect.json --port 18088
```

Actual startup warning:
```text
Example `components.schemas.File.example` was skipped after kernel refusal `invalid_text_representation`: invalid input syntax for type bytes: VGhlIGZ1dHVyZSBiZWxvbmdzIHRvIHRoZSBjdXJpb3VzLgo=
Seeded 0 rows across 0 resource types from spec examples
```

Relevant schema excerpt:
```json
{
  "File": {
    "example": {
      "content": "VGhlIGZ1dHVyZSBiZWxvbmdzIHRvIHRoZSBjdXJpb3VzLgo=",
      "id": "6r65pjq33banznomn7q22sj44e",
      "name": "foo.txt",
      "size": 35
    },
    "properties": {
      "content": { "type": "string", "format": "byte" }
    },
    "type": "object"
  }
}
```

Expected response/output:
OpenAPI `type: string, format: byte` means a base64-encoded string. A valid base64 example should either seed as bytes or be skipped with a precise unsupported-byte-example warning before hitting the kernel. It should not be reported as `invalid input syntax for type bytes`, which is the Postgres-style hex bytea parser rejecting a valid OpenAPI byte string.

HTTP request/curl:
No HTTP request is needed; the failure is visible during startup before client traffic. The e2e client used for the campaign was `/tmp/onepassword_connect_client.py`.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:576-581` normalizes `format: byte` and `format: binary` to the kernel `bytes` type. `src/protocol/rest/seed.rs:516-519` then converts byte fields from JSON examples into `ScalarValue::Text` unchanged. During `execute_mutation`, kernel coercion for `ValueType::Bytes` expects Postgres bytea hex text via `parse_bytea_hex()` in `src/kernel/coerce.rs:106-162`, so a valid OpenAPI base64 byte string is rejected. The seed path should decode OpenAPI `format: byte` base64 into `ScalarValue::Bytes`, preserve binary as unsupported if needed, or issue a precise REST seed warning without invoking the wrong kernel text parser.
close_reason: Completed: REST seed decodes OpenAPI format: byte examples before kernel mutation.

title: REST topology misclassifies multi-parameter parent collections as singletons
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official GitHub REST API OpenAPI 3.x description, reduced to a repository route subset.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.yaml -o /tmp/github_api.yaml
# reduce to the repository subset used by the campaign, preserving recursively referenced components
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/github_repos_subset.yaml --serve --port 18141
python3 /tmp/github_repos_subset_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18141
```

Representative repro requests:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18141/repos/octocat/Hello-World/issues
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18141/repos/octocat/Hello-World/issues   -H 'Content-Type: application/json'   -d '{"title":"Found a bug"}'
```

Actual responses:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"resource `issues` has no path parameter matching primary key column `id`"}
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues"}
```

Expected response/output:
`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues` declares a top-level array of `issue` resources and should be classified/executed as a child collection read (`200 []` on empty state is fine). `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues` declares a create operation and should reach request validation/create semantics (201 for a valid body, or a precise validation/refusal), not route-level outside-CRUD.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL operation: GET /repos/octocat/Hello-World/issues: status=501, expected=200, body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', 'detail': 'resource `issues` has no path parameter matching primary key column `id`'}
FAIL operation: POST /repos/octocat/Hello-World/issues: status=501, expected=201, body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', 'detail': 'REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues'}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/topology.rs::path_shape_binding()` only recognizes child collections shaped like `/parent/{id}/children` and child singletons shaped like `/parent/{id}/children/{id}`. GitHub repository routes have a two-parameter parent identity, e.g. `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues`. Because that shape is not recognized, `auto_topology_entry()` falls into the `has_more_than_one_parameterized_segment()` schema-first branch around `src/protocol/rest/topology.rs:249-263` and defaults the relationship to `ChildSingleton` when no path relationship was found. That makes collection GET routes enter singleton PK lookup and makes collection POST routes route-refusals.

This is related to earlier parent-scoped routing work but is not a duplicate of the old generic-refusal bug: the post-bd-1xrt topology now classifies the route, but assigns the wrong relationship for multi-column parent scopes.

title: Add live Postgres reference execution to the parity ledger
description: docs/goal.md calls for a clean conformance harness against real Postgres for the declared subset, but the current parity ledger only compares twin observations to pinned fixture postgres_observed rows. Implement an optional live reference mode, gated by TWINNING_DIFF_POSTGRES_URL, that creates or resets a scratch schema/database, runs the same declared cases against real Postgres and the twin, compares rows/errors/sqlstate, and emits ledger metadata distinguishing pinned fixture mode from live reference mode. title: Synthetic API interaction dataset — web-scale LLM tool-use training data generated from twinned APIs
description: Run 100 different LLM agents against 1000 twinned APIs with 20 benchmark tasks each. Capture complete interaction trajectories: the full sequence of calls an agent makes to accomplish a task, including retries, corrections, auth failures, and eventual success or failure — not just (call, response) pairs. The dataset captures the full distribution of how LLMs navigate APIs across the space of tasks and API surfaces. This dataset does not currently exist. The inputs exist (OpenAPI specs are public, LLM APIs are accessible) but the controlled instrumented API surface has been missing. The twin closes that gap: infinitely patient, rate-limit-free, deterministic responses for any given input. What the dataset enables: fine-tuning LLMs on API navigation competence with ground-truth correct trajectories as the target; preference learning from trajectory comparison (single-shot correct completions preferred over trajectories with retries); tool-use benchmark evaluation where ground truth is task accomplishment against a contract-faithful API, not syntactically valid JSON generation; studying how different API design patterns (REST vs RPC-style, pagination strategies, auth schemes) affect LLM agent success rates. Smaller-scale first step: generate (tool_call, response) pairs across the full parameter space of a single spec — valid calls, invalid calls at each parameter, multi-step sequences — and use as few-shot examples in aibuildout agent prompts. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md section 4.

title: REST GET /projects refuses Asana inline data wrapper, yielding 0 executable routes
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Asana OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/asana.com/1.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/asana.com/1.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/asana_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/asana_openapi.json --serve --port 18193
```

Startup result:
```text
[rest] 0 routes ok (0 confidence) | 0 waterfall fallbacks | 167 refused
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18193/projects'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route uses an inline response object wrapper schema, which is not materialized as an executable REST resource in v0. Path: /projects"}
```

Expected response/output:
Asana uses the common response wrapper shape `{ "data": ... }`. For `GET /projects`, the 200 response schema is inline:
```json
{"type":"object","properties":{"data":{"type":"array","items":{"$ref":"#/components/schemas/ProjectCompact"}}}}
```
The REST twin should classify this as an executable collection route, likely `ReadMany` over `ProjectCompact`, and return an empty Asana-shaped wrapper such as `{"data":[]}` when no rows are seeded. At minimum, inline response wrappers that directly contain `$ref` resources should not force 0% route coverage for the whole spec.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL Asana route coverage not zero: non_refusal_responses=0, operations=167
STATUS_COUNTS
DELETE 501: 13
GET 501: 79
POST 501: 61
PUT 501: 14
SUMMARY operations=167 assertions=502 failures=1
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:563-570` converts otherwise generic refusals with inline object or wrapper responses into hard route refusals whenever there is no top-level `response_schema_ref`. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1008-1022` emits the inline response wrapper refusal. The classifier does not inspect inline response wrappers whose `data` property contains an array or object `$ref`, so schema-first routing cannot derive `ProjectCompact`/`ProjectResponse` resources from Asana's inline envelope style.

Priority: P2 because this is a missing feature/refusal that collapses a large real-world OpenAPI 3 spec to 0 executable routes without a crash.

title: REST spec parser emits object-without-properties warning for scalar schemas
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public World Time API OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/worldtimeapi.org/20210108/openapi.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/worldtimeapi.org/20210108/openapi.yaml -o /tmp/worldtime.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/worldtime.yaml --port 18085
```

Actual startup warnings include three copies of:
```text
Schema has type object but no properties; resource has zero fields.
```

The scalar schemas in this spec are:
```yaml
DateTimeTextResponse:
  type: string
ErrorTextResponse:
  type: string
ListTextResponse:
  type: string
```

Expected output:
Scalar component schemas should not be described as `type object`. The parser should either omit the zero-field resource warning for scalar response schemas or emit a precise warning such as `Schema has type string and is not materialized as a REST resource in v0`. Startup warnings should help distinguish real empty object resources from scalar/text response helper schemas.

HTTP request/curl:
No HTTP request is needed to reproduce; this is visible at startup before client traffic. The campaign client would only observe expected v0 route refusals for the non-CRUD time lookup endpoints.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:336-342` warns whenever `resolved.properties.is_empty()` and the schema is not `array`, but the message hard-codes `type object` without checking `schema_type_string(&resolved)`. For `type: string` schemas, this produces a false object/no-properties warning. The fix should branch on the actual schema type or suppress this warning for scalar component schemas.
close_reason: Completed scalar-schema warning fix; fmt, clippy, cargo test, and World Time startup repro passed.

title: LLM API navigation benchmark — standardized MMLU-equivalent evaluation for tool-use agent competence
description: Build the standardized benchmark for LLM API navigation competence. Structure: 500 benchmark tasks across 50 public APIs each with a twinned endpoint; tasks stratified by complexity (single call, multi-step workflow, error recovery, pagination, auth flow); scoring on task completion rate, call efficiency (calls per task), auth compliance (correct on first try), error handling quality (correct behavior on 429/503/404); public leaderboard where agents are submitted and scored. The twin fleet runs the benchmark — every agent submission gets the same deterministic API surface; score differences reflect agent competence not API availability or rate limits. Why this matters: current LLM tool-use evaluation asks whether the model generates valid JSON. The right question is whether the model can accomplish a real task against a real API surface. The twin makes this answerable at scale without real API access, real credentials, or real rate limits. This benchmark would be cited by every LLM lab claiming tool-use competence. CMD+RVL is positioned to build it: the twin exists, the e2e campaign infrastructure (spec download, client exercise, bead filing) is the benchmark infrastructure with a scoring layer added. Wrap the twin with a logging middleware recording every request with session ID and timestamp; instrument across multiple agents; extract trace metrics (spec-reading behavior, retry rate, parameter coverage, route efficiency, auth compliance rate). Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md section 5.

title: bd-auth.2: Auth credential extractor — check_auth() for bearer, basic, apiKey header/query/cookie
description: Wave 2 (after bd-auth.1). New file src/protocol/rest/auth.rs. pub fn check_auth(required: &[String], schemes: &[SecurityScheme], headers: &HeaderMap, query: &str) -> AuthExtract. AuthExtract: Present | Missing { scheme, location, header_name } | Malformed { scheme, detail }. Bearer/Basic: Authorization header with correct prefix. apiKey: named header, query param, or Cookie header. Any one required scheme satisfied → Present. Unit tests: bearer present/missing/malformed, apiKey header/query present/missing, public route always Present.

title: REST e2e campaign: GitHub REST API — scale, oneOf/anyOf, deep nesting
description: Autonomous REST e2e campaign against the GitHub REST API OpenAPI spec. Spec URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.json. Setup: curl -L --fail <URL> -o /tmp/github_openapi.json && cargo build && target/debug/twinning rest --spec /tmp/github_openapi.json --serve --port 18400. Write a Python client at /tmp/github_client.py that exercises 60+ operations across: orgs, repos, issues, pull_requests, commits, users. Test: singleton vs collection routing, 401 enforcement without auth header, deeply nested routes (3+ param path segments, e.g. /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/comments), response shape validation. Focus areas that stress twinning: oneOf/anyOf response schemas (GitHub uses extensively), very large spec scale (900+ ops startup table), mixed resource shapes where some have no primary key analog. File bugs as beads using br create with full repro steps, spec URL, actual/expected responses, root cause hypothesis. Coordinate via Agent Mail. Report: operations tested, assertions made, failures with detail.
close_reason:
Campaign executed through startup gates and stopped at confirmed pre-listen blockers.

Setup/results:
- Downloaded GitHub REST OpenAPI spec to /tmp/github_openapi.json from the requested URL.
- Exact setup failed before listen: E_OPENAPI_SPEC_TOO_LARGE, 12,515,925 bytes over the 10 MiB raw limit. Filed bd-8ovl.
- Minified semantic equivalent to /tmp/github_openapi.min.json, 6,704,885 bytes.
- Minified spec failed before listen: E_OPENAPI_SCHEMA_CONFLICT for webhook-fork.created_at allOf merge, oneOf vs string. Filed bd-1f2b.
- Wrote /tmp/github_client.py with 81 declared operations covering orgs, repos, issues, pulls, commits/git, users, and search. The client validates all operation declarations against the GitHub spec and is ready to run once startup blockers are fixed.
- GitHub spec currently declares no components.securitySchemes and no global security, so bearer/401 shape enforcement is not applicable for this downloaded spec version.

Assertions/gate:
- Client declaration validation: 81 operations, 0 declaration failures.
- Full requested Rust gate passed: cargo fmt --check && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo test.

No server-side route assertions could run because the twin never reached listen state.

title: REST GET /api/v1/ returns 404 for declared Kubernetes discovery endpoint
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Kubernetes core v1 OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/master/api/openapi-spec/v3/api__v1_openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/master/api/openapi-spec/v3/api__v1_openapi.json -o /tmp/kubernetes_core_v1_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/kubernetes_core_v1_openapi.json --serve --port 18250
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18250/api/v1/ \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer e2e'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
content-type: application/json

{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}
```

Expected response/output:
`GET /api/v1/` is a declared path in the spec. Its 200 response is `#/components/schemas/io.k8s.apimachinery.pkg.apis.meta.v1.APIResourceList`, with required `groupVersion` and `resources` fields. A fresh twin should either return a spec-shaped discovery response (for example an empty `resources` list with a groupVersion) or refuse the endpoint precisely as unsupported/non-CRUD. It should not return a generic resource-missing 404, because the path exists and this is not a singleton lookup miss.

Related same-class repro for watch streams:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18250/api/v1/watch/configmaps \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer e2e'
```
Actual: the same `404 {"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}` even though the spec declares a 200 `WatchEvent` stream/object response.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
collection_get_status: 16
  - GET /api/v1/: collection GET expected 200/501, got 404, body=b'{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}'
  - GET /api/v1/watch/configmaps: collection GET expected 200/501, got 404, body=b'{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}'
  - GET /api/v1/watch/pods: collection GET expected 200/501, got 404, body=b'{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}'
SUMMARY operations=242 assertions=300 failures=52
```

Root cause hypothesis:
Schema-first routing currently accepts non-resource/static endpoints as normal read routes. For a path with no path parameters, `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:282-315` normalizes `ReadOne` to a filtered scan with `limit=1`. When the backend has no row for the synthetic response resource, `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:215-217` emits the generic `not_found` envelope. Non-CRUD discovery and watch-stream endpoints need either a dedicated static/list-stream response path or an early `unsupported_shape` route refusal instead of falling through to singleton empty-state encoding.

Priority: P2 because this is a declared endpoint returning the wrong status/refusal class rather than an explicit unsupported-shape response.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Fix note: schema-first/static GET routes that are non-CRUD discovery-style object responses, plus OpenAPI watch/stream paths, now classify as explicit 501 unsupported_shape route refusals before backend reads. Repro now returns 501 unsupported_shape for GET /api/v1/ and GET /api/v1/watch/configmaps; Kubernetes campaign client is down to the pre-existing HEAD body false positives only (6). Gate passed: cargo fmt --check && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo test.

title: REST item update returns 200 empty body when target row is missing
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Google Ad Exchange Buyer OpenAPI 3.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/googleapis.com/adexchangebuyer/v1.2/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/googleapis.com/adexchangebuyer/v1.2/openapi.json -o /tmp/google_adexchangebuyer.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/google_adexchangebuyer.json --port 18085
```

Repro request 1:
```bash
curl -i -X PUT 'http://127.0.0.1:18085/accounts/999' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"id":999,"cookieMatchingUrl":"https://example.test/match"}'
```

Actual response 1:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{}
```

Repro request 2:
```bash
curl -i -X PATCH 'http://127.0.0.1:18085/accounts/999' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"id":999,"cookieMatchingUrl":"https://example.test/match"}'
```

Actual response 2:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{}
```

The row is not created or updated:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18085/accounts/999'
```
returns:
```http
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
content-type: application/json

{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}
```

Expected response:
For item-resource REST updates, a missing target should not be reported as a successful empty update. The conservative v0 behavior should be `404 Not Found` with the structured `not_found` envelope, matching `GET /accounts/999`, unless the route/spec explicitly opts into upsert semantics. Returning `200 {}` hides a failed update from clients and makes the response body shape inconsistent with a successful update.

Client used for the campaign:
`/tmp/google_adexchangebuyer_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18085` produced 4 failures: status/body assertions for `PUT /accounts/{id}` and `PATCH /accounts/{id}` against an empty backend.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/kernel/mutation.rs:174-184` returns `MutationResult { tag: Update, rows_affected: 0, returning_rows: [] }` when no rows match the update predicate. That is reasonable for SQL `UPDATE`, but `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:112-116` encodes any update with empty `returning_rows` as HTTP `200 {}` without checking `rows_affected` or the REST route kind. The REST encoder should map `RouteKind::Update` with `rows_affected == 0` to the same structured 404 used for missing read-one resources, while preserving SQL/Postgres mutation semantics elsewhere.
close_reason: Implemented REST update-miss 404 encoding; cargo test and Google Ad Exchange repro pass. Global fmt/clippy currently blocked by active normalize.rs work outside this bead.

title: REST inline request body schemas skip required/type validation
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public OWASP crAPI OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json -o /tmp/crapi_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/crapi_openapi.json --port 18150
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18150/identity/api/auth/login' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{}'

curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18150/identity/api/auth/login' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":{"bad":["type"]},"password":"Password123!"}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{"message":null,"role":null,"token":null,"type":null}
```

Expected response/output:
The inline requestBody schema for this operation requires `email` and `password`, both strings. Missing required fields and invalid field types should produce a 400/422 validation error before any mutation succeeds. They should not return a successful response body.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /identity/api/auth/login missing required fields: status=200, body='{"message":null,"role":null,"token":null,"type":null}'
FAIL POST /identity/api/auth/login invalid body type: status=200, body='{"message":null,"role":null,"token":null,"type":null}'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:484-487` only turns request schemas into `request_resource_name` when the request body schema has a component `$ref`. Inline request body schemas set `request_body_schema_present=true` but leave `request_resource_name=None`. Then `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:366-384` falls back to the target/response resource, so required/type checks run against `JwtResponse`/`CRAPIResponse` shapes rather than the inline request schema. Because those response resources have no required input fields, `{}` and malformed values pass.
notes: Fixed by carrying inline REST request body schema metadata on RouteEntry and validating required fields plus shallow JSON types in the normalizer before target-resource mutation validation. Self-filed bug from the OWASP crAPI e2e campaign; repro now returns 422 missing_required_field for {} and 400 type_mismatch for object-valued email.

title: REST POST /Folders rejects Name and silently drops lower-case name
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Xero Files OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml -o /tmp/xero_files.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/xero_files.yaml --serve --port 18160
python3 /tmp/xero_files_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18160
```

Representative repro 1, documented example casing:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18160/Folders \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"Name\":\"My Docs\"}"
```

Actual response 1:
```http
HTTP/1.1 422 Unprocessable Entity
content-type: application/json

{"code":"missing_required_field","detail":"required field `name` is missing on resource `folders`","field":"name","resource":"folders"}
```

Representative repro 2, lower-case required field:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18160/Folders \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"name\":\"My Docs\"}"
```

Actual response 2:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{"Email":null,"FileCount":null,"Id":null,"IsInbox":null,"Name":null}
```

Expected response/output:
The Xero schema has an inconsistent required entry (`name`) and property casing (`Name`), while the operation examples use `Name`. The twin should not both reject the documented example body and then accept lower-case `name` while silently dropping the value from the persisted/returned `Name` field. It should normalize this mismatch coherently or refuse the spec/operation precisely before returning success with null data.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /Folders example body: status=422, expected=200
FAIL POST /Folders preserves Name: body={'code': 'missing_required_field', 'detail': 'required field `name` is missing on resource `folders`', 'field': 'name', 'resource': 'folders'}
PASS POST /Folders lower-case required field
FAIL POST /Folders lower-case value is not silently dropped: body={'Email': None, 'FileCount': None, 'Id': None, 'IsInbox': None, 'Name': None}
FAIL PUT /Folders/{FolderId} example body: status=422, expected=200/404
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs` preserves `required` names and property names without reconciling case mismatches, and `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs::validate_required_for()` checks for exact request JSON keys. `mutation_fields_from_object()` later materializes target columns by exact property/column name (`Name`), so a lower-case `name` key can satisfy required validation but is ignored when building the row, producing a successful response with `Name: null`.

title: REST POST /companies/{companyId}/apiCredentials/{apiCredentialId}/generateApiKey requires generated apiKey in request
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Adyen ManagementService OpenAPI 3.1.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/adyen.com/ManagementService/1/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/adyen.com/ManagementService/1/openapi.json -o /tmp/adyen_management_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/adyen_management_openapi.json --serve --port 18241
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18241/companies/company-1/apiCredentials/cred-1/generateApiKey \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 422 Unprocessable Entity
content-type: application/json

{"code":"missing_required_field","detail":"required field `apiKey` is missing on resource `generateapikeyresponses`","field":"apiKey","resource":"generateapikeyresponses"}
```

Expected response/output:
This action endpoint declares no request body and a `200` response schema `GenerateApiKeyResponse` with required server-generated field `apiKey`. The REST twin should not require the generated response field to be present in the request. In v0 it should either synthesize a schema-shaped success response or refuse the unsupported action shape explicitly at startup/request time with a 501 `unsupported_shape`. It must not produce a request validation error for a field the client cannot send.

Same root cause appears on these Adyen routes:
- `POST /companies/{companyId}/apiCredentials/{apiCredentialId}/generateClientKey` requiring `clientKey`
- `POST /companies/{companyId}/webhooks/{webhookId}/generateHmac` requiring `hmacKey`
- `POST /merchants/{merchantId}/apiCredentials/{apiCredentialId}/generateApiKey` requiring `apiKey`
- `POST /merchants/{merchantId}/apiCredentials/{apiCredentialId}/generateClientKey` requiring `clientKey`
- `POST /merchants/{merchantId}/webhooks/{webhookId}/generateHmac` requiring `hmacKey`

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /companies/{companyId}/apiCredentials/{apiCredentialId}/generateApiKey status: expected one of [200, 201, 202, 204, 404, 501] got 422 body={"code":"missing_required_field","detail":"required field `apiKey` is missing on resource `generateapikeyresponses`","field":"apiKey","resource":"generateapikeyresponses"}
FAIL POST /companies/{companyId}/apiCredentials/{apiCredentialId}/generateClientKey status: expected one of [200, 201, 202, 204, 404, 501] got 422 body={"code":"missing_required_field","detail":"required field `clientKey` is missing on resource `generateclientkeyresponses`","field":"clientKey","resource":"generateclientkeyresponses"}
FAIL POST /companies/{companyId}/webhooks/{webhookId}/generateHmac status: expected one of [200, 201, 202, 204, 404, 501] got 422 body={"code":"missing_required_field","detail":"required field `hmacKey` is missing on resource `generatehmackeyresponses`","field":"hmacKey","resource":"generatehmackeyresponses"}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/topology.rs` classifies these POST action paths as executable mutations because the response schema is a component resource with object fields. The route then reaches `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs::normalize_insert()` with `resource` equal to the response resource (`generateapikeyresponses`, `generateclientkeyresponses`, or `generatehmackeyresponses`) and no real request resource/body. `mutation_fields_from_object()` plus required-field validation requires non-null response columns like `apiKey` from the client, so a server-generated response field is misinterpreted as a required request column. A route-layer guard near `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:966`/`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1356` should identify no-body POST actions with generated scalar response resources and route them to a precise unsupported-action refusal unless/ until response synthesis exists.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Implemented in src/protocol/rest/routes.rs. No-body action-style POST routes whose response resource has required generated secret fields (apiKey/clientKey/hmacKey) now become precise route refusals instead of executable creates that ask the client for response-only values. Added regression test no_body_action_post_with_generated_response_fields_gets_precise_refusal. Repro now returns HTTP 501 unsupported_shape for POST /companies/{companyId}/apiCredentials/{apiCredentialId}/generateApiKey. Final Adyen Management e2e: operations=119 assertions=270 failures=0. Gate passed: cargo fmt --check && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo test.

title: GitHub REST campaign fails allOf merge on webhook-fork.created_at oneOf vs string
description:
Discovered during bd-1bsj GitHub REST API campaign after minifying the GitHub OpenAPI spec to get below the raw 10MB loader limit.

Spec URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.json
Local minimized spec: /tmp/github_openapi.min.json

Repro:
1. curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.json -o /tmp/github_openapi.json
2. jq -c . /tmp/github_openapi.json > /tmp/github_openapi.min.json
3. cargo build
4. target/debug/twinning rest --spec /tmp/github_openapi.min.json --serve --port 18400 --json

Actual:
Process exits 2 with refusal code E_OPENAPI_SCHEMA_CONFLICT. Message: OpenAPI allOf merge for schema `webhook-fork` has conflicting types for field `created_at`. Detail: left_type=oneOf, right_type=string, field=created_at.

Expected:
The REST catalog should tolerate GitHub-style polymorphic allOf fields enough to classify the large spec, ideally preserving a deterministic broad type/refusal rather than rejecting the entire spec before startup.

Root cause hypothesis:
src/protocol/rest/spec.rs merge_all_of treats differing normalized property types as a hard schema conflict. GitHub combines repository-like schemas where `created_at` appears as oneOf(integer|string date-time) in one branch and string/date-time in another. The v0 REST adapter already treats oneOf as text elsewhere, so this conflict should likely converge to the existing broad scalar handling instead of blocking catalog construction.
close_reason: Completed: relaxed allOf scalar conflict merging for polymorphic, untyped scalar, and integer/number GitHub schema variants while preserving concrete conflict refusals.

title: REST GET /api/v1/Activities/{id} returns array for singleton object route
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public FakeRESTApi OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://fakerestapi.azurewebsites.net/swagger/v1/swagger.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://fakerestapi.azurewebsites.net/swagger/v1/swagger.json -o /tmp/fakerestapi_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/fakerestapi_openapi.json --serve --port 18168
python3 /tmp/fakerestapi_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18168
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18168/api/v1/Activities/1
curl -i -X DELETE http://127.0.0.1:18168/api/v1/Activities/1
```

Actual responses:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /api/v1/Activities/{id}"}
```

Expected response/output:
`GET /api/v1/Activities/{id}` declares a singleton object response, `#/components/schemas/Activity`. With an empty twin it should return a singleton not-found envelope (`404 {"code":"not_found",...}`) or a JSON object if a row exists; it should never encode an item route as a collection array. The matching `DELETE /api/v1/Activities/{id}` route is a CRUD item route and should execute (FakeRESTApi declares 200), not receive a generic outside-CRUD refusal. The same pattern reproduces for `/api/v1/Authors/{id}`, `/api/v1/Books/{id}`, and `/api/v1/CoverPhotos/{id}`. `/api/v1/Users/{id}` is the control case and correctly returns singleton 404/200 behavior.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /api/v1/Activities/1 empty state is singleton not_found: status=200, body='[]'
FAIL DELETE /api/v1/Activities/1 is classifiable CRUD: status=501, body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /api/v1/Activities/{id}"}'
FAIL GET /api/v1/Authors/1 empty state is singleton not_found: status=200, body='[]'
FAIL GET /api/v1/Books/1 empty state is singleton not_found: status=200, body='[]'
SUMMARY assertions=37 failures=23
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/topology.rs:338-344` defaults schema-first candidates to `ResourceRelationship::RootCollection` when neither `path_shape_binding` nor prefix-scoped relationship inference produces a relationship. For prefixed paths like `/api/v1/Activities/{id}`, the unprefixed item suffix is not available to `path_shape_binding`, so the schema-first `Activity` response resource is bound as a collection. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:683-711` only corrects object responses to singleton for child collections and one-literal root paths; it does not handle prefixed item paths. The result is `GET` routed as `ReadMany` and `DELETE` falling through to the generic route refusal.

This is related to the closed `bd-elyn` Twilio regression but not a duplicate: that closure covered `/v1/Faxes/{Sid}` after prefix stripping; FakeRESTApi shows the same class still leaks through for two-literal prefixes and component object responses after the current tree's fixes.

title: REST inline object response schemas are not classified
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public APIs.guru OpenAPI Directory spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apis.guru/2.2.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apis.guru/2.2.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/apis_guru_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apis_guru_openapi.json --port 18134
python3 /tmp/apis_guru_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18134
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18134/providers.json'
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18134/github.com/services.json'
```

Actual responses:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /providers.json"}
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /{provider}/services.json"}
```

Expected response/output:
Both operations declare inline JSON object response schemas with a `data` array property. The twin should either classify those inline response resources and preserve the object shape, or return a precise unsupported-schema refusal that explains inline response object schemas are outside REST twin v0. The generic outside-CRUD route refusal is misleading because the route is declared and has a concrete response schema.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /providers.json status: status=501, expected 200
FAIL GET /providers.json data array: body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', ...}
FAIL GET /{provider}/services.json status: status=501, expected 200
FAIL GET /{provider}/services.json data array: body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', ...}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:286-299` builds catalog resources only from `components.schemas`. The `providers.json` and `services.json` successful response schemas are inline object schemas under the operation response, so they never become `RestCatalog.resources`. Then `src/protocol/rest/topology.rs:555-571` calls `schema_resource_name()`, and `src/protocol/rest/topology.rs:599-604` only derives a resource from `$ref` or array `items`, not inline object `properties`. The classifier therefore has no schema-first candidate and falls back to the generic route refusal.
close_reason: Completed: inline response object schemas now get precise REST refusals instead of generic outside-CRUD

comments.author: Zac

Actual: 501 unsupported_shape with generic outside-CRUD detail for path /. Expected: 200 object preserving the inline response schema fields _embedded, _links, page_number, page_size. Same root cause class as bd-1fiv: inline object response schemas are not classified/cataloged, so declared routes fall through to generic route refusal.

Actual: generic outside-CRUD 501 for both routes. Expected: preserve inline list-wrapper object responses with faxes/media arrays and meta objects, or give a precise unsupported inline response schema refusal. Same root cause class as bd-1fiv: inline object response schemas are not cataloged/classified.

title: Provable tenant isolation — cryptographic proof that clients never confuse tenant contexts across 10000 request scenarios
description: Spin up one twin instance per tenant, each with a unique keypair. Every twin response is signed with the tenant-specific private key. Test harness verifies every response the client receives is signed by the correct key for the tenant context it is operating in. A response signed by the wrong key is a cryptographically proven tenant isolation violation — not a heuristic assertion. Run across 10000 request scenarios spanning all valid operations across all tenants. Output is an isolation proof report: test_run_id, scenarios count, tenant_contexts, violations count, signed_by, attestation hash. This report is a compliance artifact suitable for SOC 2: We ran 10000 tenant-boundary scenarios and found zero isolation violations — produced automatically, not by manual penetration testing. Every release generates a new proof; regressions show up as violations in the isolation proof before they reach production. Tenant isolation testing moves from we think it works to we have a continuous attestation chain proving it works. CMD+RVL angle: metadata API enforces tenant isolation across salt, crediq, wilcox; cmdrvl-cli supports all three tenants; tenant credential confusion is a real bug class currently tested only manually. This pattern automates it and produces a compliance artifact. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md section 8.

title: REST inline scalar request bodies accept empty object but reject valid scalar JSON
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Keycloak Admin REST API OpenAPI 3.0.3 spec.

Spec URL:
https://www.keycloak.org/docs-api/latest/rest-api/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://www.keycloak.org/docs-api/latest/rest-api/openapi.json -o /tmp/keycloak_admin_openapi.json
target/debug/twinning rest --spec /tmp/keycloak_admin_openapi.json --serve --port 18310
```

Invalid/missing required scalar body repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18310/admin/realms/master/organizations/org-1/identity-providers \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
```

Expected response:
This operation declares `requestBody.required: true` with `application/json` schema `{ "type": "string" }`. An object body, or an empty body, should produce a 400/422 type/missing-body refusal before the operation succeeds.

Valid scalar body repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18310/admin/realms/master/organizations/org-1/identity-providers \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '"google"'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
content-type: application/json

{"code":"type_mismatch","detail":"field `<body>` expected object but received string","expected":"object","field":"<body>","received":"string"}
```

Expected response:
The valid JSON string body should be accepted by the request-shape validator, then the route should proceed or return a precise unsupported-shape refusal if scalar request bodies are outside the v0 executable subset. It should not reject the declared scalar body while accepting `{}`.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /admin/realms/{realm}/organizations/{org-id}/identity-providers missing_body_validation: status=204, body=b''
FAIL POST /admin/realms/{realm}/organizations/{org-id}/identity-providers invalid_body_validation: status=204, body=b''
SUMMARY operations=401 assertions=1019 failures=2
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:613` (`parse_json_body`) always parses JSON request bodies as objects and returns an empty object for empty bodies. `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:1049` (`validate_inline_request_body_schema`) validates inline object properties but never validates the top-level inline schema type or `requestBody.required`. For an inline scalar request schema, the valid scalar body is rejected as `expected object`, while `{}`/empty body flows through as an empty mutation and `encode.rs` emits the declared `204` success.
close_reason: Completed: scalar top-level JSON request bodies now classify as explicit REST unsupported-shape refusals; added route regression and verified Keycloak repro

title: REST Stripe spec startup refuses recursive file_link/file schema refs
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official public Stripe OpenAPI 3.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json -o /tmp/stripe_spec3.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/stripe_spec3.json --serve --port 18174
```

Actual response/output:
```text
REFUSAL [E_OPENAPI_REF_CIRCULAR]
Circular OpenAPI $ref chain detected while resolving `file_link`.
detail: {"ref":"#/components/schemas/file","schema":"file_link"}
```

Expected response/output:
The REST twin should not reject the whole Stripe spec because two response schemas are recursively expandable. It should catalog the non-recursive fields/routes and degrade the recursive edge to a bounded JSON/text field or a warning/refusal for the affected field/route. Startup should reach route reporting so classifiable Stripe routes can be exercised.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:875-899` treats any repeated `$ref` in `resolve_schema_reference()` as a fatal `E_OPENAPI_REF_CIRCULAR` while fully resolving component properties. Stripe has a common recursive response model: `#/components/schemas/file.properties.links.properties.data.items.$ref` points to `#/components/schemas/file_link`, and `#/components/schemas/file_link.properties.file.anyOf[1].$ref` points back to `#/components/schemas/file`. That recursive expansion should be bounded during schema normalization instead of refusing the entire OpenAPI document.

Priority: P2 because this is a startup refusal/missing feature for a large real-world OpenAPI 3.0 spec, not wrong data after startup.

title: REST path-item request schema candidates leak into GET routes
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public OWASP crAPI OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json -o /tmp/crapi_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/crapi_openapi.json --port 18150
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18150/workshop/api/shop/orders/1'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
`GET /workshop/api/shop/orders/{order_id}` declares a 200 object response with an `orders` field containing an `Order`. With empty state, the route should return a singleton object when present or a structured 404. It should not read from the `ProductQuantity` request-body resource and return an array.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /workshop/api/shop/orders/{order_id} response shape: expected object, got list, body='[{"product_id":1,"quantity":1}]'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/topology.rs:614-619` computes one schema-first candidate for the whole path item and collects request-body candidates from every operation on that path. On `/workshop/api/shop/orders/{order_id}`, the GET response is an inline wrapper object that does not yield a component resource, while the sibling PUT request body is `ProductQuantity`. The path-level schema candidate therefore becomes `productquantitys`, and the GET route inherits that mutation input resource as its read target.

title: bd-v1r.4: flat-crud policy with singular/plural normalization
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Depends on
bd-3jp2 (bd-v1r.3) — RouteEntry must have matched_policy + effective_resource_name fields

## Can run in parallel with
bd-v1r.5 (schema-first), bd-v1r.6 (prefix-scoped) — all three Wave 3 beads are independent

## Goal
Implement the flat-crud policy as a proper named policy function, adding singular/plural normalization so `/pets` → `Pet` and `/users` → `User`.

## Context

Current classify_route() does flat-crud implicitly: resource_name = first path segment, gate on catalog.resources.contains_key(resource_name). This breaks on plural path names because spec schemas are singular.

## Deliverables

### 1. Normalization helper — `src/protocol/rest/policy.rs`

```rust
/// Normalize a plural or mixed-case path segment to a schema resource name.
/// Examples: "pets" → "Pet", "users" → "User", "Pets" → "Pet", "pet" → "Pet"
pub fn normalize_resource_name(segment: &str) -> String
```

Rules (in order):
1. Strip trailing 's' if result len ≥ 2 (naive depluralization)
2. Strip trailing 'ies' → 'y' (e.g. entities → entity)
3. Title-case the result

Edge cases to handle: already singular ("Pet" → "Pet"), empty string → empty string.

### 2. flat_crud_classify function

```rust
pub fn flat_crud_classify(
    path: &str,
    catalog: &RestCatalog,
    base_prefix: Option<&str>,
) -> Option<String>  // returns effective_resource_name if matched
```

Logic:
1. Strip base_prefix from path if Some
2. Split remaining path, take first non-empty segment
3. Try exact catalog lookup → return as-is if hit
4. Try normalize_resource_name → return normalized if hit
5. Return None (no match)

### 3. Wire into RouteEntry classification site

In classify_route(), when policy is FlatCrud OR Auto: call flat_crud_classify(), set matched_policy = Some(FlatCrud) and effective_resource_name if matched.

## Tests

- `flat_crud_pets_plural`: /pets → Pet (petstore catalog)
- `flat_crud_users_plural`: /users → User
- `flat_crud_already_singular`: /pet → Pet
- `flat_crud_with_prefix`: /api/pets with base_prefix=/api → Pet
- `flat_crud_no_match`: /v1/estimates/price with no prefix → None (not a catalog hit)
- `normalize_entities`: "entities" → "Entity"

## Acceptance

Petstore e2e: all 3 routes classified, 0 refusals.
`cargo test -p twinning rest::policy::flat_crud` green.
`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

title: REST GET /crx/packmgr/installstatus.jsp returns [] for object response
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Adobe Experience Manager OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/adobe.com/aem/3.7.1-pre.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/adobe.com/aem/3.7.1-pre.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/adobe_aem_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/adobe_aem_openapi.json --serve --port 18231
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18231/crx/packmgr/installstatus.jsp'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response:
`GET /crx/packmgr/installstatus.jsp` declares a `200` JSON response of `#/components/schemas/InstallStatus`, an object:

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "status": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "finished": { "type": "boolean" },
        "itemCount": { "type": "integer" }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

On an empty twin, returning a structured 404 is acceptable for a singleton object route. Returning `200 []` is wrong because it silently presents an object response schema as an empty collection.

Second route with the same root cause:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18231/libs/granite/security/truststore.json'
```

Actual:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected:
`GET /libs/granite/security/truststore.json` declares `#/components/schemas/TruststoreInfo`, an object with `exists` and `aliases` properties, so the twin should not return a collection array.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /crx/packmgr/installstatus.jsp response shape: status=200; expected object; body='[]'
FAIL GET /libs/granite/security/truststore.json response shape: status=200; expected object; body='[]'
SUMMARY operations=48 assertions=127 failures=2
```

Root cause hypothesis:
This is a residual version of the object-response singleton bug, but for multi-literal schema-first topology paths. In `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:720-746`, topology entries are classified before the waterfall. `route_relationship_for_response_shape()` at `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:749-776` only converts `RootCollection` + object response into `RootSingleton` if `root_object_response_should_be_singleton()` returns true. That helper at `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:779-786` returns false for multi-literal paths without path params because it only recognizes terminal param routes or a single literal segment. The later schema-first fallback in `route_kind_from_schema_first_response()` at `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:962-990` would classify a no-param object response as `ReadOne`, but it never runs because high-confidence topology already won.

Priority: P1 because the route returns a successful `200 []` with the wrong response shape instead of an explicit refusal or singleton 404.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Fixed in src/protocol/rest/routes.rs by treating no-param multi-literal GET routes with declared object success responses as root singletons when topology classification wins before the schema-first fallback. Repro curls for /crx/packmgr/installstatus.jsp and /libs/granite/security/truststore.json now return HTTP 404 not_found instead of 200 []. Adobe AEM client now reports operations=48 assertions=127 failures=0. Full gate passed: cargo fmt --check && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo test.

title: Vendor contract attestation — signed daily proof that external APIs conform to their published OpenAPI specs
description: Run twinning rest against a pinned spec version daily in CI. Download the current published spec, diff the hash, replay the request corpus against both the twin (contract) and the real API (ground truth). Emit a signed attestation on every run: timestamp, api, spec_version, spec_hash, corpus_hash, verdict, divergences. Two failure modes: spec changed AND suite fails = vendor broke their published contract; spec changed AND suite passes = vendor silently extended it. Publish attestations to a queryable endpoint over time to build a spec fidelity reputation layer for the API ecosystem — vendors whose specs consistently match their behavior get high fidelity scores; vendors who routinely diverge get flagged. The twin is the instrument; the attestation corpus is the product. CMD+RVL angle: aibuildout depends on 4+ external APIs (Stripe, OpenAI, Perplexity, metadata API); each gets a daily canary with a stored attestation chain. When a vendor breaks their contract, you have a timestamped corpus-backed proof useful for billing disputes, support escalations, and API version pinning decisions. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md section 1.
