title: REST GET /terminals returns bare [] instead of ListTerminalsResponse wrapper object
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Adyen ManagementService OpenAPI 3.1.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/adyen.com/ManagementService/1/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/adyen.com/ManagementService/1/openapi.json -o /tmp/adyen_management_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/adyen_management_openapi.json --serve --port 18241
```

Repro:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18241/terminals
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
The spec declares `GET /terminals` response `#/components/schemas/ListTerminalsResponse`, an object wrapper with a single array field:
```json
{"data":[{"id":"S1F2-000150183300032", "status":"OnlineToday"}]}
```
The twin should preserve the declared object envelope, for an empty twin returning at least `200 {"data": []}`. Returning a bare array silently violates the OpenAPI response shape.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /terminals body shape: expected object for declared response, got []
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs::component_response_wrapper()` only attaches `ResponseWrapper` metadata when `resource_is_array_wrapper_for_hints()` returns true. That helper currently requires a metadata field such as `count`, `total`, `status`, or `message` (`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:2222`). `ListTerminalsResponse` has exactly one standard payload array field named `data` and no metadata fields, so the route is classified as `ReadMany` but `response_wrapper` remains `None`. `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs::encode_read_many_payload()` then serializes the result as a bare array instead of wrapping it under `data`.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Implemented in src/protocol/rest/routes.rs. Component response wrappers now accept a single standard payload array field with no metadata fields, so schemas like Adyen ListTerminalsResponse attach ResponseWrapper { array_field: data }. Added regression test component_data_only_wrapper_response_records_wrapper. Repro now returns HTTP 200 with {"data":[]} for GET /terminals. Adyen e2e rerun: operations=119 assertions=270 failures=6, with the remaining failures all covered by bd-1e8h. Gate passed: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test.

title: REST GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps/{name} fabricates empty ConfigMap
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Kubernetes core v1 OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/master/api/openapi-spec/v3/api__v1_openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/master/api/openapi-spec/v3/api__v1_openapi.json -o /tmp/kubernetes_core_v1_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/kubernetes_core_v1_openapi.json --serve --port 18250
```

Exact curl repro against a freshly started empty twin:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18250/api/v1/namespaces/default/configmaps/sample \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer e2e'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{"apiVersion":"","binaryData":null,"immutable":false,"kind":"","metadata":null,"data":[]}
```

Expected response/output:
The backend starts empty (`No spec examples found - backend starts empty`). `GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps/{name}` is a singleton item route. With no stored ConfigMap named `sample`, it should return a `404 {"code":"not_found", ...}` or be explicitly refused if the path/resource shape is unsupported. It must not fabricate a successful ConfigMap object filled with defaults and `data: []`.

A second representative route with the same behavior is:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18250/api/v1/namespaces/default/secrets/sample \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer e2e'
```
which returns `200 {"apiVersion":"","immutable":false,"kind":"","metadata":null,"stringData":null,"type":"","data":[]}` from empty storage.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
singleton_empty_200: 2
  - GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps/{name}: empty singleton returned 200 body=b'{"apiVersion":"","binaryData":null,"immutable":false,"kind":"","metadata":null,"data":[]}'
  - GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets/{name}: empty singleton returned 200 body=b'{"apiVersion":"","immutable":false,"kind":"","metadata":null,"stringData":null,"type":"","data":[]}'
SUMMARY operations=242 assertions=300 failures=52
```

Root cause hypothesis:
This is a different manifestation from the earlier Stripe incidental-array fix. Kubernetes `ConfigMap` and `Secret` are normal singleton resources, but their standard `data` fields plus `metadata` fields match the response-wrapper heuristics. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:2490-2516` treats standard `data`/`items`/`results` fields as wrapper payload candidates even on item schemas, while `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:2536-2548` treats `metadata` as wrapper metadata. The wrapper attached through `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:655-686` lets an empty singleton item route encode as a successful wrapper/read-many response instead of using the normal `not_found` path in `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:215-217`.

Priority: P1 because this is silent wrong data for an empty singleton resource.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Fix note: tightened component response-wrapper detection to require array payloads except nested results object envelopes; Kubernetes ConfigMap/Secret singleton routes now return an explicit 501 unsupported_shape instead of fabricated empty records from empty storage; focused tests and full gate passed.

Update all import paths. cargo build produces identical binary. Acceptance: cargo test green; crates/twinning-kernel standalone build succeeds.

title: REST POST /v1/quote/contractCall returns 500 for response/returning mismatch
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public LI.FI OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://docs.li.fi/openapi.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://docs.li.fi/openapi.yaml -o /tmp/lifi_openapi.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/lifi_openapi.yaml --serve --port 18189
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18189/v1/quote/contractCall' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"fromChain":1.25,"fromToken":"tw-test","fromAddress":"tw-test","toChain":1.25,"toToken":"tw-test","toAmount":"tw-test","toContractAddress":"tw-test","toContractCallData":"tw-test","toContractGasLimit":"tw-test"}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
content-type: application/json

{"code":"kernel_error","detail":"mutation result contains RETURNING rows without REST response column metadata"}
```

Related exact repro showing the same class with mismatched arity:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18189/v1/relayer/relay' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"tokenOwner":"tw-test","chainId":1.25,"callData":"tw-test"}'
```

Actual related response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
content-type: application/json

{"code":"kernel_error","detail":"result row arity 4 does not match response column count 1"}
```

Expected response/output:
Both routes are advertised as executable in the startup route table. A valid-looking request body must not produce a 500. If REST v0 cannot synthesize the declared response resource from the request/target resource, the route should be refused precisely before execution or encoded with a consistent response metadata/returning shape.

Root cause hypothesis:
For mutation routes whose request/target resource differs from the declared response resource, `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:1484-1490` asks the kernel to return safe columns from the mutation target resource, while `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:162-180` encodes those returned rows using `route.response_fields` computed from the response schema/resource in `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1384-1407`. When the response fields are empty (`ToolsResponse`) the encoder emits "RETURNING rows without REST response column metadata"; when the response fields count differs (`RelayResponse.status` vs request fields) it emits a row arity 500. The classifier should either align returning columns with response fields or refuse unsupported response synthesis.

Priority: P1 because advertised executable POST routes return internal server errors.

title: Mainframe regulatory sandbox — twin an entire core banking system (VSAM + IMS + CICS + flat files) for stress testing on commodity hardware
description: Banks run core banking on IBM mainframes: data in VSAM datasets and IMS hierarchical databases, business logic in COBOL batch jobs, transactions via CICS. Regulatory stress tests (300bp rate shock, liquidity scenarios) require months of setup and mainframe compute time. With twinning, reconstruct the full interface surface from schema artifacts: VSAM datasets from COBOL copybooks, IMS databases from DBDs, CICS transactions from top-50 EXEC CICS commands, flat file outputs from output copybooks. Load stress test scenario data into twin fleet. Compile COBOL batch jobs with GnuCOBOL, point file I/O at VSAM twins. Execute COBOL programs. Collect output from flat file twins. Stress test produced in hours on commodity hardware in a completely isolated environment. Attestation chain: every twin produces signed reports and content-addressed snapshots; every batch job's input and output state is captured; entire execution is reproducible from attestation chain alone — regulator can verify independently. Dual-run validator: run batch jobs against twin fleet AND against real mainframe (non-production copy); compare output flat files byte by byte; divergences are twin fidelity gaps. Commercial impact: banks pay tens of millions annually for mainframe stress test infrastructure; this collapses to commodity hardware costs. Prerequisites: VSAM twin, IMS twin, CICS twin (top-50 surface), flat file twin all stable; GnuCOBOL integration layer. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 4.

title: Cargo workspace split — protocol-per-crate modularity (postgres, rest, future: arrow/snowflake)
description:
## Goal

Split twinning from a single monolithic binary into a Cargo workspace where each wire protocol is a separate crate. A deployment that only needs the REST twin does not compile or link any Postgres, Arrow, Snowflake, or other protocol code. Each new protocol added to twinning costs zero binary size to users who don't use it.

## Why now (context)

At 2 protocols (postgres + rest) this is not urgent. File now to:
1. Document the invariant that must be held while protocols are built (no cross-protocol imports)
2. Establish the target crate layout so new protocols land in the right place
3. Prevent structural debt that would make the split painful later

**This epic is blocked until the current REST wire protocol work and routing engine are complete.**

## Design

### Target workspace layout

```
twinning/
├── Cargo.toml                   (workspace root, no code)
├── crates/
│   ├── twinning-kernel/         # shared: storage, kernel, backend, IR, catalog,
│   │                            #   refusal, report, snapshot, declaration,
│   │                            #   materialize, query_trace, verify_bridge,
│   │                            #   result, runtime
│   ├── twinning-postgres/       # pgwire protocol — depends on twinning-kernel only
│   ├── twinning-rest/           # HTTP/OpenAPI protocol — depends on twinning-kernel only
│   └── twinning/                # top-level binary — depends on selected protocol crates
```

Future protocols slot in as new crates (`twinning-arrow`, `twinning-snowflake`, etc.) with zero changes to existing crates.

### Feature flags on the binary crate

```toml
[features]
default = ["rest", "postgres"]
rest    = ["dep:twinning-rest"]
postgres = ["dep:twinning-postgres"]
all     = ["rest", "postgres"]
```

Released binary: `--features all`. REST-only test container: `--no-default-features --features rest`. Postgres-only: `--no-default-features --features postgres`.

### The invariant to hold now (before the split)

**No protocol module may import from another protocol module.**

`src/protocol/postgres/*` may only import from `src/kernel`, `src/backend`, `src/catalog`, `src/refusal`, `src/report`, `src/snapshot`, `src/ir`, `src/result`, `src/runtime`.

`src/protocol/rest/*` may only import from the same shared layer.

Neither protocol may import from the other. Violations today make the workspace split require untangling rather than just moving directories. A lint or CI check should enforce this invariant continuously.

### What moves where

**twinning-kernel** (renamed from the current implicit shared layer):
- `src/kernel/` → `twinning-kernel/src/kernel/`
- `src/backend/` → `twinning-kernel/src/backend/`
- `src/catalog.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/catalog.rs`
- `src/ir.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/ir.rs`
- `src/refusal.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/refusal.rs`
- `src/report.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/report.rs`
- `src/snapshot.rs` + `src/snapshot/` → `twinning-kernel/src/snapshot/`
- `src/declaration.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/declaration.rs`
- `src/materialize.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/materialize.rs`
- `src/query_trace.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/query_trace.rs`
- `src/verify_bridge.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/verify_bridge.rs`
- `src/result.rs` → `twinning-kernel/src/result.rs`
- `src/runtime.rs` + `src/runtime/` → `twinning-kernel/src/runtime/`

**twinning-postgres**:
- `src/protocol/postgres/` → `twinning-postgres/src/`
- Dependencies: twinning-kernel, tokio, serde, serde_json, signal-hook, thiserror, sqlparser

**twinning-rest**:
- `src/protocol/rest/` → `twinning-rest/src/`
- Dependencies: twinning-kernel, axum, tokio, serde, serde_json, serde_yaml, ureq, thiserror

**twinning** (binary crate):
- `src/cli.rs`, `src/config.rs`, `src/lib.rs`, `src/main.rs`, `src/doctor.rs`, `src/migration_proof.rs`, `src/orchestration_manifest.rs`, `src/port.rs`
- operator.json stays here
- Dependencies: twinning-kernel, twinning-postgres (feature-gated), twinning-rest (feature-gated), clap, serde_json, thiserror

## Beads

### bd-ws.1 — Cross-protocol import audit and CI lint

Audit current source for any cross-protocol imports (postgres importing rest or vice versa). Add a clippy/rustc deny or a simple `grep` CI check that asserts `src/protocol/postgres` contains no references to `protocol::rest` and vice versa. This is the only bead that can be done before the blocking epic closes — it establishes the invariant without restructuring anything.

Acceptance: CI check added; current codebase passes it cleanly; documented in AGENTS.md.

### bd-ws.2 — Workspace Cargo.toml and twinning-kernel extraction

Create `Cargo.toml` at workspace root. Move shared modules (kernel, backend, catalog, ir, refusal, report, snapshot, declaration, materialize, query_trace, verify_bridge, result, runtime) into `crates/twinning-kernel/`. Update all imports. `cargo build` produces identical binary.

Acceptance: `cargo test` green; `cargo build --manifest-path crates/twinning-kernel/Cargo.toml` succeeds standalone.

### bd-ws.3 — Extract twinning-postgres crate

Move `src/protocol/postgres/` into `crates/twinning-postgres/`. Update binary crate to depend on it. Wire postgres feature flag.

Acceptance: `cargo build --no-default-features --features postgres` succeeds and produces a working pgwire binary. `cargo build --no-default-features --features rest` produces a binary with zero postgres code (verified by `nm` or `strings`).

### bd-ws.4 — Extract twinning-rest crate

Move `src/protocol/rest/` into `crates/twinning-rest/`. Update binary crate to depend on it. Wire rest feature flag.

Acceptance: `cargo build --no-default-features --features rest` succeeds and produces a working REST twin. `cargo build --no-default-features --features postgres` produces a binary with zero axum/ureq/serde_yaml (verified by `nm` or binary size delta).

### bd-ws.5 — Feature matrix CI validation

Add CI matrix: `[rest, postgres, rest+postgres, all]`. Each build must succeed and pass `cargo test` in its feature configuration. Binary size reported per build as a regression guardrail.

Acceptance: CI matrix green; binary sizes documented in AGENTS.md or a `BUILDS.md` artifact.

## Ordering

bd-ws.1 is the only bead that can open before the blocking epic closes — it's a pure audit/lint with no restructuring. All others wait for the REST wire protocol to be complete.

Wave order after unblock:
bd-ws.2 → bd-ws.3 (parallel with bd-ws.4) → bd-ws.5
close_reason: Completed workspace split: all protocol-per-crate child beads are closed and verified.

title: REST inline wrapper response schemas collapse to bare arrays
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public OWASP crAPI OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json -o /tmp/crapi_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/crapi_openapi.json --port 18150
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18150/workshop/api/shop/products'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[{"id":1,"image_url":"images/seat.svg","name":"Seat","price":"10.00"}]
```

Expected response/output:
The declared 200 response is an inline object wrapper:
```json
{"credit": 0, "products": [ ... ]}
```
The twin should preserve that wrapper shape, or return a precise unsupported inline-wrapper refusal if v0 cannot materialize it. It should not collapse the response to a bare array.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /workshop/api/shop/products response shape: expected object, got list, body='[{"id":1,"image_url":"images/seat.svg","name":"Seat","price":"10.00"}]'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:552-558` only attaches `RouteEntry.response_wrapper` through `response_wrapper()`, which requires a component `response_schema_ref`. Inline response wrappers are detected as `ResponseBodyShape::ArrayWrapper`, but no `ResponseWrapper` metadata is retained. `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:223-230` therefore encodes `ReadMany` as a bare row array instead of the declared inline wrapper object.
notes: Fixed by deriving ResponseWrapper metadata from inline object response schemas that contain an array property, carrying static scalar wrapper fields such as credit, and emitting those fields in the REST encoder. Self-filed crAPI repro now returns {"credit":0,"products":[...]} for GET /workshop/api/shop/products instead of a bare array.

title: REST read-many rejects declared non-column query params as unknown fields
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public openapi3_parser Uber example.

Spec source page:
https://www.ruby-toolbox.com/projects/openapi3_parser

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kevindew/openapi3_parser/main/spec/support/examples/uber.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kevindew/openapi3_parser/main/spec/support/examples/uber.yaml -o /tmp/openapi3_parser_uber.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/openapi3_parser_uber.yaml
```

Repro request:
```bash
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:8080/products?latitude=37.775&longitude=-122.418"
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 422 Unprocessable Entity
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unknown_field","detail":"field `latitude` is not declared on resource `products`","field":"latitude","resource":"products"}
```

Expected response:
`latitude` and `longitude` are declared query parameters on `GET /products` in the OpenAPI spec, so they should not be treated as unknown resource fields. With an empty backend, the conservative expected v0 response is `200 []` after accepting/ignoring non-filter query params, or at minimum a precise unsupported-query-shape refusal that explains declared non-column filters are unsupported. Returning `unknown_field` is misleading and makes a valid spec-declared request look like client supplied an undeclared field.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs` `query_predicate()` first checks `route.declared_query_params`, then assumes every non-pagination declared query parameter maps to a `ResourceSchema` column via `resource_column(resource, &param)` around lines 420-430. Real APIs often declare operational query parameters such as location filters that are not response resource fields. The normalizer needs to distinguish declared query params that are filterable resource columns from declared non-column query params, probably accepting/ignoring the latter in v0 with a warning or returning a more accurate `UnsupportedShape`.

title: Incremental spec synthesis — AI research loop proposes each spec addition, twin validates each increment before the next is proposed
description: Inverts the premise of every other idea in this corpus. Previous ideas use the twin to validate something that already exists. This uses the twin as the design oracle while the API is being created. The loop: an AI agent does domain research (reads documentation, studies similar APIs, understands the problem space) and proposes the next increment to the OpenAPI spec — a new endpoint, a new field on a response schema, a new error code. Before proposing the next increment, it twins the current spec and validates that the addition is coherent and usable. The twin is the grounding mechanism at each step. You cannot propose a spec addition that produces an incoherent API surface because the twin tells you immediately. What the twin reveals at each increment that reading YAML cannot: (a) Structural coherence — does the new schema reference types that are already declared, or did this increment introduce an orphan type? Does the authentication on this endpoint match the scheme on adjacent endpoints? (b) The stranger test — after each addition, spawn a fresh agent that has seen only the current spec and ask it to exercise the new endpoint; if the agent cannot construct a valid request from the spec alone, the endpoint is under-determined; the twin is the execution oracle, the naive agent is the usability oracle; both run at every increment; (c) Backward compatibility gate — every proposed addition is replayed against simulated clients written against earlier spec versions; if adding a required response field breaks a v1 client, the gate rejects the addition before it enters the spec; the spec can only grow in backward-compatible directions; (d) Multi-stakeholder negotiation — different agents represent different consumer perspectives (mobile client, server-to-server integration, analytics consumer); each proposes additions and objections to every increment; every proposal is twinned immediately; the spec that emerges has been validated from all declared consumer angles not just the producer's perspective. The key distinction from spec-first development (bd-32e5): spec-first writes the full spec then both sides develop against it; this generates the spec one verified increment at a time with a research loop proposing each increment and the twin grounding each proposal in executable reality. CMD+RVL angle: designing the metadata v2 API, the Cairn data agent API surface, any new internal service. Instead of writing a spec and discovering incoherence during implementation, each design decision is verified before the next decision is made. Prerequisite: REST twin stable. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 13.

title: Living digital twin — WAL-driven in-memory Postgres replica serving queries at RAM bandwidth with tournament-mode overlay economics
description: Production Postgres streams its write-ahead log to the twin via logical replication. Twin maintains a complete in-memory replica of the declared subset, updated continuously. Queries that would be expensive on production (full table scans, large aggregations, expensive joins on a 500GB table) served from twin at memory bandwidth — not disk I/O, not buffer pool contention, not lock waits. This is not a read replica: a read replica has the same disk-based performance characteristics as the primary; the twin stores everything in memory with its own in-memory operation model and serves queries at RAM speed against a hot working set that fits entirely in L3 cache for the declared subset. Performance tier: for hot datasets that fit in available RAM with read-heavy access patterns, the living digital twin is a fundamentally different performance tier than any disk-based replica — analytical queries taking 800ms on production take 2ms on the twin. Tournament mode connection: the twin already supports tournament-mode overlay semantics (shared base snapshot plus lightweight per-session overlays); WAL stream applies to the shared base; session overlays handle read-your-writes semantics for sessions that have written but whose writes have not yet replicated. Staleness bound: lags production by at most replication latency (typically <100ms same-datacenter); SET twinning.staleness_ok = true routes to twin for analytics; transactions requiring read-after-write route to production. CMD+RVL angle: aibuildout enrichment pipeline runs expensive analytical queries against metadata database; route to living digital twin instead of production — same data within replication lag, RAM-speed execution, zero production impact. Prerequisite: Postgres tournament twin v0 complete, logical replication consumer. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 8.

title: MCP wire protocol twin — JSON-RPC 2.0 server twin from initialize+tools/list catalog
description: Add twinning mcp subcommand that twins a Model Context Protocol server. Input: either a live MCP server invocation (twinning mcp --server 'npx @scope/server args') from which twinning runs initialize + tools/list at startup to build the catalog, or a static manifest file. Transport: JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio and HTTP/SSE (matching MCP transports). Catalog built from: serverInfo, declared capabilities, tools (name + input_schema), resources (URI templates + schema), prompts (name + arguments). Protocol handlers: initialize (capability negotiation response), tools/list (emit catalog), tools/call (return typed stub from output schema if defined, unsupported_shape 501 if stateful/destructive or schema-less), resources/list, resources/read (empty stub), prompts/list, prompts/get. Auth: same bearer/apiKey shape compliance as REST twin -- check Authorization header per declared auth scheme before dispatching. Startup report: tool catalog table with name, input_schema summary, estimated executability. Refusal taxonomy: same envelope as REST (unsupported_shape, missing_auth, etc). Implementation: new src/protocol/mcp/ module and twinning-mcp crate (after workspace split lands). Blocked on bd-1o98 (workspace split) so mcp lands cleanly as a feature-gated crate. Key difference from REST: tool call outputs are schema-less -- twinning stance is typed stub for tools with well-defined output patterns, unsupported_shape for anything stateful. This makes MCP clients testable: they can verify dispatch, auth enforcement, and input schema validation without a real backend.

title: REST response wrapper schemas override mutation target resources
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Apache Qakka OpenAPI 3.0.3 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apache.org/qakka/v1/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apache.org/qakka/v1/openapi.json -o /tmp/qakka.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/qakka.json --port 18136
python3 /tmp/qakka_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18136
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X DELETE 'http://127.0.0.1:18136/queues/demo'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"resource `apiresponses` has no primary key for path lookup"}
```

Expected response/output:
`DELETE /queues/{queueName}` is an item mutation on the path resource `Queue`/`queues`; the declared success response is a wrapper/status object `ApiResponse`. The twin should use the path/request resource for mutation targeting and path lookup, while using the response schema only for output shape. It should not target the `apiresponses` wrapper resource and then fail because the wrapper has no primary key.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL DELETE /queues/{queueName} status: status=501, expected 200
body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', 'detail': 'resource `apiresponses` has no primary key for path lookup'}
```

Startup excerpt:
```text
DELETE /queues/{queueName}  schema-first  apiresponses  medium  ok  [conflict]
[Flat CRUD candidate `queues` disagrees with schema-first candidate `apiresponses`; schema-first wins.]
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/topology.rs::auto_topology_entry()` lets schema-first response candidates win when they disagree with the path-derived resource. For mutation/delete routes with generic response wrappers such as `ApiResponse`, that makes `RouteEntry.effective_resource_name` point at the response wrapper instead of the path resource. Then `normalize.rs` performs path lookup against `apiresponses`, which has no primary key, and returns the unsupported shape. The classifier needs to keep target-resource derivation separate from response-shape derivation for mutation routes.

title: Enforce tournament-mode startup reset and memory budgets
description:
Make tournament economics an enforced acceptance surface rather than documentation and baseline tests only.

Current gap:
- PLAN_TWINNING treats bounded-memory overlays, fast reset, and shared base snapshots as correctness requirements.
- The repo has storage budget and restore/reset tests, but no end-to-end tournament workload gate that fails on unacceptable startup, reset, or resident-memory behavior.

Scope:
- Define a representative Postgres twin workload: restore/base snapshot, repeated write overlays, reset cycles, and final snapshot/report emission.
- Measure startup time, reset time, overlay growth, and private/RSS memory where the platform exposes it reliably.
- Add deterministic budget thresholds or platform-normalized thresholds with explicit skip/fail behavior.
- Keep performance artifacts separate from snapshot hashes.
- Make regressions visible in CI or in a clearly documented stop-ship local gate if CI hosts cannot measure reliably.

Acceptance target:
- Reset should remain fast enough for tournament loops, with a concrete budget recorded in the test or fixture.
- Shared base state must not be copied per candidate.
- Overlay cleanup must restore the committed base state exactly.

Non-goals:
- No new storage engine rewrite unless the gate proves the existing backend cannot meet the budget.
- No silently best-effort performance warnings.
acceptance_criteria:
- A tournament workload test exercises restore/base state, repeated overlays, reset, and final artifact emission.
- Startup, reset, overlay growth, and memory metrics are captured with deterministic thresholds or documented platform-normalized thresholds.
- The gate fails or emits an explicit stop-ship failure when budgets are exceeded; it does not silently warn.
- Snapshot hashes before and after reset prove base-state restoration exactly.
- CI behavior is documented: required where reliable, explicitly skipped with reason where platform measurement is unavailable.

title: REST DELETE /api/v4/projects/{id}/pipeline_schedules/{pipeline_schedule_id}/variables/{key} returns 202 {} on empty target
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official GitLab OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/raw/master/doc/api/openapi/openapi_v3.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/raw/master/doc/api/openapi/openapi_v3.yaml -o /tmp/gitlab_openapi_v3.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/gitlab_openapi_v3.yaml --serve --port 18251
```

Representative repro against an empty twin:
```bash
curl -i -X DELETE 'http://127.0.0.1:18251/api/v4/projects/1/pipeline_schedules/2/variables/KEY'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 202 Accepted
content-type: application/json

{}
```

Expected response/output:
The operation declares a `202` JSON response body with schema `#/components/schemas/APIEntitiesCiVariable`. On an empty backend, the delete should return a not-found response instead of reporting success. If a row is deleted and the route declares a JSON response body, the body should match the declared `APIEntitiesCiVariable` shape or the route should be refused if v0 cannot return deleted rows. It should not return a success status with `{}` for a declared object response.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAILURE DELETE /api/v4/projects/{id}/pipeline_schedules/{pipeline_schedule_id}/variables/{key} response shape: status=202; missing fields variable_type,key,value,hidden,protected,masked; body='{}'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:361-377` builds delete mutations with `returning: Vec::new()` even when the route declares a JSON response schema. Then `src/kernel/mutation.rs:232-237` returns a delete result with `rows_affected: 0` for empty targets, but `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:140-147` does not check `rows_affected` for deletes and emits `202 {}` whenever the route success status is not 204. Delete encoding should treat zero affected rows as not-found and should either request/encode returning rows for declared JSON delete responses or refuse that shape.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Fixed by CopperBasin: delete normalization now requests returning columns for body-bearing DELETE responses, delete encoding returns 404 for rows_affected=0 before applying declared success status, and body-bearing deletes encode returning rows instead of hard-coded {}. Verified with GitLab curl repro and full fmt/clippy/test gate.

Slack (slackAuth bearer) — same. Petstore (no schemes) — all requests pass without auth header, no false-positive 401s. Metrics: 100% of auth-required routes return 401 when credential missing; 0 false positives on public routes.

title: REST GET /documents/{id}/payments ignores invalid path id
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Billingo API v3 OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/billingo.hu/3.0.7/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/billingo.hu/3.0.7/openapi.json -o /tmp/billingo_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/billingo_openapi.json --serve --port 18213
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18213/documents/not-an-id/payments' \
  -H 'X-API-KEY: twinning-test'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response:
The spec declares the `id` path parameter on `GET /documents/{id}/payments` as integer. An invalid path value such as `not-an-id` should be rejected with a 400/422 type validation response before returning the child collection.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /documents/{id}/payments invalid path param rejected: status=200, body='[]'
SUMMARY operations=31 assertions=85 failures=10
```

Root cause hypothesis:
The route is classified as `ReadMany` over the `paymenthistorys` resource. `normalize_read_many()` in `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:260-278` builds only a query predicate and limit; it never receives or validates `path_params`, so parent-scoped child collection parameters are ignored entirely. `normalize_read_one()` does validate path parameters through `pk_predicate()` at `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:281-312`, but the read-many path has no equivalent parent-scope validation/coercion.

comments.author: CopperBasin
comments.text: Fixed in this session. REST routes now preserve declared path parameter schema metadata, and request normalization validates captured path parameter values before dispatching read or mutation operations. Billingo repro GET /documents/not-an-id/payments now returns 400 type_mismatch instead of 200 empty array. Billingo client is down to the remaining bd-3dt0 response-shape failures only. Validation passed: cargo fmt --check; cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test.

title: bd-v1r.7: Waterfall classifier — try policies in order, attribute per route, emit refusal reason
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Depends on
bd-2svi (bd-v1r.0 ResourceTopology), bd-1jel, bd-2jth, bd-2h5n (evidence extractors)

## Goal

Wire the ResourceTopology into classification. Also removes two early-exit guards from classify_route() that currently block the topology for the exact routes it needs to handle.

## CRITICAL CODE REALITY: Two early-exit guards must be removed

Current classify_route() in routes.rs has these guards at lines ~287-299:

```rust
if is_nested_path(pattern) {
    return RouteKind::Refusal { detail: "Nested resource paths not supported..." };
}

if is_fixed_prefix_crud_path(pattern) && !catalog.resources.contains_key(resource_name) {
    return RouteKind::Refusal { detail: "CRUD-shaped routes behind fixed path prefixes..." };
}
```

These guards refuse nested paths and prefix-prefixed paths BEFORE any topology lookup.
They must be removed or replaced with topology-aware logic, because:
- 1Password /vaults/{id}/items is a nested path → currently refused before topology
- WireMock /__admin/mappings is a fixed-prefix path → currently refused before topology

The guards represented v0 correctness (refuse what we can't handle). In v1, the topology CAN
handle these shapes. Remove both guards; the topology lookup + waterfall fallback replaces them.

## Deliverables

### 1. Update classify_route() signature

From:
```rust
fn classify_route(catalog: &RestCatalog, path: &str, method: Method, pattern: &PathPattern, resource_name: &str) -> RouteKind
```

To:
```rust
fn classify_route(catalog: &RestCatalog, path: &str, method: Method, pattern: &PathPattern, resource_name: &str, topology: &ResourceTopology, config: &RoutingConfig) -> RouteKind
```

### 2. Remove the two early-exit guards

Delete is_nested_path() early return and is_fixed_prefix_crud_path() early return.
(is_nested_path() and is_fixed_prefix_crud_path() helper functions can remain for use in
topology building but must not gate classification.)

### 3. Classification flow

```rust
// Step 1: topology lookup
if let Some(entry) = topology.path_bindings.get(path_pattern_key) {
    if entry.confidence >= Confidence::Medium {
        return route_kind_from_topology(entry, method, pattern);
    }
}
// Step 2: waterfall fallback (for Low confidence or no binding)
classify_with_waterfall(catalog, path, method, pattern, config)
```

### 4. Update build_route_registry() signature

From: pub fn build_route_registry(catalog: &RestCatalog) -> RouteRegistry
To:   pub fn build_route_registry(catalog: &RestCatalog, topology: &ResourceTopology, config: &RoutingConfig) -> RouteRegistry

Thread topology and config through to route_entry() and classify_route().

### 5. Stat logging

After classification, emit:
[rest] topology: N pinned, N high, N medium | waterfall fallback: N | refused: N

## Tests

- classify_nested_path_reaches_topology: /vaults/{id}/items is NOT refused by early guard
- classify_prefix_path_reaches_topology: /__admin/mappings is NOT refused by early guard
- classify_uses_topology_first: petstore, all routes at High confidence
- classify_falls_back_to_waterfall: route not in topology, waterfall classifies it
- classify_refuses_with_structured_reason: totally unknown route, logs all sources

## Acceptance

cargo test -p twinning rest::routes::classify green.
No route is refused solely because of is_nested_path() or is_fixed_prefix_crud_path() guards.
`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

title: REST GET /api/v4/groups/{id}/enterprise_users rejects OpenAPI-valid string id path params
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official GitLab OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/raw/master/doc/api/openapi/openapi_v3.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/raw/master/doc/api/openapi/openapi_v3.yaml -o /tmp/gitlab_openapi_v3.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/gitlab_openapi_v3.yaml --serve --port 18251
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18251/api/v4/groups/my-group/enterprise_users'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
content-type: application/json

{"code":"type_mismatch","detail":"field `id` expected integer but received my-group","expected":"integer","field":"id","received":"my-group"}
```

Expected response/output:
The operation declares the path parameter as `oneOf: [{type: string}, {type: integer}]`, so `my-group` is an OpenAPI-valid path value. The route should either execute normally against empty state or return a precise startup/runtime unsupported-shape refusal for ambiguous GitLab project/group path IDs. It should not reject the request as an integer type mismatch after the route was classified executable.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
SUMMARY operations=1696 assertions=4348 failures=489
483 failures were this class, e.g.
FAILURE GET /api/v4/groups/{id}/enterprise_users primary: unexpected status=400, body='{"code":"type_mismatch","detail":"field `id` expected integer but received id-44","expected":"integer","field":"id","received":"id-44"}'
FAILURE GET /api/v4/projects/{id}/resource_groups primary: unexpected status=400, body='{"code":"type_mismatch","detail":"field `id` expected integer but received id-1537","expected":"integer","field":"id","received":"id-1537"}'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1512-1535` stores declared OpenAPI path parameter schemas, and `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:496-522` validates them correctly. But after that, `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:1414-1465` chooses the resource primary-key column for lookup and `comparison_for_column()` at `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:1672-1684` coerces the raw path value against the response resource column type. For GitLab resources with integer `id` columns, this overrides the declared `oneOf(string, integer)` path parameter contract and rejects valid string IDs.
notes: Fixed by CopperBasin: REST normalizer now treats declared string-capable path parameters targeting non-null integer resource IDs as guaranteed no-match predicates instead of coercing them through integer PK point lookup. Exact GitLab repro GET /api/v4/groups/my-group/enterprise_users now returns 404 not_found instead of 400 type_mismatch. Validation: cargo fmt --check; cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test.

title: Close remaining Postgres plan gaps from PLAN_TWINNING
description:
Rollup for the remaining Postgres-centered gaps between docs/PLAN_TWINNING.md and the current implementation.

Context:
- Current implementation is honest bootstrap plus one-child run_once live mode.
- PLAN_TWINNING still describes a broader Postgres tournament wedge: interactive server lifecycle, fuller manifest-backed SQL subset, tournament storage economics, richer parity gates, and migration-proof orchestration.
- This epic should remain Postgres-only. It must not become a non-SQL interface or multi-engine bucket.

Scope:
- Track concrete, independently shippable Beads for runtime/server lifecycle, live mutation/read coverage, parity gates, query capture lineage, storage economics, and twin-pair proof orchestration.
- Keep all support claims manifest-backed and canary-proven.

Non-goals:

- No real hosting platform or live customer-data workflow.
- No broad SQL claims beyond canaries.
close_reason: Completed all concrete Postgres plan-gap beads

title: Legacy schema modernization — discover ideal schema from application usage archaeology, emerge it incrementally, prove equivalence, generate verified migration
description: Synthesizes bd-2r0q (query log replay), bd-2ndf (codebase audit), bd-rto9 (incremental data model synthesis), and bd-38ku (adversarial equivalence prover) into a complete legacy database modernization pipeline. Core insight: the applications are the specification for the legacy database. They encode through decades of usage what the database is actually supposed to do, even when the schema itself is a mess. The answer to 'what should the ideal schema be' is already written in the query logs and application code. Five-stage pipeline: (1) Schema archaeology — bootstrap a Postgres twin from legacy DDL; run query log replay and codebase audit simultaneously; result is a complete usage map: every table, column, join, filter ranked by frequency and cost; this is the legacy schema as actually used, stripped of everything added and never touched. (2) Semantic inference — agent reads the usage map and infers what the legacy schema means independent of what it says; naming is usually terrible but usage reveals semantics: a column filtered IN ('01','02','03') is an undeclared enum; a column always joining to one specific table is an undeclared foreign key; columns always NULL for certain row types reveal single-table inheritance that should be two tables; columns always read together are a candidate entity; a column written once and never updated is an immutable fact that belongs in a different modeling pattern; agent produces a semantic model: real entities, real relationships, real constraints in modern terms. (3) Ideal schema emergence — feed semantic model into incremental data model synthesis loop (bd-rto9); agent proposes the clean schema one verified increment at a time with the declared access patterns from step 1 as the query workload oracle; every increment is twin-validated; the schema that emerges is provably capable of serving every access pattern the legacy system actually served. (4) Equivalence proof — translate all queries from the usage map to work against the new schema; run adversarial equivalence prover (bd-38ku): legacy twin on left, new schema twin on right, full query corpus against both; any query where results differ is a translation error or genuine semantic difference requiring human review; witness set is exact — not 'something might differ' but 'this specific query returns different results'; prove equivalence before migrating a single byte of data. (5) Migration generation — sequence of verified DDL deltas from step 3 is the schema migration script already proved correct; data migration ETL is verified by replaying query log against post-migration new-schema twin; any query that worked before but fails or differs after migration is caught before production. CMD+RVL angle: any legacy data source being onboarded into the metadata catalog; this is the onboarding pipeline for data products with messy inherited schemas. Prerequisite: bd-2r0q, bd-2ndf, bd-rto9, bd-38ku all complete. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 15.

title: bd-auth.5: Add Auth column to startup routing report and auth summary line
description: Wave 3 parallel (after bd-auth.1). Add Auth column to per-route startup routing table: public | bearer | apiKey(X-API-KEY) | apiKey(?api_key) | multi(bearer|apiKey). Add to summary: N routes require auth, M are public. Acceptance: 1Password spec shows bearer for vault/item routes; Petstore shows public for all routes.

title: Snowflake seed JSONL contract export and import
description: Implement the simplest Snowflake seed JSONL v1 path. Export a deterministic LLM-fillable seed contract from the Snowflake catalog and import filled seed-data JSONL as Snowflake table rows before serve or run mode. V1 supports schema-backed startup only; seed import with materialize-source-url is an explicit structured refusal. Seed data replaces any rows already present from schema INSERT statements for startup. No fake data generation in twinning.
design: Add small CLI flags such as --export-seed-contract FILE and --seed FILE on the snowflake command. Contract targets are Snowflake table names. Import operates on SnowflakeCatalog table rows, not the shared Postgres kernel storage. Rows must include every declared column exactly once; nullable columns may be null. Type validation should use existing SnowflakeType coercion rules or a small shared helper with source materialization. No key or constraint enforcement is promised unless the Snowflake catalog already exposes that metadata.
acceptance_criteria: Golden contract export for a tiny Snowflake DDL fixture is stable. Import tests cover valid rows, malformed JSONL, unknown table, unknown column, missing column, type mismatch, and unsupported type refusal. Config tests refuse seed with materialize-source-url. A DDL-with-INSERT fixture proves --seed replacement semantics. Query tests prove imported rows are returned through existing SELECT result paths and existing JSON and Arrow result encoders. Report row counts are deterministic.

title: Replace pgwire_listener SQLSTATE decode panic helper with Result-based helper
description: Focused bug-hunt follow-up. tests/pgwire_listener.rs still decodes backend ErrorResponse SQLSTATEs with a helper that panics on malformed frames. Replace it with a Result-based helper and update the assertions so malformed protocol frames surface as test failures through explicit expectations instead of helper panics.
close_reason: Replaced pgwire_listener panic-based SQLSTATE decode helper with Result-based helper

title: Support schema-load materialized endpoints in twin-pair orchestration
description:
Upgrade twin-pair proof orchestration from restore-only inputs toward the endpoint model in the futures plan by supporting schema-load plus source-materialized endpoints.

Current gap:
- Twin-pair proof has a prototype report model and restore-backed paths.
- Tests currently preserve a refusal for schema-load bootstrap/materialization orchestration.
- PLAN_TWINNING_FUTURES makes twin-pair migration proof the first deferred artifact direction, reusing the Postgres kernel and snapshot contract.

Scope:
- Define endpoint specs for legacy and candidate twins that can bootstrap from schema plus materialized source data, not only prebuilt snapshots.
- Reuse existing catalog/schema normalization, materialized row loading, report/snapshot hashing, and refusal taxonomy.
- Freeze both endpoints into comparable snapshots before replay/proof phases.
- Preserve deterministic artifact identities and explain endpoint identity in the proof report.
- Replace the schema-load refusal test with successful orchestration coverage, while preserving refusal tests for genuinely unsupported endpoint shapes.

Non-goals:
- No direct live production database mutation.
- No non-Postgres endpoints.
- No new truth/canonicalization decisions inside twinning; input materialization remains already-decided data.
acceptance_criteria:
- Twin-pair endpoint specs can bootstrap both sides from schema plus materialized source data as well as restore-backed snapshots where already supported.
- Proof reports include deterministic endpoint identities and source/materialization artifact references without inventing truth decisions.
- Existing schema-load refusal coverage is replaced by successful orchestration coverage for the newly supported endpoint shape.
- Unsupported endpoint shapes still produce explicit refusal envelopes.
- Snapshot hashes for materialized endpoints are stable across repeated runs with the same schema and data.

title: Snowflake Marketplace vendor replacement optimizer — twin every listing and current vendor feed, prove optimal replacement bundle with coverage matrix, quality proof, and cost model
description: Requires a Snowflake wire protocol twin (new protocol family). The pipeline uses twinning for structural qualification and the existing Crucible spine (benchmark, verify, assess, pack) for coverage and quality — no custom evaluation logic needed. Phase 1 — Structural qualification via twin: twin the schema of every Marketplace listing and the current vendor feed; twin answers field compatibility (semantic field mapping from names, descriptions, types — px_close vs SECURITY_PRICE_CLOSE vs close_price_usd), query structural validity (can canonical queries execute against this schema?), EXPLAIN analysis (will queries be efficient given declared indexes?); eliminates non-viable listings without spending a single Snowflake credit; narrows hundreds of listings to a small survivor set. Phase 2 — Sample pull: request free sample data from structural survivors only (most Marketplace providers offer this); this is the only phase that touches real data. Phase 3 — Spine evaluation against survivors: benchmark — vendor feed sample is the gold set, Marketplace listing sample is the candidate; benchmark scores exactly how much of the gold set the listing covers and where it diverges; this is the coverage tool; verify — does the Marketplace data satisfy declared constraints (value ranges, referential integrity, freshness rules, data quality assertions); assess — policy decision given benchmark score and verify results: viable replacement or not, with quantified scores; pack — sealed evidence bundle with all benchmark, verify, and assess artifacts; this is the board-level decision artifact. Phase 4 — Bundle optimization: model optimal combination of assessed listings against vendor feed cost; output is assess-scored proof with price tag. Phase 5 — Continuous discovery: auto-twin new listings for structural qualification immediately on publication; sample pull and spine evaluation only if structural qualification passes. Key insight: this is not a custom pipeline — it is the existing Crucible spine workflow (benchmark, verify, assess, pack) with Marketplace data as input, the same tools that prove migration quality today applied to vendor replacement. CMD+RVL angle: cmdrvl-curves clients pay for vendor financial data feeds; the spine tools already exist; the only new primitive is the Snowflake wire protocol twin. Requires Snowflake wire protocol twin, bd-38ku (equivalence prover), bd-2r0q (query log replay). Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 16.

title: REST POST /collections/{collectionId}/items targets STAC parent collection
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public STAC Server OpenAPI 3.0.3 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stac-utils/stac-server/main/src/lambdas/api/openapi.yaml

Source docs:
https://stac-utils.github.io/stac-server/latest/reference/openapi/

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stac-utils/stac-server/main/src/lambdas/api/openapi.yaml -o /tmp/stac_server_openapi.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/stac_server_openapi.yaml --serve --port 18211
python3 /tmp/stac_server_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18211
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18211/collections/demo/items' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"type":"Feature","stac_version":"1.0.0","id":"feature-1","geometry":null,"bbox":[0,0,1,1],"properties":{"datetime":"2020-01-01T00:00:00Z"},"links":[],"assets":{}}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"request body schema `<inline request body schema>` omits required target column(s) [description, extent, license] for resource `collections`; REST twin v0 cannot synthesize server-generated or default values"}
```

Expected response/output:
`POST /collections/{collectionId}/items` is the STAC transaction route for creating an item in a collection. The request body is a STAC Item/Feature object with required item fields such as `id`, `type`, `stac_version`, `geometry`, `bbox`, `properties`, `links`, and `assets`. The route should target the item/feature resource, or if REST v0 cannot materialize required array/object fields, it should produce a route-level item-shape refusal. It should not validate the item body as though the client must supply parent collection fields such as `description`, `extent`, and `license`.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /collections/demo/items create collection item status: status=501, expected=[200, 201], body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"request body schema `<inline request body schema>` omits required target column(s) [description, extent, license] for resource `collections`; REST twin v0 cannot synthesize server-generated or default v'
SUMMARY assertions=37 failures=1
```

Root cause hypothesis:
The adaptive topology sees the first literal path segment `collections` and binds nested STAC item routes to the parent `collections` resource instead of the terminal child resource or request/response schema. The startup route table shows `POST /collections/{collectionId}/items` as `flat-crud`, resource `collections`, confidence `high`. In `src/protocol/rest/topology.rs:328-336`, `auto_topology_entry()` prefers `flat_in_catalog` when `schema_in_catalog` is absent even if `path_shape_binding()` identified a child collection shape. Then `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:557-581` accepts that classification, and `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:88-97` normalizes the POST against `route.effective_resource_name` (`collections`), leading to the misleading missing parent fields refusal.

Priority: P2 because an advertised executable nested create route deterministically refuses valid STAC Item bodies due to wrong target-resource classification.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Fixed in this session. Topology now lets a concrete child path-shape resource beat a parent flat-CRUD candidate when schema evidence is absent or only wrapper-shaped. Verified the STAC repro now returns a precise item-body refusal (`request body field `bbox` has array type...`) instead of missing parent `collections` fields; `/tmp/stac_server_client.py` reports 38 assertions, 0 failures. Quality gate passed: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test.

title: Document OpenAPI vs x-twinning behavioral parity gaps
description:
Document the gap surfaced by the OpenFIGI twin experiment: OpenAPI schemas describe request/response envelope shape, but they do not necessarily encode provider behavior for misses, optional auth, rate-limit tiers, per-item warnings, ambiguity, or unstubbed request fallback.

Observed case:
- Live OpenFIGI `POST /v3/mapping` with CUSIP `000000000` returns HTTP 200 with `[{"warning":"No identifier found."}]`.
- The current OpenFIGI fixture only stubs success for CUSIP `037833100`.
- An unstubbed but schema-valid dummy CUSIP falls through to Twinning schema synthesis and returns `{"data":null,"error":null,"warning":null}`, which is valid-looking but not live-like.
- The fixture marks `ApiKeyAuth` as required, so Twinning returns 401 without the header; live OpenFIGI allows unauthenticated requests with a lower rate-limit tier.

Acceptance criteria:
- Add documentation near the REST/x-twinning docs or OpenFIGI fixture explaining schema shape vs behavioral parity.
- Include the OpenFIGI success, miss, and optional-auth examples as concrete cases.
- Explain that agents with live API access should capture representative request/response pairs and encode them as `x-twinning.response-stubs`.
- Warn that unstubbed schema fallback can hide provider-semantic gaps and should not be treated as live parity.
- Point Canon/OpenFIGI tests at documented stub-authoring guidance rather than relying on live OpenFIGI for routine validation.
close_reason: Documented in docs/OPENFIGI_SEARCH_SEMANTIC_TWIN_PACKET.md with live-measured /search corpus, x-twinning overlay, replay proof, and stub-authoring guidance. Behavioral-parity control gaps deferred to bd-3r96.

title: REST component response refs are not resolved for route classification
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Apideck Webhook OpenAPI 3.0.3 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apideck.com/webhook/9.3.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apideck.com/webhook/9.3.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/apideck_webhook.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apideck_webhook.json --port 18135
python3 /tmp/apideck_webhook_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18135
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18135/webhook/logs'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /webhook/logs"}
```

Expected response/output:
The `eventLogsAll` operation declares its `200` response as `$ref: "#/components/responses/GetWebhookEventLogsResponse"`, and that component response points at `#/components/schemas/GetWebhookEventLogsResponse`. The twin should resolve component response refs during route classification, then either execute the route with the declared wrapper object shape or return a precise unsupported response-ref refusal. It should not report the declared route as outside the CRUD subset.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /webhook/logs status: status=501, expected 200
FAIL GET /webhook/logs data array: body={'code': 'unsupported_shape', ...}
```

Startup excerpt:
```text
GET /webhook/logs  -  -  -  REFUSED
REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /webhook/logs
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs::ComponentsObject` does not retain `components.responses`, so route construction cannot resolve response component refs. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs::response_ref()` returns the response `$ref` string directly, but `component_resource_name()` only accepts `#/components/schemas/...`; `#/components/responses/...` therefore never becomes a schema/resource candidate. Routes whose path literal does not independently match a catalog resource, such as `/webhook/logs`, fall through to the generic outside-CRUD refusal.
close_reason: Completed: component response refs now resolve to JSON schemas for REST classification and wrapper encoding

title: REST OpenAPI YAML numeric response status keys fail startup decode
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Bookstore REST Example OpenAPI 3.0.0 YAML spec.

Spec URL:
https://gist.githubusercontent.com/jhigginbotham/7a1c8aa12b74c7c15fd8f053a438843d/raw/Bookstore-REST-Example_v1.0.oas3.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://gist.githubusercontent.com/jhigginbotham/7a1c8aa12b74c7c15fd8f053a438843d/raw/Bookstore-REST-Example_v1.0.oas3.yaml -o /tmp/bookstore_rest_example.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/bookstore_rest_example.yaml --port 18151
```

Actual response:
```text
REFUSAL [E_OPENAPI_PARSE]
OpenAPI spec `/tmp/bookstore_rest_example.yaml` could not be decoded.
detail: {"error":"invalid type: integer `200`, expected a string","source":"/tmp/bookstore_rest_example.yaml"}
```

Expected response/output:
The REST twin should parse real OpenAPI YAML that uses unquoted numeric response status keys such as `200:` and `404:` under `responses`, or normalize those keys before serde decoding. Startup should not fail before route classification.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs::OperationObject.responses` is typed as `HashMap<String, ResponseObject>`. `serde_yaml` preserves unquoted YAML mapping keys like `200:` as integers, so deserialization fails before `parse_rest_catalog_bytes()` can build a catalog. A bounded fix is to deserialize response maps with a custom key visitor accepting both string and integer status-code keys, or deserialize the raw document into a YAML value and normalize `responses` keys before typed decoding.

title: REST child singleton metadata routes return arrays instead of object or 404
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Apicurio Registry OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json -o /tmp/apicurio_registry.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apicurio_registry.json --port 18132
python3 /tmp/apicurio_registry_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18132
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18132/artifacts/artifact-1/meta'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
The OpenAPI operation `getArtifactMetaData` declares a `200` response body of `#/components/schemas/ArtifactMetaData`, an object. With no matching state, the twin should return a structured 404 if the metadata row is absent, or a JSON object if present. It should not return a collection array for a singleton metadata route.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
PASS GET /artifacts/{artifactId}/meta status: status=200, expected=[200, 404], body=[]
FAIL GET /artifacts/{artifactId}/meta response object shape: body=[]
```

Root cause hypothesis:
The topology/classifier treats the three-segment shape `[literal, param, literal]` as `ResourceRelationship::ChildCollection` regardless of the operation response schema. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs::route_kind_from_relationship()` maps `ChildCollection + GET` to `RouteKind::ReadMany`, so singleton metadata routes with object response schemas execute as filtered scans and encode empty results as `[]` rather than object/404 semantics.
close_reason: Completed: child object response GET routes now use singleton encoding

title: bd-v1r.9: Regression suite — 6 campaign specs achieve ≥80% route classification
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Depends on
bd-2ngm (bd-v1r.8) — full pipeline including topology + report must be in place

## Goal

Regression suite for 6 campaign specs. Two coverage metrics: (1) route coverage ≥80%, (2) confidence coverage ≥90% of classified routes at High or Pinned confidence.

## Campaign Specs

| Spec | Source | Expected topology result | Route coverage | Confidence target |
|------|--------|-------------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Petstore | tests/fixtures/routing/petstore.yaml | all flat-crud High | 100% | 100% High |
| Reqres | tests/fixtures/routing/reqres.yaml | /api scope, User High | ≥80% | ≥90% High |
| WireMock admin | tests/fixtures/routing/wiremock-admin.yaml | x-twinning pinned OR auto prefix-scoped High | ≥80% | ≥90% Pinned/High |
| Uber estimates | tests/fixtures/routing/uber-estimates.yaml | /v1 scope detected, High | ≥80% | ≥90% High |
| 1Password | tests/fixtures/routing/1password.yaml | nested Item ChildCollection High | ≥80% | ≥90% High |
| Google Books | tests/fixtures/routing/google-books.yaml | /books/v1 scope or schema-first | ≥80% | ≥80% High |

## Deliverables

### 1. Test fixtures — `tests/fixtures/routing/`

Download and commit 6 static spec files. No network access in tests.

### 2. topology_coverage helper — `tests/routing_coverage.rs`

```rust
fn check_topology_coverage(
    spec_path: &str,
    config: RoutingConfig,
    min_route_coverage: f64,
    min_confidence_coverage: f64,  // NEW: fraction of classified routes at High or Pinned
) -> (usize, usize, usize) // (classified, total, high_confidence_count)
```

Assert both: classified/total >= min_route_coverage AND high_conf/classified >= min_confidence_coverage.

### 3. Six #[test] functions

```rust
#[test]
fn petstore_100pct_all_high() {
    check_topology_coverage("petstore.yaml", RoutingConfig::default(), 1.0, 1.0);
}

#[test]
fn reqres_80pct_90pct_high() {
    check_topology_coverage("reqres.yaml", RoutingConfig::default(), 0.80, 0.90);
}
// ... etc
```

### 4. Conflict report in test output

Print the topology's warnings vec so regressions show exactly which routes degraded and why.

## Acceptance

All 6 tests pass with auto-detection (no manual --routing flags).
Both coverage metrics pass (route ≥80% AND confidence ≥90% for 5 of 6 specs).
`cargo test --test routing_coverage` green.

title: REST listener returns 404 instead of 405 for unsupported method on declared path
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public openapi3_parser Uber example.

Spec source page:
https://www.ruby-toolbox.com/projects/openapi3_parser

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kevindew/openapi3_parser/main/spec/support/examples/uber.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kevindew/openapi3_parser/main/spec/support/examples/uber.yaml -o /tmp/openapi3_parser_uber.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/openapi3_parser_uber.yaml
```

Repro request:
```bash
curl -i -X POST "http://127.0.0.1:8080/products" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{}"
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
content-type: application/json

{"code":"not_found","detail":"route not found"}
```

Expected response:
The path `/products` exists in the OpenAPI spec, but only declares `GET`. A request using `POST /products` should be distinguished from an unknown path and return `405 Method Not Allowed` with a structured `method_not_allowed` JSON error, ideally including an `Allow: GET` header.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/listener.rs` `dispatch_inner()` calls `match_route(&state.routes, method, uri.path())` around lines 416-422. `match_route()` filters by method and path together, so a path match with a method mismatch is indistinguishable from a missing path and falls into the 404 branch. The listener likely needs a method-agnostic path-pattern match before the 404 branch so it can return 405 when the path exists for another method.
close_reason: Completed: declared paths with unsupported methods now return structured 405 with Allow header; added listener regression test and verified the Uber spec repro.

title: REST GET /team.info returns bare array for Slack singleton wrapper
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Slack Web API OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec after `bd-2byt` fixed collection wrappers.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/slack.com/1.7.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/slack.com/1.7.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json --serve --port 18201
python3 /tmp/slack_web_api_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18201 /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18201/team.info"
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
The Slack spec declares `GET /team.info` as an object response with wrapper fields such as `ok` and a singleton payload field `team`. In an empty twin it should either return an object preserving that declared wrapper shape, for example `{"ok":true,"team":null}`, or return a precise unsupported-shape refusal. It must not return a top-level bare array for an object response.

Related campaign failures with the same shape:
```text
FAIL GET /reminders.info response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /team.info response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /users.profile.get response shape: expected object, got list
```

Additional direct repros:
```bash
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18201/reminders.info?reminder=R123"
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18201/users.profile.get?user=U123"
```
Both returned `200 []`.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs::response_wrapper()` can attach wrapper metadata to routes, but `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:184` only passes `route.response_wrapper` into `encode_read_many_payload()`. `RouteKind::ReadOne` goes through `encode_read_one_payload()` without wrapper support, so singleton wrapper routes that classify to a component resource serialize the empty result as the generic read payload shape instead of the declared wrapper object. A narrow fix likely needs singleton response-wrapper metadata and encoder handling for `ReadOne`, without regressing collection wrappers fixed by `bd-2byt`.

title: REST GET /conversations.history returns bare array for Slack wrapper
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Slack Web API OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/slack.com/1.7.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/slack.com/1.7.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json --serve --port 18199
python3 /tmp/slack_web_api_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18199 /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18199/conversations.history?channel=C0123456789"
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
The Slack spec declares `GET /conversations.history` as an object response with required fields `ok`, `messages`, `has_more`, `pin_count`, `channel_actions_ts`, and `channel_actions_count`. In an empty twin it should still return an object preserving the wrapper shape, for example `{"ok":true,"messages":[],...}` or a precise unsupported-shape refusal. It must not return a top-level bare array.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /conversations.history response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /admin.conversations.search response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /reminders.info response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /reminders.list response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /team.accessLogs response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /team.info response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /team.integrationLogs response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /usergroups.list response shape: expected object, got list
FAIL GET /users.profile.get response shape: expected object, got list
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs::inline_response_wrapper()` only records inline wrapper metadata when the array payload field matches path/resource hints. Slack response wrappers use semantic payload names such as `messages`, `channels`, `reminder`, `team`, `usergroups`, and `profile` that do not match method-shaped paths like `/conversations.history`, so the route can classify against the inner resource but loses the declared wrapper. `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs` then serializes the read result as the bare resource array. The router should preserve inline response wrapper shape when an inline success schema is an object with a component-ref payload and metadata fields, even when the payload field name does not match the path literal.

title: REST GET /availabilities returns 404 for nested Nitro response wrapper
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public BBC Nitro OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/bbc.com/1.0.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/bbc.com/1.0.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/bbc_nitro_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/bbc_nitro_openapi.json --serve --port 18173
python3 /tmp/bbc_nitro_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18173
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18173/availabilities
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18173/v1/episodes/episode-pid/ancestors
```

Actual responses:
```http
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
content-type: application/json

{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"resource `nitros` has no primary key for path lookup"}
```

Expected response/output:
Both routes declare a 200 response with `application/json` schema `#/components/schemas/nitro`. The `nitro` schema is a response envelope with fields such as `pagination` and `results`; empty state should still return a successful wrapper, e.g. `200 {"results": ...}` with empty result content and stable wrapper fields. It should not be encoded as a singleton read that returns resource-level 404, and child/list endpoints should not reach path lookup against the wrapper resource.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /availabilities returns nitro wrapper: status=404, body='{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}'
FAIL GET /v1/episodes/episode-pid/ancestors returns nitro wrapper: status=501, body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"resource `nitros` has no primary key for path lookup"}'
FAIL GET /broadcasts?page=1&page_size=1 accepts declared pagination: status=404, body='{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}'
SUMMARY assertions=29 failures=26
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1418-1431` only recognizes component response wrappers when the cataloged response resource has a direct array column. BBC Nitro's `nitro` response schema instead has a nested `results` object (`results -> #/components/schemas/results`) plus metadata fields. Since `resource_is_array_wrapper()` returns false, no `ResponseWrapper` is attached. Then `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:683-710` treats object-shaped GET responses as singleton routes, which leads `encode_read_one_payload()`/normalization to return 404 or a no-primary-key path lookup refusal instead of encoding an empty wrapper response.

This is related to wrapper detection bugs such as `bd-3rig`, but this repro is specifically for nested result-envelope component schemas where the array/list fields are inside a referenced `results` object rather than directly on the response wrapper.
