title: REST POST /Files/{FileId}/Associations drops FileId path field
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Xero Files OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml -o /tmp/xero_files.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/xero_files.yaml --serve --port 18160
python3 /tmp/xero_files_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18160
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18160/Files/4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c/Associations \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"ObjectId\":\"1270bf7c-5d18-473a-9231-1e36c4bd33ed\",\"ObjectGroup\":\"Contact\",\"ObjectType\":\"Business\"}"
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
content-type: application/json

{"AssociationDateUtc":null,"CreatedDateUtc":null,"FileId":null,"Name":null,"ObjectGroup":"Contact","ObjectId":"1270bf7c-5d18-473a-9231-1e36c4bd33ed","ObjectType":"Business","SendWithObject":null,"Size":null}
```

Expected response/output:
The operation creates an association under `/Files/{FileId}/Associations`; the returned association schema contains `FileId`. The created row should include the path-scoped `FileId` value (`4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c`) or return a precise unsupported-shape refusal if v0 cannot materialize parent-scoped create fields. Returning 201 with `FileId: null` is silent wrong data.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
PASS POST /Files/{FileId}/Associations
PASS POST /Files/{FileId}/Associations shape
FAIL POST association copies FileId from path: body={'AssociationDateUtc': None, 'CreatedDateUtc': None, 'FileId': None, 'Name': None, 'ObjectGroup': 'Contact', 'ObjectId': '1270bf7c-5d18-473a-9231-1e36c4bd33ed', 'ObjectType': 'Business', 'SendWithObject': None, 'Size': None}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs::normalize_insert()` builds insert rows only from the JSON request body via `mutation_fields_from_object()`. It does not merge matching path parameters into the mutation row for child create routes. `FileId` is present in the target resource and in the path params, but because it is absent from the body it is written as null in the returned row.

title: bd-v1r.6: prefix-scoped policy — auto-detect base prefix, strip it, then classify
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Depends on
bd-3jp2 (bd-v1r.3), bd-1jel (bd-v1r.4 flat-crud normalize helper)

## Can run in parallel with
bd-v1r.4 (flat-crud), bd-v1r.5 (schema-first)

## Goal
Implement the prefix-scoped policy: detect a shared base prefix across all paths, strip it, then run flat-crud on the remainder. Auto-detection uses a ≥60% threshold; user can override with --base-prefix or x-twinning.base-prefix.

## Why this matters

Campaign failures all had a common base prefix:
- Reqres: /api/users, /api/products, /api/unknown → prefix /api
- Uber: /v1/estimates/price, /v1/estimates/time, /v1/me → prefix /v1
- Google Books: /books/v1/volumes, /books/v1/bookshelves → prefix /books/v1
- WireMock: /__admin/mappings, /__admin/requests → prefix /__admin

After stripping the prefix, flat-crud (with normalization) classifies the remainder.

## Deliverables

### 1. auto_detect_prefix function — `src/protocol/rest/policy.rs`

```rust
/// Returns the longest prefix shared by ≥60% of the spec's paths.
/// Returns None if no such prefix exists or if paths < 2.
pub fn auto_detect_prefix(paths: &[&str]) -> Option<String>
```

Algorithm:
1. Collect all path segments, group by first N segments
2. For each candidate prefix (longest first), compute coverage fraction
3. Return first prefix where coverage ≥ 0.60
4. Minimum prefix depth: 1 segment (e.g. /v1, /api, /__admin)
5. Maximum prefix depth: auto (stop when coverage drops below threshold)

Edge case: single-path specs → return None (nothing to compare against).

### 2. prefix_scoped_classify function

```rust
pub fn prefix_scoped_classify(
    path: &str,
    catalog: &RestCatalog,
    base_prefix: &str,  // resolved prefix (auto-detected or user-supplied)
) -> Option<String>
```

Logic: strip base_prefix from path, call flat_crud_classify on remainder with base_prefix=None.

### 3. PrefixScopedClassifier struct (stateful, per spec)

```rust
pub struct PrefixScopedClassifier {
    pub detected_prefix: Option<String>,
    pub effective_prefix: String, // final resolved prefix
}

impl PrefixScopedClassifier {
    pub fn new(
        all_paths: &[&str],
        config_prefix: Option<&str>,
    ) -> Self
}
```

Constructor runs auto_detect_prefix, then applies user override if Some.

### 4. Wire into classify_route

Instantiate PrefixScopedClassifier once at route-building time (not per-route). Pass it into the waterfall in Wave 4.

## Tests

- `auto_detect_prefix_api`: ["/api/users", "/api/products", "/api/orders"] → Some("/api")
- `auto_detect_prefix_v1`: ["/v1/me", "/v1/estimates/price", "/v1/estimates/time"] → Some("/v1")
- `auto_detect_prefix_mixed`: ["/api/users", "/health", "/metrics"] → None (health+metrics break threshold)
- `auto_detect_prefix_admin`: ["/\_\_admin/mappings", "/\_\_admin/requests", "/\_\_admin/scenarios"] → Some("/\_\_admin")
- `prefix_scoped_classify_reqres`: /api/users with prefix /api → User
- `user_override_beats_auto_detect`

## Acceptance

`cargo test -p twinning rest::policy::prefix_scoped` green.
Reqres e2e: all /api/* routes classified with prefix-scoped policy.
`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

title: Update operator.json and doctor capabilities to reflect REST twin surface
description:
## Problem

`twinning --describe` emits operator.json that describes a postgres-only tool. The REST twin is now twinning's primary feature surface and is completely absent from the manifest. An agent or orchestrator reading `twinning --describe` gets a false picture of the tool's capabilities.

Specific gaps:

**invocation.usage**: `twinning rest --spec <FILE>` missing entirely. Only postgres and proof usage strings present.

**options**: All REST flags absent — `--spec`, `--host`, `--port`, `--run`, `--serve`, `--report`, `--canary`, `--strict`, `--routing`, `--base-prefix`. Auth mode flag (`--auth-mode`) will be added by bd-bni1 and should be included once that bead lands.

**current_runtime_behavior.implemented_surface**: No mention of:
- REST twin (OpenAPI 3.x → HTTP server)
- v1 adaptive routing policy engine (flat-crud, schema-first, prefix-scoped, topology graph)
- ResourceTopology with confidence scoring
- x-twinning OpenAPI extension
- Startup routing report with route coverage and confidence metrics
- Auth shape compliance (upcoming bd-bni1)
- Remote $ref resolution via ureq (bd-hepv)

**deferred_surface**: Still lists "non-Postgres protocol families" as deferred — REST is live, not deferred.

**artifacts**: `twinning.rest-report.v0` is mentioned in doctor.rs capabilities but missing from operator.json artifacts array.

**doctor.capabilities**: The doctor capabilities report in `src/protocol/rest/doctor.rs` (or wherever rendered) says "V0 bypasses auth, refuses nested paths, omits pagination, and treats oneOf/anyOf as text" — this is now stale after the e2e campaign. Nested paths are handled via topology, oneOf/anyOf are partially handled (bd-3hbt), auth is upcoming.

## Deliverables

### 1. Update operator.json

`invocation.usage` — add REST usage strings:
```
"twinning rest --spec <FILE> [--host HOST] [--port PORT] [--run COMMAND | --serve] [--report FILE] [--canary FILE] [--routing POLICY] [--base-prefix PREFIX] [--strict] [--json]"
"twinning rest --spec <FILE> --json"
```

`options` — add REST flag objects:
- `{ "name": "spec", "flag": "--spec", "type": "file_path", "description": "OpenAPI 3.x YAML or JSON spec file. Supports local refs; remote $ref URLs fetched at startup." }`
- `{ "name": "host", "flag": "--host", "type": "string", "default": "127.0.0.1", "description": "REST listen host" }`
- `{ "name": "port", "flag": "--port", "type": "integer", "default": 8080, "description": "REST listen port" }`
- `{ "name": "run", "flag": "--run", "type": "string", "description": "Run one child command against the live REST server, then exit" }`
- `{ "name": "serve", "flag": "--serve", "type": "flag", "description": "Run interactive REST server until SIGINT/SIGTERM" }`
- `{ "name": "report", "flag": "--report", "type": "file_path", "description": "Write REST session report as JSON (twinning.rest-report.v0)" }`
- `{ "name": "canary", "flag": "--canary", "type": "file_path", "description": "Assert REST session report against canary expectations" }`
- `{ "name": "routing", "flag": "--routing", "type": "string", "description": "Routing policy override: flat-crud | schema-first | prefix-scoped | auto (default)" }`
- `{ "name": "base_prefix", "flag": "--base-prefix", "type": "string", "description": "Explicit path prefix to strip before resource classification (e.g. /api, /__admin)" }`
- `{ "name": "strict", "flag": "--strict", "type": "flag", "description": "Fail startup if any route cannot be classified" }`

`current_runtime_behavior.implemented_surface` — add:
- "REST twin: OpenAPI 3.x YAML/JSON → live HTTP server with Postgres-backed storage"
- "v1 adaptive routing policy engine: flat-crud, schema-first, prefix-scoped evidence sources combined into ResourceTopology at startup"
- "ResourceTopology with confidence scoring (Pinned/High/Medium/Low) and per-route conflict reporting"
- "x-twinning OpenAPI extension for per-spec routing policy, base prefix, and auth mode overrides"
- "Startup routing report: route coverage, confidence coverage, per-route classification table"
- "Remote $ref URL resolution with 5s timeout and in-process cache"
- "Response wrapper detection for data/items/results/entries payload fields and hint-matched semantic names"
- "Auth shape compliance: enforce credential presence per declared security schemes, 401/403 with WWW-Authenticate (upcoming bd-bni1)"

`deferred_surface` — remove "non-Postgres protocol families" (REST is live). Add:
- "Auth credential validation (any non-empty credential in declared format is accepted in shape mode)"
- "Pagination response envelope materialization"
- "PUT/PATCH/DELETE route coverage beyond POST/GET"

`artifacts` — add:
```json
{
  "name": "twinning.rest-report.v0",
  "kind": "session_report",
  "description": "REST session report: spec identity, routing table with confidence scores, exercised endpoints, refusals, constraint violations, and optional canary assertions"
}
```

### 2. Update doctor capabilities description

In `src/protocol/rest/doctor.rs` (or `src/doctor.rs` — wherever the REST capabilities description string lives), update:

Old: `"V0 bypasses auth, refuses nested paths, omits pagination, and treats oneOf/anyOf as text."`

New: `"V1 REST twin with adaptive routing (flat-crud, schema-first, prefix-scoped topology), response wrapper detection, remote $ref resolution, and auth shape compliance. Nested paths handled via ResourceTopology. Bypasses auth credential validation in bypass mode (shape mode enforces credential presence)."`

### 3. Bump operator.json version field

Update `"version"` in operator.json to match `Cargo.toml` current version (check `env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")` at build time, or keep in sync manually).

## Acceptance criteria

- `twinning --describe | jq '.invocation.usage'` includes REST usage strings
- `twinning --describe | jq '.options[] | select(.name == "spec")'` returns the spec option
- `twinning --describe | jq '.current_runtime_behavior.implemented_surface'` mentions REST twin and ResourceTopology
- `twinning --describe | jq '.artifacts[] | select(.name == "twinning.rest-report.v0")'` returns the artifact
- `twinning doctor capabilities --json | jq '.commands[] | select(.command | contains("rest"))'` returns the REST command entry with updated description
- `cargo test` passes (no regression)

title: bd-v1r.5: schema-first policy — derive resource from response schema $ref
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Depends on
bd-3jp2 (bd-v1r.3) — RouteEntry.response_resource_name must be populated

## Can run in parallel with
bd-v1r.4 (flat-crud), bd-v1r.6 (prefix-scoped)

## Goal
Implement the schema-first policy: ignore path structure entirely and use the response schema $ref component name as the resource identifier. This is the most robust policy for real-world APIs where path naming conventions don't match schema names.

## Why this matters

WireMock: GET /__admin/mappings returns schema `StubMappings` → effective resource = StubMappings
1Password: GET /vaults/{id}/items returns schema `Items` → effective resource = Items
Google Books: GET /books/v1/volumes returns schema `Volumes` → effective resource = Volumes

None of these are classifiable by path. All are trivially classifiable by response schema.

## Deliverables

### 1. schema_first_classify function — `src/protocol/rest/policy.rs`

```rust
pub fn schema_first_classify(
    route: &RouteEntry,
    catalog: &RestCatalog,
) -> Option<String>  // returns effective_resource_name if response schema is in catalog
```

Logic:
1. Take `route.response_resource_name` (already computed in bd-v1r.3)
2. Check catalog.resources.contains_key(response_resource_name)
3. Return Some(response_resource_name) if hit, else None

For array response schemas (e.g. Pets = array of Pet items): also check the `items` $ref — if the items type is a catalog resource, return that. This handles the common pattern where the list wrapper schema differs from the item schema.

### 2. Wire into classify_route

When policy is SchemaFirst OR Auto: call schema_first_classify(), set matched_policy and effective_resource_name if matched.

### 3. Catalog lookup for array item schemas

In `src/protocol/rest/spec.rs`, when building RestCatalog, record the items $ref for array-typed schemas:

```rust
pub struct ResourceMeta {
    pub item_resource: Option<String>, // e.g. "Pet" for Pets = array of Pet
}
```

The classify function should also accept `ResourceMeta` so it can dereference the item type.

## Tests

- `schema_first_direct_hit`: route with response_resource_name="Pet" in catalog → Some("Pet")
- `schema_first_array_unwrap`: route with response_resource_name="Pets" (array wrapper), catalog has "Pet" as items type → Some("Pet")
- `schema_first_miss`: route with no response schema → None
- `schema_first_unknown_schema`: response schema name not in catalog → None

## Acceptance

`cargo test -p twinning rest::policy::schema_first` green.
Reqres e2e (GET /api/users returns Users schema): classified as User via schema-first.
`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

title: Spotify audio-analysis route advertised ok but runtime-refuses no primary key
description:
Spec URL: https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/spotify.com/sonallux/2023.2.27/openapi.json
Local spec: /tmp/spotify_sonallux_openapi.json
Client: /tmp/spotify_sonallux_client.py
Client output: /tmp/spotify_sonallux_client.out

Repro:
1. Start the twin:
   cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/spotify_sonallux_openapi.json --serve --port 18281
2. Observe startup table marks this route executable:
   GET /audio-analysis/{id}  schema-first  audioanalysisobjects  bearer  ok
3. Request it:
   curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer test-token' http://127.0.0.1:18281/audio-analysis/11dFghVXANMlKmJXsNCbNl

Actual response:
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"resource `audioanalysisobjects` has no primary key for path lookup"}

Expected response:
The twin should not advertise this route as startup `ok` if the runtime cannot build a path lookup. Either classify/refuse it at startup with a precise unsupported_shape reason, or map the declared path parameter to a valid response-resource identity and return the normal empty-state/object response.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs` classifies GET /<resource>/{id} as `RouteKind::ReadOne` based on path shape (see `route_kind_from_crud_pattern`, around lines 986-1000) after schema-first selects `audioanalysisobjects`. It does not check whether the selected response resource has a primary key or a column/path alias that can satisfy the `{id}` lookup. The runtime later discovers this in `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs` around lines 1537-1543 and returns `RestRefusal::UnsupportedShape` (`resource ... has no primary key for path lookup`).

title: REST parser drops declared HEAD operations from route registry
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Authentiq API OpenAPI 3.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/6-dot-authentiqio.appspot.com/6/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/6-dot-authentiqio.appspot.com/6/openapi.json -o /tmp/authentiq.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/authentiq.json --port 18082
```

The spec declares `head` operations for both `/key/{PK}` and `/scope/{job}`. The twin starts, but startup warnings only mention the non-HEAD operations because HEAD is silently dropped.

Repro requests:
```bash
curl -I http://127.0.0.1:18082/key/public-key
curl -I http://127.0.0.1:18082/scope/job123
```

Actual output:
```http
HTTP/1.1 405 Method Not Allowed
content-type: application/json
content-length: 45
```

Expected output:
Because HEAD is declared in the OpenAPI spec, it should not be treated as an undeclared method on an otherwise matching path. If REST twin v0 does not support HEAD execution, it should still register/classify the declared operation and return the normal structured route-refusal envelope, for example HTTP 501 `unsupported_shape` with detail `REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /key/{PK}`. Startup warnings should also account for the declared HEAD operations.

Client used for the campaign:
`/tmp/authentiq_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18082` produced 4 failures, all from the two declared HEAD operations returning 405 instead of the expected declared-route refusal.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs` `PathItem` models `get`, `post`, `put`, `patch`, and `delete`, but has no `head` field, so serde drops OpenAPI HEAD operations during parsing. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs` `operations()` around lines 429-446 also only enumerates those five methods, and `Method` has no `Head` variant. This is distinct from generic method-mismatch handling: the operation is declared in the spec but never enters the route registry.

title: Snowflake seed JSONL template export and import
description: Implement the shared seed JSONL contract for the Snowflake wire twin after the Postgres contract is settled. Add CLI flags on twinning snowflake to export a seed template from the Snowflake catalog and import seed rows into SnowflakeCatalog table state before serve or run mode. Keep it simple: no generation, no faker data, no source sampling. Template export should include database/schema/table identity, columns, Snowflake declared types, normalized row value expectations, and nullable/default metadata where available. Seed import should make rows visible to supported SELECT and metadata/result paths and should not imply broader DML support. Acceptance: export-seed-template FILE writes deterministic JSONL; seed FILE loads deterministic table rows; invalid JSONL, unknown table/column, and incompatible value shapes return structured refusals; report row counts include seeded rows; focused tests cover SELECT from seeded rows and invalid seed refusal.
close_reason: Superseded by tightened seed JSONL v1 beads bd-3qz1, bd-2szc, bd-2ugj, bd-1zc4, and bd-140n.

title: REST GET /v1/quote returns 500 for projected json/array response columns
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public LI.FI OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://docs.li.fi/openapi.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://docs.li.fi/openapi.yaml -o /tmp/lifi_openapi.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/lifi_openapi.yaml --serve --port 18189
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18189/v1/quote?fromChain=1&toChain=1&fromToken=tw-test&toToken=tw-test&fromAmount=tw-test&fromAddress=0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
content-type: application/json

{"code":"kernel_error","detail":"read results currently support only null, boolean, integer, and text-like scalar values"}
```

Expected response/output:
The startup route table advertises `GET /v1/quote` as executable (`schema-first`, resource `steps`, status `ok`). A baseline read should not return a 500. It should either serialize the safe scalar subset of the seeded row, return an empty/missing resource response, or be refused explicitly before startup/runtime execution if the response shape is unsupported.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /v1/quote baseline not server error: status=500, body='{"code":"kernel_error","detail":"read results currently support only null, boolean, integer, and text-like scalar values"}'
FAIL GET /v1/quote/toAmount baseline not server error: status=500, body='{"code":"kernel_error","detail":"read results currently support only null, boolean, integer, and text-like scalar values"}'
FAIL GET /v1/status baseline not server error: status=500, body='{"code":"kernel_error","detail":"read results currently support only null, boolean, integer, and text-like scalar values"}'
FAIL GET /v1/relayer/quote baseline not server error: status=500, body='{"code":"kernel_error","detail":"read results currently support only null, boolean, integer, and text-like scalar values"}'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs:1476-1483` projects every catalog column for REST reads via `projection_columns()`, including `json`, `bytes`, and `array` columns. The read kernel later converts projected `KernelValue`s to `ScalarValue`s in `src/kernel/read.rs:468-484` and rejects `KernelValue::Json`, `KernelValue::Bytes`, and `KernelValue::Array` with this 500-level storage/kernel error. REST already has a safe response-column filter (`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1818-1820`), and read projection likely needs the same safe-scalar filter or a precise route/runtime refusal.

Priority: P1 because an advertised executable GET route returns an internal server error.

title: REST OpenAPI array item and allOf metadata retention
description: Retain enough OpenAPI schema metadata for arrays and allOf-merged request/response shapes to support OpenFIGI. Needed metadata includes root array request/response schemas for /mapping, array item schemas, nullable item flags, minItems/maxItems for two-element intervals, object response wrappers such as { values: array<string> }, and allOf inheritance from CommonSearchMappingRequest into MappingJob/SearchRequest/FilterRequest.
design: Extend RestCatalog/ResourceSchema/ResourceColumn/route metadata without breaking existing normalized_type behavior. Keep warnings for still-unsupported array shapes until consuming beads implement them. Avoid ad hoc OpenFIGI path checks; model the schema shape generically.
acceptance_criteria: Spec parser tests assert BulkMappingJob and BulkMappingJobResult are root arrays of object refs; NullableNumberInterval and NullableDateInterval retain minItems=2/maxItems=2 and nullable items; inline_response_200 retains values: array<string>; allOf properties are visible on MappingJob/SearchRequest/FilterRequest.
notes: Depends on B01 and B02. Touch likely file: crates/twinning-rest/src/spec.rs plus route/catalog tests.

title: Implement production twin-pair orchestration runner
description: Implement the production twin-pair orchestration phases defined in PLAN_TWINNING_FUTURES once the manifest parser exists: preflight endpoints, materialize or restore snapshots, verify compatibility, replay through the declared protocol surface, emit the proof report, and hand report/snapshot paths to pack without sealing inside twinning.
close_reason: Completed: implemented restore-backed twin-pair orchestrate runner with manifest-driven endpoint identities, replay/report emission, output snapshot handoff, bundle directory creation, artifact hash checks, and explicit schema-load refusal coverage.

title: twinning federate CLI suite — federation protocol subcommands for the open fixed income standard
description:
Implement the full `twinning federate` subcommand family that enables participants to join the industry schema federation. This is the participant-facing tooling described in docs/PLAN_OPEN_STANDARD_FIXED_INCOME.md — it makes the participant journey a one-afternoon operation.

Subcommands to implement:

`twinning federate init --schema schema.sql`
Bootstrap a twin from the participant's DDL. Sets up local state for the federation workflow. At minimum: validates the DDL, runs the existing postgres bootstrap, writes a local .twinning-federate/ directory with twin state and session metadata. For now this is a wrapper around the existing postgres twin bootstrap — the new piece is the session state directory.

`twinning federate analyze --pg-stat-statements postgresql://host/db`
Connect to the participant's live Postgres, pull pg_stat_statements view (already normalized to $1/$2 placeholders — no actual data values present), and produce a local query workload file. Output is a JSON array of normalized query templates with frequency weights. The participant reviews this file before anything leaves their environment.

`twinning federate preview`
Display what will be shared: the abstract semantic model derived from the schema and query workload. Shows entity names, relationships, and access patterns — no table names, column names, or data values unless participant opts in. This is the consent step — nothing leaves until `submit` is run.

`twinning federate submit --endpoint <url>`
POST the approved semantic model to the federation endpoint. The semantic model is the output of `preview`, not the raw schema or query log. The POST payload is a JSON document containing: entity list (anonymous or named per participant preference), relationship graph, access pattern signatures.

`twinning federate validate --increment N`
The federation proposes a DDL increment (fetched from the endpoint or provided as a local file). This command: applies the proposed DDL to the local twin, runs the participant's declared query workload against it, reports pass/fail with the specific failing query patterns annotated. Output: increment N validation PASS/FAIL, query count, plan quality distribution, missing index suggestions. Runs entirely locally — no data leaves.

`twinning federate report --increment N`
Submit the validation result from `validate` back to the federation endpoint. The report contains only the pass/fail result and the annotated failing query patterns (if any) — not the participant's data.

`twinning federate views --output <file>`
Generate the SQL views that bridge the participant's internal schema to the current federated standard schema. Output is a .sql file containing CREATE VIEW statements of the form `CREATE VIEW bond AS SELECT CPNRT AS coupon_rate, ...`. The mapping is derived from the semantic field mapping established during `analyze` and the federated DDL.

`twinning federate verify-views --source postgresql://host/db`
Run equivalence verification: execute a sample of the participant's declared query patterns through the generated views against the federated schema, compare results to the same queries against their internal schema, report equivalence pass/fail. This is the participant's equivalence proof — the cryptographic claim that the standard serves their workload.

Implementation notes:
- Session state lives in .twinning-federate/ in the working directory
- All network operations are to/from the federation endpoint only (not to participant's internal DB, which is accessed locally)
- pg_stat_statements pull requires only SELECT on pg_stat_statements — no superuser needed
- The semantic inference step (analyze → abstract model) is the hardest part: entity name inference from table/column names, relationship inference from foreign key constraints and JOIN patterns in query log, access pattern classification (point lookup vs range scan vs aggregation)
- Phase 3 tooling in the plan — not needed for Phase 1 (seed with cmdrvl-curves), but must exist before a second participant can join

Reference: docs/PLAN_OPEN_STANDARD_FIXED_INCOME.md — The Tooling section and Participant Journey section.

title: REST Stripe collection routes return singleton 404 instead of empty collection
description:
Found during `bd-87fq` Stripe REST e2e campaign.

Spec URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json
Local spec: /tmp/stripe_openapi.json
Client: /tmp/stripe_client.py
Client output: /tmp/stripe_client.out
Server command used because port 18401 was already occupied:

```bash
target/debug/twinning rest --spec /tmp/stripe_openapi.json --serve --port 18411 --auth-mode shape --routing auto
```

Repro:

```bash
curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything' 'http://127.0.0.1:18411/v1/customers?limit=3'
curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything' 'http://127.0.0.1:18411/v1/charges?limit=3'
curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything' 'http://127.0.0.1:18411/v1/payment_intents?limit=3'
curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything' 'http://127.0.0.1:18411/v1/products?limit=3'
curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything' 'http://127.0.0.1:18411/v1/prices?limit=3'
curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything' 'http://127.0.0.1:18411/v1/subscriptions?limit=3'
curl -i -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything' 'http://127.0.0.1:18411/v1/invoices?limit=3'
```

Actual:
Each top-level collection route above returns `404` with `{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}` against the empty backend. The same pattern also appeared on nested collection routes like `/v1/customers/cus_test_123/payment_methods`, `/v1/customers/cus_test_123/bank_accounts`, and `/v1/charges/ch_test_123/refunds`.

Expected:
Executable collection/search routes should not use singleton not-found semantics. With no rows they should return a `200` empty collection/list wrapper when materializable, or a precise `501 unsupported_shape` refusal if the route cannot be represented without partial semantics.

Campaign details:
The client exercised 67 authorized operations and 8 missing-auth probes across customers, charges, payment_intents, products, prices, subscriptions, and invoices. Status counts were `{"200": 7, "404": 19, "405": 1, "501": 40}`. No 500s or panics occurred.

Root cause hypothesis:
The routing table reports these list routes as `ok` prefix-scoped collection routes, but execution/encoding appears to apply singleton path lookup or `ReadOne` semantics when the backend has no rows. Inspect route-kind classification and read planning between `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs`, `src/protocol/rest/policy.rs`, and `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs` for prefix-scoped collection routes.
close_reason: Fixed prefix-scoped collection classification so Stripe root and child collections remain read-many and return empty collection bodies instead of singleton 404s.

title: REST mutation encoder ignores declared 204 no-content success responses
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Apicurio Registry OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json -o /tmp/apicurio_registry.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apicurio_registry.json --port 18132
python3 /tmp/apicurio_registry_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18132
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18132/rules' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"type":"VALIDITY","config":"FULL"}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
content-type: application/json

{"config":"FULL","type":"VALIDITY"}
```

Expected response/output:
The OpenAPI operation `createGlobalRule` declares success status `204` with no response body. The twin should honor declared no-content mutation responses when a route is classified as executable:
```http
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
```

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /rules (createGlobalRule) status: status=201, expected=[204], body={'config': 'FULL', 'type': 'VALIDITY'}
FAIL POST /artifacts/{artifactId}/rules (createArtifactRule) status: status=201, expected=[204], body={'config': 'FULL', 'type': 'VALIDITY'}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/encode.rs::encode_mutation_payload()` hardcodes `201 Created` for every insert and emits a returning row whenever the kernel returns one. `RouteEntry` currently carries response fields but not the OpenAPI-declared success status or a no-content marker, so the encoder cannot distinguish POST operations that should return `204` from conventional create operations that should return `201` with a body.
close_reason: Completed: mutation encoding honors declared bodyless 204 success responses

title: REST GET /v1/accounts/{account}/people/{person} returns synthetic row for empty singleton
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official public Stripe OpenAPI 3.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json -o /tmp/stripe_spec3.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/stripe_spec3.json --serve --port 18174
```

Exact curl repro against a freshly started empty twin:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18174/v1/accounts/acct_test/people/person_test \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_twinning'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{"account":"","additional_tos_acceptances":null,"address":null,"address_kana":"","address_kanji":"","created":0,"dob":null,"email":"","first_name":"","first_name_kana":"","first_name_kanji":"","future_requirements":"","gender":"","id":"","id_number_provided":false,"id_number_secondary_provided":false,"last_name":"","last_name_kana":"","last_name_kanji":"","maiden_name":"","metadata":null,"nationality":"","object":"","phone":"","political_exposure":"","registered_address":null,"relationship":null,"requirements":"","ssn_last_4_provided":false,"us_cfpb_data":"","verification":null,"full_name_aliases":[]}
```

Expected response/output:
The backend starts empty for this spec (`No spec examples found - backend starts empty`). A singleton path with account/person identifiers should either return `404 {"code":"not_found"...}` for the missing resource or be explicitly refused if the path lookup cannot be normalized. It must not return a fabricated successful `Person` object full of default scalar values.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
singleton_empty: 33
  - GET /v1/accounts/{account}/people/{person}
  - GET /v1/accounts/{account}/persons/{person}
  - GET /v1/apple_pay/domains/{domain}
SUMMARY operations=587 assertions=1055 failures=404
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1663-1684` treats any array-typed response column as a response-wrapper payload. Stripe's `person` schema has an incidental array field (`full_name_aliases`), so `component_response_wrapper()` at `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1431-1514` misclassifies the singleton object schema as an array wrapper and emits static default values for every other scalar field. The classifier path around `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:683-700` then lets the response shape override the singleton relationship into a collection-style read, so empty storage encodes as a 200 wrapper object instead of a missing singleton.

Priority: P1 because this is wrong data/silent success for an empty singleton resource.

title: SQL codebase audit — crawl GitHub repos for SQL statements, extract referenced tables from metadata catalog, twin them, find schema drift and performance regressions
description: Crawl a codebase (local or GitHub) for all SQL: .sql files, migration files, embedded SQL strings in Python/Ruby/JS/Go source, ORM raw query calls. Parse each statement with sqlparser (already a Cargo dependency) to extract exactly which tables and columns are referenced — not the whole schema, just what each query touches. Catalog lookup: pull DDL for only those tables from the metadata catalog, targeted per-query extraction. Bootstrap a Postgres twin from that minimal DDL — fast, disposable, one twin per query or per file. Execute each SQL against the twin and classify results: (a) schema drift — 'column foo does not exist' means column was renamed or dropped; 'relation bar does not exist' means table dropped or renamed; type mismatch errors mean column type changed; these are queries that are silently broken or will break soon; (b) performance regressions — query runs clean but EXPLAIN shows full sequential scan on a large table, nested loop on an unindexed foreign key, missing index usage, N+1 patterns detectable from query shape. Rewrite pass: for performance candidates, hand them to the SQL tournament (bd-gwlk) to generate optimized alternatives against the same twin; emit plan comparison showing original vs optimized EXPLAIN side by side. Structured report output: schema drift findings with exact error, file path, and line number; performance findings with EXPLAIN output and severity score; suggested rewrites with plan delta. Pre-migration safety angle: before applying a schema migration, run every SQL statement in the codebase against the post-migration twin; every failure is a query that will break in production after the migration ships — full impact surface known before a single byte of schema change lands. CI gate variant: on every PR, extract only the SQL that changed or that references tables touched by changed migrations, run through the audit pipeline, fail the PR if schema drift or severe plan regressions are found. sqlparser is already in Cargo.toml at version 0.53 — the parsing layer exists. CMD+RVL angle: audit cmdrvl-cli, aibuildout, and any repo with SQL against the metadata catalog schema on every metadata migration. Prerequisite: Postgres tournament twin v0 complete, metadata catalog DDL export. Companion to bd-gwlk (SQL tournament). Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 11.

title: bd-v1r.8: Startup routing report — per-route policy attribution table
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Depends on
bd-1xrt (bd-v1r.7) — classification must populate confidence + conflict fields

## Goal

Print the routing attribution table at startup with confidence and conflict columns so operators immediately see which routes are solid vs. uncertain.

## Deliverables

### 1. RoutingReport struct — `src/protocol/rest/report.rs` (new file)

```rust
pub struct RouteAttribution {
    pub method: String,
    pub path: String,
    pub matched_policy: Option<EvidenceSource>,
    pub effective_resource: Option<String>,
    pub confidence: Option<Confidence>,
    pub conflict: Option<String>,     // what the losing evidence source said
    pub status: AttributionStatus,
}
```

### 2. Display format

```
METHOD  PATH                         POLICY        RESOURCE    CONFIDENCE  STATUS
------  --------------------------   -----------   ----------  ----------  ------
GET     /pets                        flat-crud     Pet         high        ok
POST    /pets                        flat-crud     Pet         high        ok
GET     /pets/{petId}                flat-crud     Pet         high        ok
GET     /vaults/{id}/items           schema-first  Item        high        ok  [flat-crud said: Vault]
GET     /__admin/mappings            x-twinning    StubMap     pinned      ok
GET     /v1/mystery                  —             —           —           REFUSED
```

Rows where confidence=low or conflict is present: highlighted with [brackets] showing what the losing source said.

### 3. Summary line

```
[rest] 6 routes ok (3 high, 2 pinned, 1 medium) | 0 waterfall fallbacks | 1 refused
       ⚠  1 conflict detected — see rows marked [conflict] above
```

### 4. Wire into startup

After classification in the main rest command handler, build RoutingReport and call log_at_startup().

## Tests

- `routing_report_shows_conflict_annotation`: entry with conflict shows [flat-crud said: Vault] in output
- `routing_report_confidence_column`
- `routing_report_summary_with_warning`

## Acceptance

`cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/petstore.yaml` prints table with confidence column.
`cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/wiremock.yaml` shows pinned for all /__admin/* routes when x-twinning present.
`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

title: REST scalar mutation response schemas are encoded as request objects
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public OWASP crAPI OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/crAPI/develop/openapi-spec/crapi-openapi-spec.json -o /tmp/crapi_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/crapi_openapi.json --port 18150
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18150/community/api/v2/coupon/new-coupon' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"coupon_code":"TRAC075","amount":"75"}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{"amount":"75","coupon_code":"TRAC075"}
```

Expected response/output:
The declared 200 response schema is `#/components/schemas/AddCouponResponse`, a scalar string. The twin should either encode a string response shape or return a precise unsupported scalar-response refusal. It should not echo the request-body resource object as the successful response.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /community/api/v2/coupon/new-coupon response shape: expected string, got dict, body='{"amount":"sample","coupon_code":"sample"}'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
Scalar response component schemas materialize as zero-field resources and are filtered out by topology/resource selection, so the route targets the request schema resource (`AddCouponRequest`). `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:131-135` then encodes mutation `RETURNING` rows using `route.response_fields`, with no branch for scalar response schemas or a precise unsupported-response refusal.
notes: Paused/returned to open. Route-layer code in the shared worktree makes scalar component response schemas return a precise 501 unsupported scalar-response refusal, and the crAPI repro POST /community/api/v2/coupon/new-coupon returned 501 with the new detail. Full gate was not completed because shared reserved files outside this bead are currently red: bd-hepv spec.rs is incomplete/unformatted and bd-15ml topology.rs is unformatted. Do not close until the full gate runs clean after those land.

title: bd-v1r.2: CLI flags --routing and --base-prefix wired into RestArgs and RestConfig
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Depends on
bd-v2s6 (bd-v1r.1): RoutingPolicy enum and RoutingConfig must exist before wiring CLI

## Goal
Surface the routing policy and prefix override as first-class CLI flags so operators can twin any API without modifying the spec.

## Deliverables

### 1. `RestArgs` — add two new optional fields

File: `src/cli/args.rs` (or wherever RestArgs is defined — find with `rg 'struct RestArgs'`)

```rust
/// Routing policy: auto | flat-crud | schema-first | prefix-scoped | nested
#[arg(long, value_enum)]
pub routing: Option<RoutingPolicy>,

/// Base path prefix to strip before resource lookup (e.g. /api, /__admin)
#[arg(long)]
pub base_prefix: Option<String>,
```

Derive `clap::ValueEnum` for `RoutingPolicy` so clap renders valid variants in help text.

### 2. `RestConfig` — carry resolved config

```rust
pub struct RestConfig {
    // existing fields ...
    pub routing: RoutingConfig,
}
```

Build site (wherever RestConfig is constructed from RestArgs + spec): call `resolve_routing_config(args.routing, args.base_prefix, parse_x_twinning(&spec))`.

### 3. Thread RoutingConfig through to classify_route

`classify_route()` in `routes.rs` currently takes `catalog: &RestCatalog`. Add `routing: &RoutingConfig` parameter and pass it from the call site.

Do not implement any new classification logic here — that is Wave 3 work. Just thread the parameter through and store it on RouteEntry as `routing_config_snapshot: Option<RoutingPolicy>` (or similar) so Wave 4–7 can read it.

## Tests

- `routing_cli_override` integration test: parse a petstore spec with `routing = Some(FlatCrud)` and `base_prefix = Some("/api")`; assert resolved config has `policy = FlatCrud` and `base_prefix = "/api"`.
- `routing_clap_parse` unit test: build an arg vector with `--routing schema-first`, parse with clap, assert variant.

## Acceptance

`cargo run -- rest --help` shows `--routing` and `--base-prefix` options.
`cargo test` passes with no regressions.
`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

title: REST API seed JSONL template export and import
description: Implement the shared seed JSONL contract for the REST API twin. Add CLI flags on twinning rest to export a seed template from the OpenAPI-derived RestCatalog and to import seed rows into the shared committed REST backend before serve or run mode. Keep it simple: no generation, no faker data, no automatic examples-as-data unless separately approved. Template export should identify resource name, route-derived fields, normalized field types, nullability, required fields, and primary-key/path lookup surfaces. Seed import should validate rows using the existing REST resource schema and kernel storage path, then make them visible to GET/list/read-one routes. Acceptance: export-seed-template FILE writes deterministic JSONL; seed FILE loads deterministic resource rows; malformed JSONL, unknown resource/field, missing required field, type mismatch, and constraint failures return structured REST/twinning refusals; report/session behavior remains deterministic; focused tests cover POST-free seeded GET/list behavior and invalid seed refusals.

title: Open standard seed — run cmdrvl-curves as participant zero for the fixed income federation
description:
Execute Phase 1 of the open fixed income data standard initiative using cmdrvl-curves as participant zero. This bead produces the artifacts needed to launch tranchelist.com as the neutral hub.

Reference: docs/PLAN_OPEN_STANDARD_FIXED_INCOME.md — Phase 1: Seed section.

Steps:

1. Export cmdrvl-curves schema DDL
   pg_dump --schema-only the cmdrvl-curves Postgres → schema.sql
   Review for anything proprietary that should be anonymized.

2. Export pg_stat_statements from cmdrvl-curves Postgres
   Query pg_stat_statements view — already normalized to $1/$2 placeholders.
   Review and optionally redact queries that reveal sensitive access patterns.
   
3. Run semantic inference (Claude does this today with existing twinning catalog output — no new tooling needed for this step)
   Input: schema DDL + normalized query workload
   Output: abstract semantic model — entity names (bond, issuer, auction, coupon_schedule), relationships (bond→issuer, bond→auction), access patterns (point lookup by CUSIP, range scan by maturity_date, aggregation by issuer_lei)
   No internal table names or column names in the output unless we opt in.

4. Produce v0.1 anonymous semantic model and v0.1 federated Treasury schema
   Target ~40 fields: instrument_id (CUSIP+ISIN), maturity_date, coupon_rate, coupon_frequency, day_count_convention, issue_date, first_coupon_date, original_face_value, issuer_lei, currency, call_schedule (structured), put_schedule (structured), reopening_flag, auction_date
   Write the schema as a versioned DDL file: federated_treasury_v0.1.sql

5. Stand up public Postgres twin of FiscalData.treasury.gov data in the v0.1 schema at tranchelist.com
   Use twinning postgres --schema federated_treasury_v0.1.sql to bootstrap the twin locally.
   Populate via --materialize-source-url pointed at a FiscalData.treasury.gov-sourced Postgres, OR manually load the public FiscalData CSV exports into the twin using the federated field names.
   Expose the twin as a publicly queryable endpoint at tranchelist.com.

6. Publish at tranchelist.com:
   - federated_treasury_v0.1.sql (MIT licensed, versioned)
   - Methodology disclosure (how the schema was derived, one participant, anonymous semantic model attached)
   - Equivalence proof for participant zero (cmdrvl-curves): v0.1 schema serves every query pattern that database declared
   - Instructions for joining

The key disclosure at launch: 'This standard was derived from one participant's production database. Here is the anonymous semantic model it came from. Here is the equivalence proof that the v0.1 schema serves every query pattern that database declared. We are seeking a second participant.'

Output artifacts:
- schema.sql (cmdrvl-curves DDL, reviewed for anonymization)
- query_workload.json (normalized pg_stat_statements, reviewed for redaction)
- semantic_model_v0.json (abstract model, anonymized, the thing that gets shared)
- federated_treasury_v0.1.sql (the v0.1 standard DDL)
- equivalence_proof_participant_zero.json (proof that v0.1 serves cmdrvl-curves workload)
- tranchelist.com publication (schema, methodology, proof, join instructions)

Note: Steps 1-4 can be executed today with existing twinning capabilities. Step 5 requires a running Postgres instance at tranchelist.com. Step 6 requires tranchelist.com publishing infrastructure.

title: Temporal Postgres twin — time-travel debugging at the wire level via MVCC versioned in-memory store
description: Every write the twin accepts is versioned with a monotonic transaction ID; the in-memory store is an MVCC log not a mutable row table. Expose SET twinning.as_of = '<timestamp>' — any pgwire client (psql, ORM, language driver) gets time travel without code changes. SET twinning.as_of_left and as_of_right enables differential time travel: run a query at two timestamps and emit both result sets with structural diff annotation. Incident replay: load twin from WAL-derived snapshot at failure timestamp, run failing query, reproduce exact database state. Migration safety: query the canonical corpus at pre-migration and post-migration schema states, diff results — regressions are query result divergences not schema diffs. Storage: retain all committed versions within a configurable retention window; beyond the window, versions are snapshotted and evicted to a twinning.snapshot.temporal.v0 content-addressed chain; any point in the window is live-queryable; any point beyond is restorable. The MVCC architecture needed for tournament-mode concurrent readers is the same structure — retain all committed versions instead of GC-ing old ones. Prerequisite: Postgres tournament twin v0 complete. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 1.

title: bd-auth.1: Parse global and per-operation security requirements into RouteEntry::required_auth_schemes
description: Wave 1. Add security: Vec<HashMap<String, Vec<String>>> to OperationObject and OpenApiDoc. For each route in build_route_registry(), resolve effective security scheme names (operation override > global > empty) and store as RouteEntry::required_auth_schemes: Vec<String>. No enforcement. Acceptance: required_auth_schemes populated correctly for 1Password (bearerAuth on vault/item routes), Petstore (empty on all routes), Slack (slackAuth on all routes).

title: Production query log replay — pull pg_stat_statements from real database, replay against twin, find pre-migration breakage and optimization targets ranked by actual production cost
description: Pull the production query log from pg_stat_statements: SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time, total_exec_time, rows FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC. This gives every distinct query shape that actually ran, how many times, and its total cost — already ranked by production impact. pg_stat_statements normalizes parameters to $1 $2 placeholders which is exactly right for twin replay: testing query structure against the schema, not testing values against data. Bootstrap a Postgres twin from the current schema DDL. Replay the query log in priority order (highest total_exec_time first). Classify results: (a) Errors — the query ran fine on the old schema but fails against the twin with the new schema; these are pre-migration breakage findings, stack-ranked by call count so the highest-traffic broken queries appear first; (b) Plan analysis — compare EXPLAIN output from the twin against the mean_exec_time from the log; if the plan looks expensive but real DB was fast, an index exists that the twin doesn't have declared; if both the twin plan and real DB were slow, genuine optimization target; (c) Top-N by total_exec_time — hand to SQL tournament (bd-gwlk) for rewrite candidates and emit plan comparison. Key distinction from codebase audit (bd-2ndf): the codebase audit finds queries that exist in source files; this finds queries that actually ran — including ORM-generated SQL, ad-hoc queries, dynamic string-built SQL, and anything that never appears in source. Together they give complete coverage: one tests what is written, the other tests what is executed. Pre-migration safety: before shipping a schema migration, replay the production query log against the post-migration twin; every error is a query that will break ranked by how often it runs; every plan change is a performance regression or improvement; full production query distribution validated against the new schema before any user sees the migration. Alternative sources: pgaudit log for full query text with actual parameters; Postgres CSV log file; RDS/Aurora performance insights export. CMD+RVL angle: run against the metadata database before every metadata migration; aibuildout enrichment queries are the highest-cost targets for the optimization pass. Prerequisite: Postgres tournament twin v0 complete, pg_stat_statements access. Companion to bd-gwlk (SQL tournament) and bd-2ndf (codebase audit). Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 12.

title: Implement pgwire startup, auth, parameter status, and session loop

Context:

close_reason: Validated existing pgwire startup, metadata, and session-loop implementation

title: Chaos organism — genetic algorithm over twin chaos configuration space to evolve minimal adversarial fault scenarios
description: Traditional chaos engineering requires human intuition about which failure scenarios matter. The chaos organism eliminates this by evolving failure scenarios automatically. A genetic algorithm operates over the space of (chaos configuration x request ordering x timing parameters). Each genome specifies which twins fail, in what patterns, at what rates, in what sequence. Each generation: spawn twin fleet with current generation's genomes, run system under test against each fleet configuration, score the results. Scoring function measures interesting failures not just error rate: responses that are wrong but not obviously wrong (200 OK with corrupted data), failures that propagate incorrectly (service A fails, service B should degrade gracefully but crashes), failures exposing undeclared state dependencies (service B behavior changes based on undocumented coupling to service A state). Interesting failure detector: twin in observation mode records every response; fitness function compares observed responses against spec-declared contracts; spec-valid but semantically wrong responses (empty array when spec says non-empty, wrong count field) score higher than simple errors — these are bugs production monitoring misses. Selection pressure toward minimal genomes: prefer smaller failure configurations producing same interesting failures as larger ones. Evolved genome is the minimal adversarial scenario human intuition would never construct. Agent swarm application: each agent evaluates one genome per generation; 50-agent swarm evaluates 50 genomes per generation; 100 generations = 5000 chaos configurations evaluated while a human engineer would review one. Prerequisites: chaos mode (bd-2xyo), contract mesh (or multi-twin orchestration). Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md section 5.

title: Expand migration proof replay manifest beyond prototype subset
description: PLAN_TWINNING_FUTURES still needs a broader checked-in replay manifest beyond point lookup, filtered scan, aggregate count, divergence, and SQLSTATE parity. Add explicit PASS/FAIL/SKIP coverage for joins, introspection, and historical-query families as the declared subset allows. Acceptance: fixture matrix accounts for supported and skipped shapes, harness coverage fails on drift, and unsupported shapes remain SKIP or protocol-visible refusals.
close_reason: Completed: expanded replay manifest accounting for PASS/FAIL/SKIP coverage, including explicit join, introspection, and historical-query SKIP boundaries with harness drift assertions.

title: Data product gap analysis — inverse Marketplace optimizer: given declared analytical use cases, rank data acquisition options by ROI with spine-quality evidence
description: Inverts the Marketplace optimizer (bd-20ps). Instead of 'what do I have and what replaces it cheaper?' asks 'what analytical questions can I not answer, and what data would I need to acquire to answer them?' Input: a set of declared analytical use cases — questions the business wants to ask but cannot. Pipeline: (1) structural qualification via twin — for every available data source (Snowflake Marketplace, vendor feeds, internal products), does its schema surface support the queries required by each use case? Eliminates non-viable sources without data access; (2) sample pull from structural survivors; (3) benchmark scores coverage against declared use case requirements — how much of the use case query workload does this source satisfy?; (4) verify validates data quality against declared constraints; (5) assess produces acquisition priority ranking: acquiring source A unlocks these five use cases, acquiring source B unlocks only one; (6) pack seals the evidence bundle. Output is a data acquisition investment thesis ranked by analytical ROI with spine-quality evidence attached. Currently this analysis costs months of data strategy consulting and produces a PowerPoint. This produces it from schema artifacts and use case declarations with benchmark and verify scores as evidence. CMD+RVL angle: cmdrvl-curves clients want to expand analytical capabilities; this tells them exactly which data acquisitions unlock the most value. Prerequisite: bd-20ps (Snowflake twin), spine tools. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_ALIEN_DIRECTIONS.md.

title: bd-v1r.0: ResourceTopology struct and build_spec_topology() — combine all evidence sources at startup
description:
## Parent epic
bd-3j4y — Adaptive routing policy engine

## Must run first — all other routing beads depend on this struct

## Goal

Define ResourceTopology and build_spec_topology(). This is the central data structure.
RoutingConfig (from bd-v2s6 / bd-v1r.1) is a parameter — implement AFTER bd-v2s6 defines it.

## Wave ordering note

bd-2svi and bd-v2s6 are both Wave 1, but bd-2svi depends on RoutingConfig being defined first.
Correct order: bd-v2s6 defines RoutingPolicy + RoutingConfig → THEN bd-2svi builds the topology struct.

## ResourceTopology struct — src/protocol/rest/topology.rs (new file)

```rust
pub struct ResourceTopology {
    pub path_bindings: HashMap<String, TopologyEntry>,
    pub prefix_scopes: Vec<PrefixScope>,
    pub warnings: Vec<TopologyWarning>,
}

pub struct TopologyEntry {
    pub resource_name: String,
    pub confidence: Confidence,
    pub relationship: ResourceRelationship,
    pub winning_evidence: EvidenceSource,
    pub conflict: Option<String>,
}

pub enum Confidence { Pinned, High, Medium, Low }

pub enum ResourceRelationship {
    RootCollection, RootSingleton, ChildCollection, ChildSingleton,
}

pub enum EvidenceSource { XTwinning, FlatCrud, SchemaFirst, PrefixScoped, Waterfall }

pub struct PrefixScope {
    pub prefix: String,
    pub path_count: usize,
    pub pinned: bool,
}

pub struct TopologyWarning {
    pub path: String,
    pub flat_crud_candidate: Option<String>,
    pub schema_first_candidate: Option<String>,
    pub description: String,
}
```

## build_spec_topology() — the core function

```rust
pub fn build_spec_topology(
    catalog: &RestCatalog,         // has resources, paths, and x_twinning after bd-v2s6
    config: &RoutingConfig,        // from bd-v2s6
) -> ResourceTopology
```

### Ambiguity resolution rule (specify concretely here, not just 'prefer schema-first for nested')

When flat-crud and schema-first disagree:
1. If path has more than one parameterized segment (is_nested_path): prefer schema-first, confidence=Medium
2. If flat-crud candidate IS in catalog AND schema-first candidate IS in catalog AND they differ:
   prefer schema-first, log TopologyWarning, confidence=Medium
3. If only flat-crud candidate is in catalog: use flat-crud, confidence=High
4. If only schema-first candidate is in catalog: use schema-first, confidence=High
5. If neither is in catalog: no binding (route will reach waterfall)

### Multi-prefix detection

Do NOT use 60% threshold to pick one prefix. Instead:
- Group all paths by first segment
- Any first-segment that appears in ≥3 paths AND does not match any catalog resource name = prefix scope candidate
- Build a PrefixScope for EACH such candidate (handles /v1 + /v2 coexisting)
- Pinned if config.base_prefix is Some (user/x-twinning override)

### Parent-child relationship detection

Path pattern matching:
/{resource} or /{resource}/{id} → RootCollection / RootSingleton
/{prefix}/{resource} or /{prefix}/{resource}/{id} → RootCollection in prefix scope
/{parent}/{id}/{resource} or /{parent}/{id}/{resource}/{rid} → ChildCollection / ChildSingleton

### x-twinning / CLI overrides = Confidence::Pinned

If config.policy is Some(specific) (not Auto): only run that evidence source, set Pinned.
If config.base_prefix is Some: use it as the prefix scope, set Pinned, skip auto-detection.

## Tests

- topology_petstore_high_confidence
- topology_reqres_prefix_detected
- topology_1password_nested_child: /vaults/{id}/items → ChildCollection, Item, High
- topology_multi_prefix: /v1/* and /v2/* both detected as separate scopes
- topology_conflict_logged: flat-crud says Vault, schema-first says Item → warning + schema-first wins
- topology_ambiguity_rule_nested_prefers_schema_first
- topology_x_twinning_pinned

## Acceptance

build_spec_topology() returns ResourceTopology with correct path_bindings.
All 6 tests pass. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

title: Postgres seed JSONL contract export and import
description: Implement the simplest Postgres seed JSONL v1 path. Export a deterministic LLM-fillable seed contract from a schema-loaded normalized Postgres catalog. Import a filled seed-data JSONL file after schema/catalog load and before bootstrap finalization, run_once listener startup, or serve startup. V1 supports schema-backed startup only; seed import with restore or materialize-source-url is an explicit structured refusal. No fake data generation in twinning.
design: Add small CLI flags such as --export-seed-contract FILE and --seed FILE on the postgres command. Contract targets are schema-qualified table names. To avoid default-expression complexity in v1, seed rows must include every declared column exactly once; nullable columns may be null, non-null columns may not. Defaults and checks are exported as metadata for the LLM agent, but twinning does not evaluate defaults. Import should convert JSON values into kernel values and execute through existing kernel storage and constraint paths so NOT NULL, primary key, unique, foreign key, check, and type behavior match live mutations. Seed rows become committed state and therefore affect report row counts and snapshot relation contents.
acceptance_criteria: Golden contract export for a tiny schema is stable. Import tests cover valid rows, malformed JSONL, unknown table, unknown column, missing column, duplicate column or duplicate field shape if representable, type mismatch, NOT NULL, PK or UNIQUE, FK, and CHECK refusals. CLI config tests refuse seed with restore and seed with materialize-source-url. Bootstrap snapshot determinism test proves same schema plus same seed file yields the same snapshot hash. run_once test proves seeded rows are visible to the first client query before any client mutation.
close_reason: Implemented Postgres seed contract export and seed-data import through committed state, with schema-only refusals and tests.

title: Freeze the representative extractor fixture and input corpus for the live canary
description:
Context:
The extractor canary is risky because it spans fixture choice, input data, and live harness behavior. The representative extractor and its deterministic fixture set should be frozen first.

Deliverables:
- one pinned extractor entrypoint or script path for the canary
- deterministic fixture/input corpus for that extractor path
- clear in-repo documentation of the subset the canary is proving
acceptance_criteria:
1. The repo contains one pinned representative extractor canary target.
2. The extractor fixture/input corpus is deterministic and checked in or otherwise reproducibly pinned.
3. The canary scope is documented tightly enough that future changes do not widen it by accident.
notes:
Target files: tests/fixtures/canaries/extractor_canary/** and extractor-canary fixture notes.
Reservation guidance: own fixture/corpus selection only.
close_reason: Pinned representative extractor entrypoint and deterministic fixture corpus with validation

title: Protect the Phase-0 bootstrap contract while v0 runtime work lands
description:
Context:
The current repo already powers working Phase-0 bootstrap pipelines. Runtime work must not silently break the existing CLI, report, snapshot, or refusal behavior while chasing shorter overall runtime. This issue is the safety roll-up for that constraint.

Deliverables:
- locked regression coverage for the current bootstrap CLI/report/snapshot contract
- explicit refusal and exit-domain coverage for current failure paths
- baseline startup/restore/reset budget capture so performance work has a reference point
acceptance_criteria:
1. Existing Phase-0 bootstrap behavior is covered by checked-in regression tests before runtime surfaces widen.
2. Current exit-domain and refusal behavior is locked by tests rather than memory.
3. A reusable budget harness exists so later runtime work can prove it did not regress the operator path.
notes:
Target files: tests/bootstrap_*.rs and tests/storage/*.rs.
Reservation guidance: roll-up only; close via child beads, not direct code claims.

title: REST seed JSONL contract export and import
description: Implement the simplest REST seed JSONL v1 path. Export a deterministic LLM-fillable seed contract from the OpenAPI-derived REST catalog and import filled seed-data JSONL into seedable REST resource tables before serving or run mode. If a seed file is provided, it replaces the current OpenAPI-example seeding path for startup; no merge behavior in v1. No fake data generation in twinning.
design: Add small CLI flags such as --export-seed-contract FILE and --seed FILE on the rest command. Contract targets are executable REST resource names that map to kernel tables; unsupported routes/resources are listed in the contract with reasons but cannot be imported. To keep v1 simple, seed rows must include every materialized resource field exactly once; nullable fields may be null. Import directly populates the shared kernel backend through existing mutation/storage validation. Existing OpenAPI example seeding remains the default only when --seed is absent.
acceptance_criteria: Golden contract export for a tiny OpenAPI fixture is stable and marks unsupported shapes with reasons. Import tests cover valid resource rows, malformed JSONL, unknown resource, unknown field, missing field, type mismatch, and unsupported resource refusal. A startup test proves --seed disables OpenAPI-example seeding rather than merging. HTTP tests prove imported rows are visible through existing list and point GET routes. Report/session output remains deterministic.

title: Implement canonical committed-state snapshot encoding and hashing
description:
Context:
Snapshot meaning is a separate contract from restore mechanics and budget measurement, so canonical encoding and hashing should land first.

Deliverables:
- canonical full-content snapshot encoding over committed state
- deterministic snapshot hashing over the declared v0 hash surface
- focused tests for excluded non-semantic fields
acceptance_criteria:
1. Identical committed state yields identical snapshot hashes across repeated runs.
2. Snapshot encoding uses full committed contents rather than overlay deltas.
3. Hash-surface exclusions are locked by focused tests.
notes:
Target files: src/snapshot/encode.rs and snapshot-hash tests.
Reservation guidance: own snapshot encoding and hashing only.

title: Implement input coercion and SQLSTATE 22P02 mapping for kernel values
description:
Context:
Type coercion is a separate failure surface from storage and constraint enforcement and should be isolated so parity failures stay legible.

Deliverables:
- coercion from client inputs into the declared runtime value model
- precise SQLSTATE 22P02 mapping for bad coercions
- focused coercion parity tests for declared types
acceptance_criteria:
1. Bad coercions surface SQLSTATE 22P02 rather than ad hoc parse errors or panics.
2. Coercion logic is reusable by insert, upsert, and later update/delete paths.
3. Coercion failures are covered by focused tests separate from storage semantics.
notes:
Target files: src/kernel/coerce.rs and coercion tests.
Reservation guidance: own coercion logic only.
close_reason: Added dedicated kernel coercion with explicit 22P02 mapping and focused parity coverage

title: REST GET /posture/integrations/{id} returns array for Tailscale singleton
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official public Tailscale API v2 OpenAPI 3.1.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.tailscale.com/api/v2

Source docs:
https://tailscale.com/docs/reference/tailscale-api
https://tailscale.com/api-docs

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.tailscale.com/api/v2 -o /tmp/tailscale_api_v2.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/tailscale_api_v2.yaml --serve --port 18212
python3 /tmp/tailscale_api_v2_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18212 /tmp/tailscale_api_v2.yaml
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18212/posture/integrations/id-1' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer twinning-test'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[{"clientId":"93013672-b00c-4344-80ca-7ecf74f9dce1","clientSecret":null,"cloudId":"global","configUpdated":"2024-06-18T13:44:28.250168Z","id":"p56wQiqrn7mfDEVEL","provider":"intune","tenantId":"d1ae389b-5207-43a2-afca-2de6b03ac7e3"}]
```

Expected response/output:
The operation `GET /posture/integrations/{id}` declares a 200 JSON response schema `#/components/schemas/PostureIntegration`, which is an object. On empty state the twin may return a JSON 404, but a successful 200 response must be a single object, not a one-element array.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /posture/integrations/{id} response shape: expected object, got list; body='[{"clientId":"93013672-b00c-4344-80ca-7ecf74f9dce1","clientSecret":null,"cloudId":"global","configUpdated":"2024-06-18T13:44:28.250168Z","id":"p56wQiqrn7mfDEVEL","provider":"intune","tenantId":"d1ae389b-5207-43a2-afca-2d'
SUMMARY operations=85 assertions=189 failures=1
```

Root cause hypothesis:
The route is schema-first (`PostureIntegration`) but the path has two literal segments plus a terminal `{id}`. `src/protocol/rest/topology.rs::path_shape_binding()` only recognizes `[literal, {id}]` as `RootSingleton`; `/posture/integrations/{id}` has no detected prefix scope and no parent path parameter, so topology falls through to `schema_in_catalog` with `ResourceRelationship::RootCollection` at `src/protocol/rest/topology.rs:338-346`. In route classification, `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:714-740` only converts root object responses to singleton when `root_object_response_should_be_singleton()` accepts the pattern, but that helper currently only handles a single literal segment at `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:744-758`. The route therefore executes as `ReadMany` and encodes a 200 array.

Priority: P1 because this is silent wrong response shape/data for an advertised executable GET route.

title: REST list responses collapse OpenAPI wrapper objects into bare arrays
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Apicurio Registry OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json -o /tmp/apicurio_registry.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apicurio_registry.json --port 18132
python3 /tmp/apicurio_registry_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18132
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18132/search/artifacts?offset=0&limit=10'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
The OpenAPI operation `searchArtifacts` declares `200` as `#/components/schemas/ArtifactSearchResults`, an object with required `count` and `artifacts` fields. An empty result should preserve the wrapper shape, for example:
```json
{"count":0,"artifacts":[]}
```
The twin should not collapse wrapper-object response schemas into a bare array.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
PASS GET /search/artifacts status: status=200, expected=[200], body=[]
FAIL GET /search/artifacts response object shape: body=[]
PASS GET /search/artifacts/{artifactId}/versions status: status=200, expected=[200, 404], body=[]
FAIL GET /search/artifacts/{artifactId}/versions response object shape: body=[]
```

Root cause hypothesis:
The REST route/encoding path currently models successful list-like reads as `RouteKind::ReadMany`, which always encodes `ReadResult` rows with `encode_read_many_payload()` as a JSON array. `RouteEntry.response_fields` captures column names but does not preserve that the OpenAPI response schema is a wrapper object with an array property plus scalar metadata (`count`). The schema-first/array unwrapping work likely selects the item resource for execution and loses the response wrapper shape before encoding.
close_reason: Completed: REST list reads preserve declared wrapper object response bodies

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Additional e2e evidence from Adyen TransferService v3 OpenAPI 3.1 spec. Spec URL: https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/adyen.com/TransferService/3/openapi.json. Repro: cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/adyen_transfer.json --port 18133; curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18133/transactions'. Actual: 200 OK with [] for a route whose 200 response schema is #/components/schemas/TransactionSearchResponse, an object wrapper with data/_links. Expected: preserve object wrapper shape, e.g. an object with data array. Root cause remains the bd-2vy0 hypothesis: schema-first picks the executable item/resource path and read-many encoding collapses wrapper object responses to bare arrays.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Additional e2e evidence from Apideck Webhook OpenAPI 3.0.3. Spec URL: https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apideck.com/webhook/9.3.0/openapi.json. Repro: cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apideck_webhook.json --port 18135; curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18135/webhook/webhooks'. Actual: 200 OK with [] for a route whose 200 response is a GetWebhooksResponse wrapper object. Expected: preserve wrapper object shape with data array and metadata. Same root cause class as bd-2vy0: list-like reads lose OpenAPI wrapper response shape and encode bare arrays.

comments.author: Zac
