title: REST POST /v1/account_links rejects spec-declared form-urlencoded body
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official public Stripe OpenAPI 3.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json -o /tmp/stripe_spec3.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/stripe_spec3.json --serve --port 18174
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18174/v1/account_links \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk_test_twinning' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
  --data 'type=account_onboarding&account=acct_test'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route declares unsupported request body content type(s) [application/x-www-form-urlencoded]; REST twin v0 only supports application/json request bodies. Path: /v1/account_links"}
```

Expected response/output:
The Stripe spec declares `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` request bodies for POST routes and a 200 JSON response for `POST /v1/account_links`. The REST twin should parse the declared form fields into the same request-resource validation/materialization path used for JSON request bodies, or at least not reject every form-backed Stripe mutation as an unsupported route. A valid form request should return the declared success status/body shape instead of a 501 route refusal.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
form_body_refused: 292
  - POST /v1/account_links
  - POST /v1/account_sessions
  - POST /v1/accounts
SUMMARY operations=587 assertions=1055 failures=404
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1091-1126` forces any POST/PUT/PATCH operation whose resolved request body does not declare JSON into a `RouteKind::Refusal`, and the refusal text hard-codes that v0 only supports `application/json`. Stripe's official OpenAPI uses `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` for almost all mutations, so the classifier refuses the route before `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs` can parse or validate any declared fields.

Priority: P2 because this is a missing feature/refusal for a large real-world OpenAPI 3.0 spec.

title: Build shared differential harness utilities for twin-vs-Postgres parity tests
description:
Context:
The current graph asks the write and read corpora to each invent their own comparison machinery. That is redundant and will make parity failures harder to interpret. This issue owns the shared differential harness layer.

Deliverables:
- one shared runner for twin-vs-Postgres corpus execution
- one shared comparison/result model for rowsets, tags, and SQLSTATEs
- one shared classification path for explicit refusal vs SKIP outcomes
acceptance_criteria:
1. Write and read parity suites reuse one shared differential support layer.
2. SQLSTATE and rowset comparison logic is not duplicated across corpora.
3. Differential failures are reported in a consistent, operator-readable format.
notes:
Target files: tests/differential/support/**.
Reservation guidance: roll-up only; close via child utilities, not direct claims.
close_reason: Shared differential runner and comparator utilities are landed and differential suite passes

title: REST POST /api/v1/Activities refuses application/json media type parameters
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public FakeRESTApi OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec.

Spec URL:
https://fakerestapi.azurewebsites.net/swagger/v1/swagger.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://fakerestapi.azurewebsites.net/swagger/v1/swagger.json -o /tmp/fakerestapi_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/fakerestapi_openapi.json --serve --port 18168
python3 /tmp/fakerestapi_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18168
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18168/api/v1/Activities \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json; v=1.0' \
  -d '{"id":1,"title":"campaign activity","dueDate":"2026-01-01T00:00:00Z","completed":false}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route declares unsupported request body content type(s) [application/*+json; v=1.0, application/json; v=1.0, text/json; v=1.0]; REST twin v0 only supports application/json request bodies. Path: /api/v1/Activities"}
```

Expected response/output:
The OpenAPI request body declares JSON-compatible media types, including `application/json; v=1.0`. Media type parameters are valid on `application/json`; the route should not be refused as non-JSON. A valid POST/PUT body should reach mutation normalization and return a JSON object. An invalid JSON field type should reach request validation and return 400/422, not the same startup-level 501 refusal.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /api/v1/Activities accepts parameterized JSON media type: status=501, body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route declares unsupported request body content type(s) [application/*+json; v=1.0, application/json; v=1.0, text/json; v=1.0]; REST twin v0 only supports application/json request bodies. Path: /api/v1/Activities"}'
FAIL PUT /api/v1/Users/1 accepts parameterized JSON media type: status=501, body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route declares unsupported request body content type(s) [application/*+json; v=1.0, application/json; v=1.0, text/json; v=1.0]; REST twin v0 only supports application/json request bodies. Path: /api/v1/Users/{id}"}'
SUMMARY assertions=37 failures=23
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1802-1807` checks request body content keys with exact case-insensitive equality against `application/json`, so `application/json; v=1.0` is rejected before route construction. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1788-1799` has the same exact-match assumption when selecting the request body schema. The fix should parse or normalize media types by their base type, accepting `application/json` with parameters and probably `application/*+json` as JSON-compatible while still refusing truly non-JSON bodies such as multipart.

title: REST POST /v1/quote/contractCalls runtime-refuses required array body field
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public LI.FI OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://docs.li.fi/openapi.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://docs.li.fi/openapi.yaml -o /tmp/lifi_openapi.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/lifi_openapi.yaml --serve --port 18189
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18189/v1/quote/contractCalls' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"fromChain":1.25,"fromToken":"tw-test","fromAddress":"tw-test","toChain":1.25,"toToken":"tw-test","toAmount":"tw-test","contractCalls":[{"fromAmount":"tw-test","fromTokenAddress":"tw-test","toContractAddress":"tw-test","toContractCallData":"tw-test","toContractGasLimit":"tw-test"}]}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"request body field `contractCalls` has array type, which REST twin v0 cannot materialize from JSON bodies"}
```

Expected response/output:
The startup route table advertises `POST /v1/quote/contractCalls` as executable (`schema-first`, resource `contractcallsrequests`, status `ok`). If REST v0 does not support required array fields in JSON request bodies, this route should be marked as a route refusal at startup or classified as unsupported before appearing executable. If it remains executable, the required array field should be materialized or validated without a runtime 501.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /v1/quote/contractCalls baseline not server error: status=501, body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"request body field `contractCalls` has array type, which REST twin v0 cannot materialize from JSON bodies"}'
FAIL POST /v1/advanced/possibilities baseline not server error: status=501, body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"request body field `chains` has array type, which REST twin v0 cannot materialize from JSON bodies"}'
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1095-1130` only refuses unsupported request body media types during route classification. It does not inspect the resolved request schema for required array/json fields. Later, `src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs::mutation_fields_from_object()` rejects array-typed fields at runtime because JSON body arrays are not materializable in v0. Required array fields should either be supported or produce a startup route refusal so the route table does not claim execution support.

Priority: P2 because this is a missing-feature/runtime refusal for routes advertised executable, not a crash.

title: COMMIT succeeds after failed transaction and publishes prior overlay writes
description:
After an error inside an explicit transaction, twinning marks the session as failed (`ReadyForQuery E`) but still accepts COMMIT and publishes earlier overlay writes into the final committed-state report.

Contract: README/PLAN require Postgres-faithful session semantics, explicit committed-state artifact boundaries, and reports/snapshots over committed state only. In Postgres, a transaction that has entered failed state cannot commit its prior writes; COMMIT after an aborted transaction rolls back.

Repro summary: run twinning postgres with tests/fixtures/differential/write_corpus/schema.sql, connect via pgwire, then: BEGIN; parse/execute valid tenant INSERT; execute invalid tenant INSERT with tenant_name NULL and observe SQLSTATE 23502 plus ReadyForQuery E; then send COMMIT.

Expected: COMMIT should not publish the transaction. The final run_once report/snapshot should show public.tenants rows = 0, or COMMIT should surface the correct failed-transaction behavior.

Actual: COMMIT returns CommandComplete COMMIT / ReadyForQuery I, run_once outcome is READY, and final report shows public.tenants rows = 1.

Evidence from scratch probe: /private/tmp/twinning_tx_abort_probe.py and /private/tmp/twinning_tx_abort_probe.json.
acceptance_criteria:
- Once a transaction receives an error, subsequent statements other than ROLLBACK or the explicit allowed recovery path return failed-transaction behavior and do not mutate state.
- COMMIT after failed transaction cannot publish prior overlay writes; final report and snapshot show no committed rows from the aborted transaction.
- ReadyForQuery transaction status remains Postgres-faithful across BEGIN, failure, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK paths.
- Add live pgwire tests for failed transaction followed by COMMIT, failed transaction followed by ROLLBACK, and statement attempted while failed.
- Verify deterministic snapshot hash is unchanged by aborted writes.
close_reason: Completed: failed transactions now abort their overlay, reject further statements with 25P02 until transaction end, COMMIT after failure rolls back, and run_once artifacts remain committed-state only with stable snapshot hashes.

title: Implement backend trait and per-session overlay visibility model
description:
Context:
Tournament-mode storage needs separate backend-contract and overlay-visibility slices so multiple agents can work without colliding. This issue is the backend/visibility roll-up.

Deliverables:
- backend trait and base snapshot access contract
- overlay visibility with commit/rollback semantics
- focused backend/visibility tests
acceptance_criteria:
1. Backend-contract and overlay-visibility child slices are both implemented through explicit beads.
2. Visibility semantics are proven independently of snapshot encoding and full mutation semantics.
3. This issue remains a roll-up only.
notes:
Target files: src/backend/base.rs and src/backend/overlay.rs.
Reservation guidance: roll-up only; close via child beads.

title: REST route classifier uses generic outside-CRUD refusal for parent-scoped collections
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public 1Password Connect OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/1password.local/connect/1.5.7/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/1password.local/connect/1.5.7/openapi.json -o /tmp/onepassword_connect.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/onepassword_connect.json --port 18088
python3 /tmp/onepassword_connect_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18088
```

Repro requests:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18088/vaults/aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa/items'
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18088/vaults/aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa/items' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"vault":{"id":"aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa"},"category":"LOGIN","title":"Example"}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /vaults/{vaultUuid}/items"}
```

Expected response/output:
`/vaults/{vaultUuid}/items` is a parent-scoped subresource collection. In v0 it can remain unsupported, but it should be classified with the same precise nested-resource refusal used for deeper item/file paths, for example:
```json
{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"Nested resource paths not supported in REST twin v0. Path: /vaults/{vaultUuid}/items"}
```

This is lower priority than execution bugs because the HTTP status remains a structured 501 refusal. The bug is that startup/runtime diagnostics blur a nested collection with generic non-CRUD paths, making e2e triage noisier.

Client output excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /vaults/aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa/items status: status=501, expected=(200, 404)
PASS GET /vaults/.../items error body: body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /vaults/{vaultUuid}/items"}', expected structured error with code
FAIL POST /vaults/aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa/items status: status=501, expected=(200,)
PASS POST /vaults/.../items error body: body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /vaults/{vaultUuid}/items"}', expected structured error with code
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:287-310` only emits the nested-resource refusal when `is_nested_path(pattern)` returns true. `is_nested_path()` currently uses `pattern.param_count() > 1 || pattern.segments.len().saturating_sub(1) > 2`, so a three-segment parent collection like `[literal vaults, param vaultUuid, literal items]` has one param and exactly two non-leading segments, causing the nested check to return false. The classifier then reaches the CRUD shape match, fails to match `[literal, param, literal]`, and falls back to the generic `route_refusal(path)`. A targeted fix should classify parent-scoped collections as nested/subresource refusals without changing the executable CRUD subset.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Additional e2e evidence from Nexmo External Accounts OpenAPI 3.0.1. Spec URL: https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/nexmo.com/external-accounts/0.1.5/openapi.json. title: REST startup rejects DigitalOcean integer maximum beyond i64
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public DigitalOcean API v2 OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URLs:
- Official repository source: https://github.com/digitalocean/openapi
- Bundled spec used for repro: https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/digitalocean.com/2.0/openapi.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/digitalocean.com/2.0/openapi.yaml -o /tmp/digitalocean_public_v2.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/digitalocean_public_v2.yaml --serve --port 18197
```

Actual response/output:
```text
REFUSAL [E_OPENAPI_PARSE]
OpenAPI spec `/tmp/digitalocean_public_v2.yaml` is not valid YAML or JSON.
detail: {"error":"paths./v2/databases/{database_cluster_uuid}/config.get.responses.200.content.application/json.schema.properties.config.anyOf[0].properties.group_concat_max_len.maximum: invalid type: integer `18446744073709552000` as u128, expected any YAML value at line 8552 column 38","source":"/tmp/digitalocean_public_v2.yaml"}
```

Expected response/output:
The spec is valid OpenAPI 3.0 YAML and should load far enough to classify/refuse individual routes. A JSON Schema numeric constraint like `maximum: 18446744073709552000` should not make the entire REST twin refuse startup. If v0 cannot enforce that numeric bound, the parser should preserve it as metadata, downgrade it, or ignore it with a warning while still building routes.

Relevant spec excerpt:
```yaml
group_concat_max_len:
  description: The maximum permitted result length, in bytes, for the GROUP_CONCAT() function.
  example: 1024
  maximum: 18446744073709552000
  minimum: 4
  type: integer
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:296` first parses the raw document into `serde_yaml::Value` with `serde_yaml::from_slice`. `serde_yaml::Value` cannot represent this OpenAPI numeric bound because it is parsed as a YAML unsigned integer outside the supported integer range, so parsing fails before `OpenApiDoc` decoding or route classification. The REST spec loader needs a lossy/JSON-compatible YAML ingestion path for schema metadata numbers that exceed Rust signed integer bounds, or the schema model needs to accept these numeric constraints without forcing them through unsupported `serde_yaml::Value` integer representation.

title: REST mutation routes ignore undeclared query parameters
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Apicurio Registry OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apicurio.local/registry/1.3.2.Final/openapi.json -o /tmp/apicurio_registry.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apicurio_registry.json --port 18132
python3 /tmp/apicurio_registry_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18132
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:18132/rules?unknown=1' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"type":"VALIDITY","config":"FULL"}'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
content-type: application/json

{"config":"FULL","type":"VALIDITY"}
```

Expected response/output:
The route does not declare an `unknown` query parameter. It should be rejected consistently with read routes, for example:
```http
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
content-type: application/json

{"code":"undeclared_query_param","detail":"query parameter `unknown` is not declared for this route","param":"unknown"}
```

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /rules?unknown=1 status: status=201, expected=400
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs` validates query parameters only through `query_predicate()`, which is called from `normalize_read_many()`. Mutation paths (`normalize_insert()`, `normalize_update()`, `normalize_delete()`) never validate `request.uri` against `RouteEntry.declared_query_params`, so undeclared query parameters on mutation routes are silently ignored while the mutation executes.

title: REST route classifier refuses flat CRUD paths when schema names differ from path resource
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from a public Reqres OpenAPI 3.0.3 gist.

Spec page:
https://gist.github.com/EDDYMENS/f7aa590591e618c690601bead7ef9287

Spec URL:
https://gist.githubusercontent.com/EDDYMENS/f7aa590591e618c690601bead7ef9287/raw/reqres.openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://gist.githubusercontent.com/EDDYMENS/f7aa590591e618c690601bead7ef9287/raw/reqres.openapi.json -o /tmp/reqres_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/reqres_openapi.json --port 18087
python3 /tmp/reqres_openapi_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18087
```

The campaign client exercises every declared operation in the spec: `GET /users`, `POST /users`, `PUT /users/{id}`, and `DELETE /users/{id}`, plus declared `page` and unknown query-param edge cases.

Representative repro requests:
```bash
curl -i http://127.0.0.1:18087/users
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18087/users?page=1'
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18087/users \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name":"morpheus","job":"leader"}'
curl -i -X PUT http://127.0.0.1:18087/users/1 \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name":"morpheus","job":"leader"}'
curl -i -X DELETE http://127.0.0.1:18087/users/1
```

Actual response pattern:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /users"}
```

For item operations the same happens with `/users/{id}`:
```json
{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /users/{id}"}
```

Client output:
```text
FAIL GET /users status: status=501, expected 200
FAIL GET /users body: body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /users"}'
FAIL GET /users?page=1 status: status=501, expected 200
FAIL GET /users?page=1 body: body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /users"}'
FAIL GET /users?unknown=1 status: status=501, expected 422
FAIL GET /users?unknown=1 body: body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /users"}'
FAIL POST /users valid status: status=501, expected one of (200, 201)
FAIL POST /users valid body: body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /users"}'
FAIL POST /users invalid type status: status=501, expected 422
FAIL POST /users invalid type body: body='{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /users"}'
FAIL PUT /users/1 valid status: status=501, expected one of (200, 204)
FAIL DELETE /users/1 status: status=501, expected 204
FAILURES 12
```

Expected response/output:
These are exact flat CRUD-shaped paths, not fixed-prefix or nested paths. The twin should either derive a `users` resource from the operation request/response schemas and execute the v0 CRUD flow, or return a precise schema/resource-derivation refusal that explains the component schemas cannot be mapped to the `/users` resource. The generic outside-CRUD refusal is misleading because the path shape itself is inside the CRUD subset.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:266-285` builds catalog resources only from component schema names. In this spec the component schemas are named `userRequest` and `userResponse`, while the path resource is `users`. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:272-287` then classifies a route by checking `catalog.resources.contains_key(resource_name)`, where `resource_name` is derived from the path literal `users`; because no catalog resource has that exact key, the classifier returns `route_refusal(path)` before the CRUD path shape is considered. A robust fix likely needs operation-level request/response schema metadata to map flat CRUD paths to differently named component schemas, with a precise refusal when a resource cannot be derived.

This is distinct from `bd-331c` because there is no fixed path prefix; `/users` and `/users/{id}` are exact flat CRUD shapes. It is also distinct from `bd-s295`: here the route never reaches create/update validation at all.
close_reason: Completed: schema-first topology keeps item relationships when the path resource name differs from the schema resource

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Additional duplicate-class repro from Up API: https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/up.com.au/v1/openapi.yaml. Local campaign used /tmp/up_api.yaml on port 18086 and /tmp/up_api_client.py; flat CRUD-shaped paths /accounts, /accounts/{id}, /webhooks, /webhooks/{id}, /categories, and /transactions returned generic 501 unsupported_shape because path literals like accounts/webhooks do not match component-derived resource names such as AccountResource/WebhookResource/ListAccountsResponse. I filed bd-7deu with full curl details before seeing this bead; closing bd-7deu as duplicate so this remains the canonical flat path/schema-name mismatch issue.

title: bd-mcp.4: stdio transport, protocol versioning, canary harness, and CI feature flag
description:
Complete the MCP twin with stdio transport (for Claude Desktop and other stdio clients), protocol version tracking, a canary smoke harness, and CI feature matrix.

## stdio transport

`pub fn run_stdio(config: McpConfig) -> Result<Execution, Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`

Invoked by `twinning mcp --stdio --server '...'` or `twinning mcp --stdio --manifest ...`.

Protocol: newline-delimited JSON over stdin/stdout. Each line is a complete JSON-RPC 2.0 message.

```rust
// Pseudocode
let catalog = load_catalog(&config.source)?;
let stdin = std::io::stdin();
let stdout = std::io::stdout();
for line in stdin.lock().lines() {
    let request: JsonRpcRequest = serde_json::from_str(&line?)?;
    if let Some(response) = dispatch(request, &catalog) {
        writeln!(stdout.lock(), "{}", serde_json::to_string(&response)?)?;
    }
}
```

This is the transport Claude Desktop uses. No Axum, no Tokio, no port — pure synchronous stdio. Single-threaded, line-by-line. Add `--stdio` flag to the CLI that routes to run_stdio instead of run (HTTP).

## Protocol version handling

MCP protocol versions are date-based strings (e.g., "2024-11-05", "2025-03-26"). Clients send their preferred version in the initialize request. The twin should:
1. Declare support for a known set of versions (hardcoded list, updated as MCP evolves)
2. In the initialize response, echo back the client's requested version if it's in the supported set, otherwise respond with the nearest supported version
3. Emit a warning in the startup report if the client requests an unsupported version

```rust
pub const SUPPORTED_MCP_PROTOCOL_VERSIONS: &[&str] = &[
    "2024-11-05",
    "2025-03-26",
];

pub fn negotiate_protocol_version(client_version: &str) -> &'static str {
    SUPPORTED_MCP_PROTOCOL_VERSIONS
        .iter()
        .find(|&&v| v == client_version)
        .copied()
        .unwrap_or(SUPPORTED_MCP_PROTOCOL_VERSIONS.last().unwrap())
}
```

This is the version-tracking mechanism that the user noted is important — MCP is an evolving spec, clients at different versions connect, and the twin should handle the negotiation gracefully rather than hard-failing on version mismatch.

## Canary harness

Add `tests/fixtures/canaries/mcp_smoke/` with:
- `manifest.json` — a minimal static catalog with 2 tools (one with outputSchema, one without), 1 resource, 1 prompt
- `cases.json` — expected JSON-RPC responses for initialize, tools/list, tools/call (stubbable), tools/call (unsupported), resources/list, prompts/list

Add `tests/canaries/mcp_smoke.rs`:
```rust
#[cfg(feature = "mcp")]
#[test]
fn mcp_smoke() {
    // Start MCP HTTP twin from manifest
    // Send each case request via reqwest or ureq
    // Assert response matches expected shape (not exact JSON, structural match)
    // Verify catalog_hash is stable across two loads of same manifest
}
```

## Feature CI matrix

Update `Cargo.toml` (binary crate) to add mcp feature:
```toml
[features]
default = ["rest", "postgres"]
rest    = ["dep:twinning-rest"]
postgres = ["dep:twinning-postgres"]
mcp     = ["dep:twinning-rest", "twinning-rest/mcp"]
all     = ["rest", "postgres", "mcp"]
```

Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml` feature matrix to add mcp and all (which now includes mcp). Document new binary size baselines in AGENTS.md.

## Files touched
- `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/stdio.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/version.rs` (new — SUPPORTED_MCP_PROTOCOL_VERSIONS, negotiate_protocol_version)
- `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/mod.rs` (add stdio, version)
- `src/cli.rs` (add --stdio flag to Mcp subcommand)
- `tests/canaries/mcp_smoke.rs` (new)
- `tests/fixtures/canaries/mcp_smoke/manifest.json` (new)
- `tests/fixtures/canaries/mcp_smoke/cases.json` (new)
- `Cargo.toml` (add mcp feature)
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (update feature matrix)
- `AGENTS.md` (update binary size baselines)

title: Expand manifest-backed read corpus for IN BETWEEN and basic GROUP BY
description:
Close the remaining read-shape vocabulary gap from PLAN_TWINNING by proving IN-list, BETWEEN, and basic aggregate GROUP BY behavior through canaries and differential tests.

Current gap:
- PLAN_TWINNING controlled vocabulary names select_in_list, select_between, and aggregate_basic_group_by.
- The checked-in canary manifest currently claims select_by_pk, select_filtered_scan, select_is_null, information_schema_public_base_tables, and join refusal, but not these read shapes.

Scope:
- Add parser/IR/executor support only for narrowly declared forms that can be matched against real Postgres behavior.
- Add manifest entries only after tests prove the shape.
- Include NULL behavior, empty lists where applicable, type coercion/refusal, ordering determinism for result comparison, and SQLSTATE parity for unsupported variants.
- Add differential fixtures that compare twinning output to real Postgres for each declared shape.

Non-goals:
- No arbitrary expression engine.
- No broad HAVING/window/subquery support.
- No join support beyond explicit supported/refused behavior already declared elsewhere.
acceptance_criteria:
- canaries/manifest.v0.json claims select_in_list, select_between, and aggregate_basic_group_by only after executable canaries exist for each.
- Differential tests compare twinning against real Postgres for representative success and refusal cases.
- NULL behavior, type coercion/refusal, empty IN-list handling where syntactically relevant, and deterministic ordering are explicitly tested.
- Unsupported adjacent forms such as HAVING, windows, subqueries, arbitrary expressions, and joins remain refused with stable envelopes.
- README/plan support claims are updated only to match the proven manifest surface.
close_reason: Expanded manifest-backed read corpus for IN, BETWEEN, and basic GROUP BY with canary and differential coverage

title: Implement table storage, key indexes, and constraint-backed insert/upsert semantics
description:
Context:
Once the value/coercion lane exists, the row-store needs a separate storage/constraint/DML sub-lane for the declared write subset. This issue is that roll-up.

Deliverables:
- table storage and keyed lookup surfaces
- constraint enforcement for the declared mutation subset
- INSERT/RETURNING and ON CONFLICT behavior through child slices
acceptance_criteria:
1. Storage, constraint, insert, and upsert child slices are all implemented through explicit beads.
2. Differential write parity is proven by the child slices, not by one monolithic patch.
3. This issue remains a roll-up; read breadth stays elsewhere.
notes:
Target files: src/kernel/write.rs and src/kernel/constraints.rs.
Reservation guidance: roll-up only; close via child beads.

title: Implement value model and Postgres type coercion for row-store mutations
description:
Context:
The row-store needs one explicit value/coercion sub-lane before storage and DML semantics expand. This issue is the roll-up for the runtime value model and input coercion.

Deliverables:
- runtime value representation for declared column types
- input coercion with SQLSTATE 22P02 mapping
- focused value/coercion tests
acceptance_criteria:
1. Value-model and coercion child slices are both implemented.
2. Bad coercions surface SQLSTATE 22P02 through the dedicated coercion slice.
3. This issue remains a roll-up for value/coercion work only.
notes:
Target files: src/kernel/value.rs and src/kernel/coerce.rs.
Reservation guidance: roll-up only; close via child beads.
close_reason: Satisfied by bd-10z and bd-2uy; focused value/coercion validation passes

title: bd-sf.5: Snowflake twin canary harness, startup report, materialize path, and CI feature flag
description:
Complete the Snowflake twin with an integration canary, startup report, the `--materialize-source-url snowflake://...` path for pulling real data, and the CI feature matrix entry.

## Canary Harness

Add `tests/canaries/snowflake_smoke.rs` with a test that starts the Snowflake twin and connects with the Python connector (via subprocess) or with a Rust HTTP client simulating the Go connector flow.

### Rust HTTP client canary (primary — no Python dep)

Simulate the Go connector's request sequence:
1. POST `/session/v1/login-request` with Go-style body (no gzip, `CLIENT_APP_ID: "Go"`) → assert success, extract token
2. POST `/queries/v1/query-request` for `SELECT 1 AS n` → assert success, assert rowtype `[{name: "N", type: "fixed", precision: 38, scale: 0}]`, assert rowsetBase64 is non-empty string
3. POST `/queries/v1/query-request` for `SHOW WAREHOUSES` → assert success, assert result has `name` column with value `TWIN_WH`
4. POST `/queries/v1/query-request` for `SHOW TABLES` → assert result rows match catalog table count
5. POST `/session?delete=true` → assert success
6. POST `/queries/v1/query-request` with deleted token → assert 401

Fixture: `tests/fixtures/canaries/snowflake_smoke/schema.sql` — a minimal DDL with 2 tables (one with each type variant that needs Arrow encoding: fixed, text, date, timestamp_ntz) and 5 rows of data each.

### Validate Arrow IPC output

In the canary, after receiving `rowsetBase64`, decode base64 → attempt to open as Arrow IPC stream using arrow-rs IPC reader. Assert the schema matches the expected rowtype. This validates that the Arrow encoding is readable by a real Arrow reader without needing Python.

```rust
let ipc_bytes = base64::decode(&rowset_base64)?;
let reader = StreamReader::try_new(Cursor::new(ipc_bytes), None)?;
let batch = reader.next().unwrap()?;
assert_eq!(batch.num_columns(), expected_columns);
assert_eq!(batch.num_rows(), expected_rows);
```

## Startup Report

```json
{
  "version": "twinning.snowflake-report.v0",
  "outcome": "PASS",
  "catalog": {
    "source": "schema_file",
    "database_name": "TWINDB",
    "schema_name": "PUBLIC",
    "catalog_hash": "...",
    "table_count": 12,
    "total_rows_materialized": 847
  },
  "session": {
    "request_count": N,
    "query_count": N,
    "show_count": N,
    "ddl_count": N,
    "error_count": N
  },
  "next_step": "Connect with snowflake-connector-python, JDBC, dbt, or SnowSQL to account=fakesnow host=127.0.0.1 port=9876 protocol=http"
}
```

Human-readable startup (non-json mode):
```
Snowflake twin ready — 12 tables, 847 rows
account: fakesnow  host: 127.0.0.1:9876
Connect: snowflake.connector.connect(account='fakesnow', host='127.0.0.1', port=9876, protocol='http', user='x', password='x')
```

Print the Python connector connection snippet — this is the most common client, and the exact kwargs are non-obvious.

## Materialize from Real Snowflake

`twinning snowflake --schema schema.sql --materialize-source-url snowflake://account.region/database`

Use subprocess approach (same pattern as Postgres materialize path which uses `psql COPY`):
```bash
python3 -c "
import snowflake.connector, json, sys
conn = snowflake.connector.connect(
    account=sys.argv[1], user=sys.argv[2], password=sys.argv[3],
    database=sys.argv[4], schema=sys.argv[5]
)
cur = conn.cursor()
cur.execute('SELECT * FROM {table}')
rows = cur.fetchall()
print(json.dumps(rows))
"
```

Environment variables for credentials:
- `SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT`, `SNOWFLAKE_USER`, `SNOWFLAKE_PASSWORD`
- `SNOWFLAKE_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH` (for key pair auth)
- `SNOWFLAKE_TOKEN` (for OAuth)

When Python connector is not installed: emit a refusal with install instructions. Check with `python3 -c 'import snowflake.connector'` before attempting.

Table fetch order: fetch tables in catalog order. For large tables, use `LIMIT 100000` by default (configurable via `--max-rows-per-table`).

## Feature CI Matrix

Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml` to add `snowflake` and update `all` to include it:
```yaml
- features: "snowflake"
  cargo_flags: "--no-default-features --features snowflake"
- features: "all"
  cargo_flags: "--features all"
```

Add snowflake binary size baseline to AGENTS.md (measure locally after implementation).

## fakesnow Compatibility Note

The fakesnow library exposes a context manager `fakesnow.server()` that starts a compatible wire server. During development, you can validate the Snowflake twin's output format by running both servers and comparing responses to the same queries. A compatibility test script in `scripts/compare_with_fakesnow.py` would be valuable: start both servers, run a set of queries against each, diff the `rowtype` and decoded Arrow schema.

## Files touched
- `tests/canaries/snowflake_smoke.rs` (new)
- `tests/fixtures/canaries/snowflake_smoke/schema.sql` (new)
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/report.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/materialize.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/listener.rs` (add session_log tracking for report)
- `Cargo.toml` (add snowflake to feature matrix)
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (add snowflake feature row)
- `AGENTS.md` (add snowflake binary size baseline)
close_reason: Completed Snowflake canary harness, startup report/session metrics, schema row materialization, Snowflake source materialization via Python connector, query row projection, CI snowflake feature-matrix row, and AGENTS size baseline. Verified with cargo fmt --check, git diff --check on touched files, cargo test -p twinning-snowflake, cargo test --no-default-features --features snowflake --test compatibility_suite snowflake_smoke, cargo test --no-default-features --features snowflake, cargo clippy -p twinning-snowflake --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo clippy --no-default-features --features snowflake --all-targets -- -D warnings, and release size builds for snowflake/all.

title: Seed JSONL v0 contract and filled-data import rules
description: Define the smallest shared seed artifact rules while keeping every exported contract twin-specific. Each twin exports a deterministic JSONL contract shaped by its own catalog or API surface. That contract is intended to be handed to an external LLM agent to fill with mock records. The twinning binary must not generate fake data, call a model, sample, infer values, or apply faker logic. V1 shared scope is only artifact versioning, deterministic target and field ordering, line envelope validation, file IO, refusal vocabulary, and import plumbing that protocol-specific beads can call.
design: V1 uses two artifact intents: an exported LLM-fillable contract and a filled seed-data JSONL file. Every line is one UTF-8 JSON object with a version, kind, twin, target kind, target identifier, and protocol-specific payload. The shared layer validates version, line kind, duplicate targets when applicable, malformed JSONL, unknown kind, and deterministic rendering. Protocol beads own field/type semantics. Seed data is complete supplied state: twinning does not apply defaults, does not generate missing fields, and does not merge multiple data sources unless a protocol bead explicitly says so.
acceptance_criteria: Golden tests pin exported contract byte ordering for at least one tiny fixture. Parser tests cover malformed JSONL, unknown version, unknown kind, duplicate target handling, and empty file behavior. The shared contract docs say LLM-fillable and no in-process generation. Import APIs return structured refusals that protocol adapters can render through existing refusal paths. No prompt text, model client, random generator, faker dependency, or sampling helper is added.
close_reason: Implemented shared Seed JSONL v0 contract parser, deterministic renderers, docs, and validation tests.

title: Snowflake seed composition with materialization and schema INSERT rows
description: Backlog after the v1 Snowflake seed path. Add explicit composition modes for seed-data JSONL with materialize-source-url and schema INSERT rows. V1 keeps the simple rule: seed import refuses materialize-source-url and replaces rows parsed from schema INSERT statements. Acceptance: documented precedence or merge model, deterministic row count/report tests, query tests for mixed sources, and structured refusals for conflicts.

title: Enhance REST twin behavioral parity controls
description:
Enhance Twinning REST so provider twins can model live API behavior beyond what vanilla OpenAPI expresses.

Motivation from OpenFIGI:
- Live OpenFIGI returns per-item miss payloads like `[{"warning":"No identifier found."}]` for bad CUSIPs.
- Current Twinning behavior for unstubbed schema-valid requests falls back to generic schema synthesis, producing `{"data":null,"error":null,"warning":null}` for an array-response endpoint. That can look valid while diverging from live semantics.
- OpenAPI security says `ApiKeyAuth` is required in the fixture, but live OpenFIGI permits unauthenticated calls with lower rate limits. Twinning currently returns 401 in shape mode.

Possible enhancements:
- Add an `x-twinning` no-stub policy per route, such as `refuse`, `error`, `empty-array`, `schema`, or `stub-required`, so tests can fail loudly when behavior has not been captured.
- Support route/provider-level optional-auth modeling: auth accepted when present, omitted auth allowed, and response headers can reflect separate rate-limit tiers.
- Improve schema synthesis for top-level array response endpoints so generated fallback preserves the response envelope shape, or refuses if it cannot do so faithfully.
- Add first-class response fixtures for per-item warning/error cases and ambiguity/multiple-candidate cases.
- Emit diagnostics in reports when a request was served by schema fallback instead of an explicit `x-twinning.response-stub`.

Acceptance criteria:
- Design doc or implementation defines explicit fallback semantics for unmatched `x-twinning` REST requests.
- OpenFIGI fixture can model success, miss, and optional-auth behavior without misleading generic fallback responses.
- REST report distinguishes explicit stub matches from schema fallback responses.
- Tests cover OpenFIGI-like array request/array response routes, authenticated and unauthenticated calls, and an unstubbed request under a strict/no-stub policy.

title: Prove byte-identical freeze restore artifacts
description: docs/goal.md sets a release bar that freeze/restore round trips are byte-identical across runs. Current snapshot content hashing is canonical over committed state, but the full artifact may still include metadata such as created_at. Audit and implement the correct artifact-level contract. Acceptance: repeated freeze + restore + refreeze produces byte-identical bytes in an explicit deterministic artifact mode, or docs/schema/tests explicitly distinguish canonical content bytes from intentionally variable human metadata; release gate/test covers the chosen contract.
close_reason: Completed: pinned the snapshot equality contract as canonical committed-state bytes rather than full envelope bytes, documented the operator-metadata exception, and added a regression assertion that full artifact bytes may differ while canonical state bytes round-trip identically.

title: bd-auth.4: Wire x-twinning auth-mode to RestAuthMode enum — bypass | shape (default)
description: Wave 3 parallel (after bd-auth.2). Add RestAuthMode enum: Bypass (skip all auth checks, v0 compat) | Shape (default, enforce credential presence). Wire XTwinningExt.auth_mode to RestAuthMode. Add --auth-mode CLI flag. Priority: --auth-mode CLI > x-twinning ext > default Shape. APIs with no security schemes unaffected (all routes public, auth check always passes). Acceptance: --auth-mode bypass restores v0 no-auth behavior; default enforces shape.
close_reason: Completed: RestAuthMode wired through CLI, x-twinning auth mode, startup resolution, and listener bypass/shape enforcement

title: REST GET /api/users paginated wrapper returns singleton 404
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official Reqres OpenAPI 3.0.3 spec.

Spec URL:
https://reqres.in/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://reqres.in/openapi.json -o /tmp/reqres_openapi.json
target/debug/twinning rest --spec /tmp/reqres_openapi.json --serve --port 18152
python3 /tmp/reqres_openapi_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18152 /tmp/reqres_openapi.json
```

Representative curl repro:
```bash
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18152/api/users?page=1&per_page=2"
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
content-type: application/json

{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}
```

Expected response/output:
`GET /api/users` declares a 200 response using `#/components/schemas/LegacyUserListResponse`, an object wrapper with pagination metadata (`page`, `per_page`, `total`, `total_pages`) and a `data` array. With an empty twin it should return a successful wrapper such as `200 {"page":..., "per_page":..., "total":0, "total_pages":..., "data":[]}` or at minimum `200 {"data":[]}`. It should not take the singleton-read path and return a resource-level 404. The same root cause reproduces on `GET /api/unknown?page=1&per_page=2`.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /api/users status: status=404, allowed=[200, 501], body='{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}'
FAIL GET /api/unknown status: status=404, allowed=[200, 501], body='{"code":"not_found","detail":"resource not found"}'
SUMMARY assertions=115 failures=9
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1150-1168` classifies component response schemas as `ArrayWrapper` only when `resource_is_array_wrapper()` returns true. `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1484-1503` treats `page` and `per_page` as wrapper metadata but does not recognize `total_pages`; it also rejects non-array fields like Reqres `support`. That leaves `RouteEntry.response_wrapper` as `None`. Then `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:193-199` treats a `ReadMany` route whose response resource equals the effective resource as a declared singleton, so `encode_read_one_payload()` returns 404 for empty rows instead of encoding the wrapper list.

title: REST GET /users returns bare array for Box entries wrapper
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official Box Platform API OpenAPI 3.0.2 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/box/box-openapi/main/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/box/box-openapi/main/openapi.json -o /tmp/box_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/box_openapi.json --serve --port 18195
python3 /tmp/box_openapi_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18195 /tmp/box_openapi.json
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18195/users?limit=1'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
`GET /users` declares `200` as `#/components/schemas/Users`, an object wrapper built with `allOf` that includes pagination fields and an `entries` array. Empty state should preserve the declared wrapper shape, for example:
```json
{"limit":1,"entries":[]}
```
It should not return a top-level JSON array. The same failure class appeared on many Box collection routes (`/collections`, `/groups`, `/webhooks`, `/workflows`, etc.).

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL GET /users response matches schema: $ expected object got list
FAIL GET /webhooks response matches schema: $ expected object got list
FAIL GET /workflows response matches schema: $ expected object got list
FAILURE_CLASSES
response_shape: 45
SUMMARY operations=294 assertions=883 failures=88
```

Root cause hypothesis:
This is a remaining gap after `bd-2vy0`. Box uses the common wrapper payload field name `entries`. In `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1965-1969`, `is_standard_response_wrapper_payload_field()` only recognizes `data`, `items`, and `results`. Because `entries` is not recognized and does not match path/resource hints like `users`, `component_response_wrapper()` leaves `RouteEntry.response_wrapper` unset. Then `src/protocol/rest/encode.rs:213-246` encodes `ReadMany` results as a bare array instead of preserving the object wrapper.

comments.author: Zac
comments.text: Implementation note: the live Box repro required two wrapper-detection adjustments. In addition to recognizing `entries` as a standard payload array field, Box pagination wrappers include an `order` array metadata field. The fix treats `order` as response-wrapper metadata so it does not make the wrapper look like it has multiple payload arrays.

title: REST additionalProperties object-map schemas materialize as zero-field arrays
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public APIs.guru OpenAPI Directory spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apis.guru/2.2.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/apis.guru/2.2.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/apis_guru_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/apis_guru_openapi.json --port 18134
python3 /tmp/apis_guru_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18134
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18134/list.json'
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18134/specs/github.com/github.json'
```

Actual responses:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[{}]
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

[]
```

Expected response/output:
The `APIs` schema is an object map: `additionalProperties` is `#/components/schemas/API`. `/list.json` and `/specs/{provider}/{api}.json` should preserve the object-map response shape, for example a JSON object keyed by API id. They should not materialize as zero-field resources or encode as arrays of empty objects.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
PASS GET /list.json status: status=200, body='[{}]'
FAIL GET /list.json object map: body=[{}]
PASS GET /specs/{provider}/{api}.json status: status=200, body='[]'
FAIL GET /specs/{provider}/{api}.json object map: body=[]
```

Startup warning:
```text
Schema has type object but no properties; resource has zero fields.
Resource apiss has no declared PK - all examples will be inserted without deduplication.
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs::SchemaObject.additional_properties` is parsed as raw JSON but `build_resource_schema()` only materializes explicit `properties` into columns. Schema-valued `additionalProperties` maps are treated like empty object resources, which produces a zero-column `apiss` table. The route layer then treats `APIs` as an executable collection and `encode_read_many_payload()` emits `[{}]` or `[]` instead of preserving the OpenAPI object-map shape.
close_reason: Completed: object-map response schemas now receive precise REST unsupported-shape refusals instead of materializing as zero-field arrays

title: REST GET /Associations/{ObjectId} returns object for array response
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Xero Files OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/XeroAPI/Xero-OpenAPI/master/xero_files.yaml -o /tmp/xero_files.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/xero_files.yaml --serve --port 18160
python3 /tmp/xero_files_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18160
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18160/Files/4ff1e5cc-9835-40d5-bb18-09fdb118db9c/Associations \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"ObjectId\":\"1270bf7c-5d18-473a-9231-1e36c4bd33ed\",\"ObjectGroup\":\"Contact\",\"ObjectType\":\"Business\"}"

curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18160/Associations/1270bf7c-5d18-473a-9231-1e36c4bd33ed?pagesize=1&page=1&sort=Name&direction=ASC"
```

Actual response from the GET:
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: application/json

{"AssociationDateUtc":null,"CreatedDateUtc":null,"FileId":null,"Name":null,"ObjectGroup":"Contact","ObjectId":"1270bf7c-5d18-473a-9231-1e36c4bd33ed","ObjectType":"Business","SendWithObject":null,"Size":null}
```

Expected response/output:
`GET /Associations/{ObjectId}` declares a 200 response schema of `type: array` with `items: Association`. Even with a path parameter, this operation is a filtered collection/search by object id. The response should be a JSON array (for example `[]` in empty state or `[ {...} ]` after the association create), not a singleton object.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
PASS GET /Associations/{ObjectId}
FAIL GET /Associations/{ObjectId} array response: body={'AssociationDateUtc': None, 'CreatedDateUtc': None, 'FileId': None, 'Name': None, 'ObjectGroup': 'Contact', 'ObjectId': '1270bf7c-5d18-473a-9231-1e36c4bd33ed', 'ObjectType': 'Business', 'SendWithObject': None, 'Size': None}, expected list
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs` / `src/protocol/rest/topology.rs` appear to classify a path with a terminal parameter as `RouteKind::ReadOne` before honoring the declared success response body shape. The route has response shape `array`, but the path topology wins and the encoder uses singleton read-one serialization. Response-array operations with path filters should remain `ReadMany`.

title: Document REST response stubs across operator, CLI, README, and OpenFIGI docs
description:
Update the public and operator-facing documentation for REST response stubs so implementers cannot land the feature as an invisible x-twinning parser change. The docs must explain that response stubs are deterministic OpenAPI spec fixtures consumed through the existing twinning rest --spec workflow, not arbitrary runtime dummy data, not a REST seed flag, and not a generated semantic simulator.

This bead exists because response stubs change how callers model use cases: the caller can maintain several OpenAPI specs or spec overlays for the same API and run separate twins for happy path, no-match, malformed/error, ambiguity, or other protocol scenarios.
design:
Documentation and operator-surface design:

- README.md: update the REST twin section and runtime contract to mention response stubs, x-twinning fixture responses, and the scenario-spec pattern. Include a compact example or link to the OpenFIGI packet.
- operator.json: update invocation/capability/current_runtime_behavior metadata so twinning --describe tells operators that REST response stubs are supported through OpenAPI x-twinning extensions. If artifacts/report metadata gains stub markers, document that too.
- src/cli.rs and generated/help surface: keep clap descriptions truthful. If no new CLI flag exists, make sure help/docs clearly imply the existing --spec path consumes x-twinning response stubs. If a new flag is introduced, document it in clap, README.md, and operator.json together.
- docs/OPENFIGI_V2_V3_REST_TWIN_PACKET.md: add the OpenFIGI-specific explanation for fixing the generic {"data":null,"warning":null} fallback, with a local command example and expected top-level array response shape.
- docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md: if it discusses REST generated responses, dummy data, synthetic examples, or x-twinning, update it to distinguish deterministic response stubs from future/generated response synthesis.
- tests/fixtures/rest/README.md or a nearby fixture README: document where response-stub fixture specs live and how scenario-specific specs should be named.
- Keep all wording aligned: response stubs are deterministic contract fixtures, not live provider simulation, random fake data, or broad mock-server semantics.
acceptance_criteria:
- README.md REST twin section lists response stubs as implemented once the feature lands.
- README.md runtime/options section explains that response stubs are declared in OpenAPI x-twinning and consumed through --spec; no separate REST --seed or dummy-data flag is implied unless actually implemented.
- README.md includes or links to an example showing several twins/spec variants for the same API use cases: happy path, no-match, error/malformed, ambiguity.
- operator.json is updated consistently with the new capability, and twinning --describe emits the updated operator metadata.
- src/cli.rs help text is accurate for the final CLI. If no CLI changes are needed, acceptance notes explicitly say why: response stubs are spec-driven and require no new flag.
- docs/OPENFIGI_V2_V3_REST_TWIN_PACKET.md includes the response-stub workaround for OpenFIGI POST /v3/mapping and the expected body shape that replaces {"data":null,"warning":null}.
- REST strategy/future docs that mention generated responses, synthetic data, or x-twinning are audited with rg and updated if they would otherwise confuse response stubs with data generation.
- Fixture documentation identifies any response-stub fixture specs and describes naming for scenario variants.
- No documentation claims live OpenFIGI calls, real OpenFIGI API keys, or external network access are required.
- Documentation references exact test commands or quality gates used by implementation, at minimum cargo test --workspace --no-default-features --features rest.
- Run rg over README.md, operator.json, docs, tests/fixtures, and src/cli.rs for response-stubs, x-twinning, OpenFIGI, REST seed, and dummy data terms before closing.
notes:
Candidate files from repo scan: README.md, operator.json, src/cli.rs, docs/OPENFIGI_V2_V3_REST_TWIN_PACKET.md, docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md, tests/fixtures/README.md, and tests/fixtures/rest or nearby fixture docs if present. Also check canaries/README.md only if response-stub canaries are added.

Preferred wording: deterministic response stubs or contract fixtures. Avoid describing this as dummy data generation unless explicitly saying it is not that.
close_reason: Updated README.md, operator.json, src/cli.rs, docs/OPENFIGI_V2_V3_REST_TWIN_PACKET.md, docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md, and tests/fixtures/README.md for response stubs, scenario-specific specs, OpenFIGI local proof, and no REST seed/dummy-data semantics. Verified twinning --version and --describe report 0.5.1/capability.

title: Add opt-in query trace artifact for live Postgres sessions
description:
Add an opt-in query capture surface for twinning live sessions so scenario-factory and lineage workflows can preserve what agents actually asked the twin, without making query text part of the deterministic snapshot meaning.

Context:
- Current run reports only record child command process metadata.
- Twin reports and snapshots do not include per-query traces.
- The lineage scenario-factory use case needs a durable record of query intent, SQLSTATE/refusal outcomes, and normalized operation identity.

Design direction:
- Add a separate artifact, likely twinning.query-trace.v0, written only when explicitly requested by CLI/config.
- Keep query trace out of twinning.snapshot.v0 hash surfaces and out of required bootstrap reports.
- Capture bounded, structured events: monotonic event id, session id, simple versus extended protocol surface, statement kind, SQL text policy, normalized Operation IR hash when available, bind parameter redaction/hash policy, transaction status before/after, command tag, affected/returned row counts, SQLSTATE or refusal code, and stable timing buckets or optional non-deterministic timing outside hashable fields.
- Default should be no query text persistence. Provide an explicit unsafe/full-text mode only if the operator asks for it.

Non-goals:
- Do not capture result row payloads by default.
- Do not capture child stdout/stderr blobs as query evidence.
- Do not turn query traces into scores or policy decisions.
- Do not require query trace support for bootstrap-only mode.
acceptance_criteria:
- CLI/config exposes an explicit opt-in path for writing a query trace artifact; default behavior writes no query trace.
- A JSON schema exists for twinning.query-trace.v0 and tests validate emitted artifacts against it.
- Live simple and extended query paths record success, SQLSTATE errors, and refusal envelopes with stable event ordering.
- Trace events include enough normalized identity to support lineage: statement kind, Operation IR hash when available, bind redaction/hash metadata, transaction before/after state, and result summary.
- Enabling query trace does not change twinning.snapshot.v0 hash for the same schema and committed state.
- Redaction/default policy is tested; full SQL or raw parameters are persisted only behind an explicit unsafe/full-text option if implemented.

title: REST seed warnings report expected array received array for unsupported array fields
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Impala Hotel Booking OpenAPI 3.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/impala.travel/hotels/1.003/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/impala.travel/hotels/1.003/openapi.json -o /tmp/impala_hotels.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/impala_hotels.json --port 18092
```

Actual startup warnings include multiple contradictory array messages:
```text
Example `components.examples.BookingCreatedExample.value` has invalid value for `bookedRooms`: expected array, received array.
Example `components.examples.BookingCreatedExampleWithPaymentBearerToken.value` has invalid value for `bookedRooms`: expected array, received array.
Example `components.examples.BookingRequestWithRatePlansExample.value` has invalid value for `rooms`: expected array, received array.
Example `components.examples.CancelledBooking.value` has invalid value for `bookedRooms`: expected array, received array.
Example `components.examples.ExampleHotel.value` has invalid value for `amenities`: expected array, received array.
```

Expected output:
If REST twin v0 cannot seed array fields, the warning should say that directly, for example `array fields are not seedable in REST twin v0`, or preserve the earlier startup wording `array is cataloged but requests using it are unsupported in REST twin v0`. It should not report identical expected and received types, because that reads like a validator bug and gives no operator action.

HTTP/client context:
The runtime client `/tmp/impala_hotels_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18092` passed all endpoint assertions using current v0 behavior. This bug is startup-warning only; no HTTP request is needed to reproduce.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/seed.rs:500-523` intentionally rejects `normalized_type == "array"` by returning `coercion_error(column, value)`. `coercion_error()` then uses `column.normalized_type` for `expected` and `json_type_name(value)` for `received`, so an array JSON value in an array column produces `expected array, received array`. The array branch should return a dedicated seed warning/error explaining that arrays are unsupported for seed materialization, instead of using the generic type-mismatch formatter.
close_reason: Completed: array seed examples now emit explicit unsupported-array seed warning.

title: PCI-DSS and GDPR compliance test harness — regulated data handling with synthetic sensitive data and automatic audit trail
description: Extend OpenAPI specs with PII classification annotations (x-pii-classification: PCI-PAN, GDPR-PERSONAL, etc). Twin emits structurally faithful but fictitious regulated data: Luhn-valid 16-digit card numbers that pass format validation but are not real cards, realistic-pattern SSNs, plausible medical record structures. Payment processing pipelines are tested against data with the full structural complexity of real cardholder data without touching a single real credential. Twin records every transmission of annotated fields: which client, which endpoint, which field, timestamp. Output is a data flow map — automatically generated evidence of where sensitive data traveled during the test run, exactly what PCI-DSS audit requires. Enforcement angle: configure twin to refuse returning fields annotated as PII when the endpoint spec does not declare them in the response schema — client tests break if they rely on data they should not have received, making compliance violations into CI failures. GDPR extension: annotate personal data fields with GDPR classification; issue a synthetic erasure request; replay full corpus — any subsequent response containing data associated with the deleted entity ID is a test failure, proving right-to-erasure compliance. CMD+RVL angle: aibuildout enrichment pipeline processes API responses that may include subscriber information and payment data; compliance twin is the development environment with automatic audit trail and no real data in dev. Commercial angle: standalone product targeting any company doing PCI-DSS or GDPR compliance testing; current alternatives are real data in staging (high compliance risk), hand-crafted fixtures (stale, low coverage), or skipping tests entirely. Prerequisite: REST twin stable, spec annotation extension design. Documented in docs/PLAN_TWINNING_REST_STRATEGY.md section 11.
