title: REST route registry ignores OpenAPI component $ref query parameters
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the official GitHub REST API OpenAPI 3.x description, reduced to a repository route subset.

Spec URL:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.yaml -o /tmp/github_api.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/github_repos_subset.yaml --serve --port 18141
python3 /tmp/github_repos_subset_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18141
```

Representative repro:
```bash
curl -i 'http://127.0.0.1:18141/repos/octocat/Hello-World/issues?per_page=1&page=1'
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
content-type: application/json

{"code":"undeclared_query_param","detail":"query parameter `per_page` is not declared for this route","param":"per_page"}
```

Expected response/output:
The GitHub operation declares pagination parameters through component parameter refs (`#/components/parameters/per-page` and `#/components/parameters/page`). The twin should treat those as declared query params. It may ignore non-column query params for v0 reads, but it should not reject declared pagination params as undeclared.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL declared pagination params: GET /repos/octocat/Hello-World/issues?per_page=1&page=1: status=400, expected=200, body={'code': 'undeclared_query_param', 'detail': 'query parameter `per_page` is not declared for this route', 'param': 'per_page'}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs::declared_query_params()` only reads `ParameterObject.location` and `ParameterObject.name` directly from path/operation parameter arrays (`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:1022-1031`). For `$ref` parameters those fields are empty. The parser model also does not retain `components.parameters` in `ComponentsObject` (`src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:104-113`), so the route registry cannot resolve `#/components/parameters/...` refs. Add parsed component parameters and resolve local parameter refs before constructing `RouteEntry.declared_query_params`.

title: bd-auth.3: Auth middleware in listener.rs — return 401/403 before route dispatch when credential missing
description: Wave 3 (after bd-auth.2). In per-request handler in listener.rs, after route match but before dispatch: call check_auth() with matched route's required_auth_schemes. Missing → 401 with WWW-Authenticate header (bearer/basic schemes) + JSON body {code: unauthorized, detail: Authorization required: <scheme> (<location>)}. Malformed → 401 with detail. Present → continue to existing dispatch. 404 still wins over 401 for unmatched paths. Acceptance: 1Password twin returns 401 without auth header, 200 with Authorization: Bearer anything.
close_reason: Completed: listener auth middleware enforces declared required_auth_schemes with 401 JSON and WWW-Authenticate while preserving 404 precedence

title: Autocommit extended-query writes require an explicit writer transaction
description:
Extended-query INSERT outside an explicit BEGIN currently fails instead of behaving like an auto-commit single-statement write transaction.

Contract: docs/PLAN_TWINNING.md says auto-commit writes are treated as single-statement write transactions, and README claims run_once supports the declared Postgres subset.

Repro summary: run twinning postgres with tests/fixtures/differential/write_corpus/schema.sql, connect via pgwire, startup, then Parse/Bind/Execute/Sync `INSERT INTO public.tenants (tenant_id, tenant_name) VALUES ($1, $2)` without sending BEGIN first.

Expected: INSERT succeeds, is committed, and final report/snapshot show public.tenants rows = 1.

Actual: extended execute returns protocol error with SQLSTATE 42P01 / code unknown_table. Detail wraps the real cause: `session overlay error: session ... does not own the mutable overlay`. This indicates the live backend requires a writer lease for writes but does not auto-open/commit one for auto-commit statements.

Evidence from scratch probe: /private/tmp/twinning_autocommit_probe.txt.
acceptance_criteria:
- Extended-query INSERT/UPSERT outside explicit BEGIN succeeds as a single-statement autocommit write when the SQL shape is manifest-supported.
- Autocommit write failures leave no committed overlay changes and produce the same SQLSTATE/refusal surface as explicit transactions for the same error.
- Final run_once report and snapshot include only committed rows from successful autocommit statements.
- Add a live pgwire regression test that exercises Parse/Bind/Execute/Sync without BEGIN and verifies final artifact row counts.
- Unsupported mutation shapes still return client-visible errors rather than process exits.
close_reason: Completed: idle extended INSERT/UPSERT portals now run as single-statement autocommit writes, successful writes commit before Sync, failures roll back without committed rows, and run_once artifacts cover only successful committed rows.

title: REST route classifier refuses flat JSON:API resources when path names differ from schema names
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Up API OpenAPI 3.0.3 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/up.com.au/v1/openapi.yaml

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/up.com.au/v1/openapi.yaml -o /tmp/up_api.yaml
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/up_api.yaml --port 18086
python3 /tmp/up_api_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18086
```

Representative flat resource requests:
```bash
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18086/accounts?page%5Bsize%5D=1"
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18086/accounts/example-id"
curl -i "http://127.0.0.1:18086/webhooks"
curl -i -X POST "http://127.0.0.1:18086/webhooks" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"data":{"type":"webhooks","attributes":{"url":"https://example.test/hook","description":"test"}}}'
```

Actual response pattern:
```http
HTTP/1.1 501 Not Implemented
content-type: application/json

{"code":"unsupported_shape","detail":"REST route is outside the CRUD route subset in v0. Path: /accounts"}
```

The same generic route refusal appears for flat collection/item paths such as `/accounts`, `/accounts/{id}`, `/categories`, `/categories/{id}`, `/transactions`, `/transactions/{id}`, `/webhooks`, and `/webhooks/{id}`. The generated campaign client exercised all 18 Up API operations plus pagination edge cases and produced 24 failures. Nested relationship paths are valid v0 refusals; the distinct bug here is the flat resource paths being refused before operation schema metadata can map them to a resource.

Expected response/output:
Flat collection and item endpoints should not be classified purely by whether the literal path segment exactly matches a component-derived resource key. For JSON:API-style specs, path names such as `accounts` and `webhooks` are backed by schemas named `AccountResource`, `ListAccountsResponse`, `WebhookResource`, `CreateWebhookRequest`, etc. The REST twin should either map these flat paths through operation response/request schema metadata or return a precise path/schema-resource mismatch refusal. The current generic outside-CRUD refusal makes these look like non-CRUD routes even though their shape is CRUD-like.

Startup notes:
The twin launched successfully. It emitted expected auth-bypass warnings for `bearer_auth`, array warnings for JSON:API list wrapper schemas, and route warnings for both flat and nested paths. The flat-path warning is the one captured by this bug.

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:272-303` classifies a route only after `resource_name(pattern)` takes the first path segment and `catalog.resources.contains_key(resource_name)` succeeds. For Up API, that first segment is `accounts`, `webhooks`, etc. `src/protocol/rest/spec.rs:266-285` builds `RestCatalog.resources` from component schema names, yielding names derived from schemas like `AccountResource`, `ListAccountsResponse`, `CreateWebhookRequest`, and `WebhookResource`, not from the plural path segment. `route_entry()` does collect `response_schema_ref` at `src/protocol/rest/routes.rs:258`, but classification has already happened using the path-derived name. A fix likely needs route target derivation from operation request/response schema metadata, or a dedicated precise refusal when a flat CRUD-shaped path cannot be mapped to a catalog resource.
close_reason: Duplicate of bd-3q08; Up API repro retained as a comment on bd-3q08.

title: REST e2e campaign: Stripe API — form-encoded request bodies, expandable objects
description: Autonomous REST e2e campaign against the Stripe REST API OpenAPI spec. Spec URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stripe/openapi/master/openapi/spec3.json. Setup: curl -L --fail <URL> -o /tmp/stripe_openapi.json && cargo build && target/debug/twinning rest --spec /tmp/stripe_openapi.json --serve --port 18401. Write a Python client at /tmp/stripe_client.py that exercises 50+ operations across: customers, charges, payment_intents, products, prices, subscriptions, invoices. Test: bearer auth enforcement (Authorization: Bearer sk_test_anything), response shape for expandable nested objects, collection listing, singleton retrieval. PRIMARY FOCUS: Stripe uses application/x-www-form-urlencoded for all write operations (POST/DELETE body), not JSON. This is almost certainly an untested path in twinnings normalizer -- test it thoroughly. Send form-encoded bodies and verify twinning handles them correctly (accepts or refuses with a precise unsupported_shape rather than a panic/500). Also test: nested parameter encoding (metadata[key]=value), amount integer fields, enum fields. File bugs as beads with full repro steps including exact curl commands showing the form-encoded body. Report: operations tested, assertions made, failures.
close_reason: Completed Stripe REST e2e campaign: exercised 55 authorized operations plus 12 missing-auth probes across customers, charges, payment_intents, products, prices, subscriptions, invoices, and related resources. Form-encoded writes returned structured unsupported_shape refusals with no 500s or panics. Follow-up bugs filed during campaign: bd-2mlj and bd-f1aj. Campaign artifacts: /tmp/stripe_openapi.json, /tmp/stripe_client.py, /tmp/stripe_client.out.

title: GitHub REST campaign exact spec is refused by 10MB raw OpenAPI limit
description:
Discovered during bd-1bsj GitHub REST API campaign.

Spec URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.json
Local spec: /tmp/github_openapi.json
Downloaded size: 12,515,925 bytes.

Repro:
1. curl -L --fail https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/rest-api-description/main/descriptions/api.github.com/api.github.com.json -o /tmp/github_openapi.json
2. cargo build
3. target/debug/twinning rest --spec /tmp/github_openapi.json --serve --port 18400 --json

Actual:
Process exits 2 with refusal code E_OPENAPI_SPEC_TOO_LARGE: OpenAPI spec `/tmp/github_openapi.json` exceeds the 10MB safety limit. Detail reports actual_bytes=12515925, max_bytes=10485760.

Expected:
The checked campaign setup should start far enough to classify/routes-test the GitHub REST spec, or the loader should make the size policy semantic/tolerant enough for large real OpenAPI specs.

Additional observation:
`jq -c . /tmp/github_openapi.json > /tmp/github_openapi.min.json` produces a semantically equivalent 6,704,885 byte file that gets past the raw byte limit, so the current limit is sensitive to pretty-printing rather than catalog complexity.

Root cause hypothesis:
src/protocol/rest/spec.rs applies MAX_SPEC_BYTES to raw file bytes before parsing. For large public OpenAPI documents, whitespace alone can flip a supported semantic spec into a process-level refusal.
close_reason: Completed: raw OpenAPI spec size limit now allows GitHub-scale source bytes while enforcing a compact semantic 10MB limit; GitHub raw spec reaches route classification.

title: bd-sf.1: Snowflake wire protocol skeleton — Axum HTTP server with session lifecycle
description:
Create `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/listener.rs` and supporting modules implementing the Snowflake HTTP wire protocol session layer. This bead covers everything EXCEPT query execution and result encoding (those are bd-sf.2 and bd-sf.3).

## Crate setup

New crate `crates/twinning-snowflake/` following the same pattern as `crates/twinning-rest/`. Add `snowflake` feature to the binary crate `Cargo.toml`:
```toml
[features]
snowflake = ["dep:twinning-snowflake"]
all = ["rest", "postgres", "mcp", "snowflake"]
```

Dependencies for the new crate:
- `twinning-kernel` (path dep)
- `axum`, `tokio`, `tower` (HTTP server)
- `serde`, `serde_json` (JSON)
- `flate2` (gzip decompression — new dependency)
- `uuid` with v4 feature (query IDs)
- `sha2` (catalog hash)

## HTTP Routes

Five routes. All paths must match exactly what the connectors send:

```
POST /session/v1/login-request
POST /session                         (handles ?delete=true for logout)
POST /session/token-request           (session token renewal)
POST /session/heartbeat
POST /queries/v1/abort-request        (stub → success)
GET  /monitoring/queries/{sfqid}      (async query status check)
```

The query route (`POST /queries/v1/query-request`) is wired in bd-sf.3 — leave it as a 501 stub here.

## Gzip Decompression (mandatory)

Python connector gzips request bodies; Go connector does NOT. Check `Content-Encoding: gzip` header before parsing:

```rust
let body = if headers.get("content-encoding").map(|v| v == "gzip").unwrap_or(false) {
    let mut decoder = flate2::read::GzDecoder::new(body.as_ref());
    let mut decompressed = Vec::new();
    decoder.read_to_end(&mut decompressed)?;
    decompressed
} else {
    body.to_vec()
};
```

## Session Storage

```rust
pub struct SnowflakeSession {
    pub token: String,
    pub master_token: String,
    pub session_id: i64,
    pub database_name: String,
    pub schema_name: String,
    pub warehouse_name: String,
    pub role_name: String,
    pub created_at: Instant,
}

pub struct SnowflakeSharedState {
    pub catalog: Arc<SnowflakeCatalog>,           // from bd-sf.2
    pub sessions: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, SnowflakeSession>>>,
    pub results_cache: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, QueryResult>>>,  // sfqid → result for monitoring
}
```

Token format: `secrets::token_urlsafe(32)` equivalent — use `uuid::Uuid::new_v4().to_string().replace("-", "")` for simplicity, or use `rand` crate for URL-safe base64. Any opaque string works; the client treats it as opaque.

Session ID: `rand::random::<i64>().abs()` — just a positive i64.

## Authorization Header Extraction

**Exact formula** (from fakesnow source: `auth[17:-1]`):
```rust
fn extract_token(headers: &HeaderMap) -> Option<&str> {
    let auth = headers.get("authorization")?.to_str().ok()?;
    // Format: "Snowflake Token="<token>""
    // Strip 17-char prefix "Snowflake Token=\"" and trailing "\""
    auth.strip_prefix("Snowflake Token=\"")?.strip_suffix("\"")
}
```

Missing Authorization header → HTTP 401, body:
```json
{"data": null, "code": "390101", "message": "No token provided", "success": false}
```

Unknown token → HTTP 401, body:
```json
{"data": null, "code": "390104", "message": "Token expired", "success": false}
```

## POST /session/v1/login-request

**URL query params** (ignore these, just accept any body regardless):
`databaseName`, `schemaName`, `warehouse`, `roleName`, `requestId`, `request_guid`

**Request body** (extract what we need):
```rust
#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct LoginRequestData {
    #[serde(rename = "SESSION_PARAMETERS", default)]
    session_parameters: serde_json::Value,
    #[serde(rename = "ACCOUNT_NAME", default)]
    account_name: String,
    #[serde(rename = "LOGIN_NAME", default)]
    login_name: String,
}
#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct LoginRequest {
    data: LoginRequestData,
}
```

No credential validation — accept everything.

**Response** (exact structure, confirmed from fakesnow + JDBC compatibility notes):

The login response uses lowercase `"data"` key (only exec responses use uppercase `"Data"`).

```json
{
  "data": {
    "token": "<session_token>",
    "validityInSeconds": 3600,
    "masterToken": "<master_token>",
    "masterValidityInSeconds": 14400,
    "sessionId": 12345678901234,
    "displayUserName": "USER",
    "serverVersion": "8.40.2",
    "firstLogin": false,
    "healthCheckInterval": 3600,
    "parameters": [
      {"name": "AUTOCOMMIT", "value": true},
      {"name": "CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE_HEARTBEAT_FREQUENCY", "value": 3600},
      {"name": "TIMEZONE", "value": "UTC"},
      {"name": "TIMESTAMP_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF3 TZHTZM"},
      {"name": "TIMESTAMP_NTZ_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF9"},
      {"name": "TIMESTAMP_LTZ_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF9 TZHTZM"},
      {"name": "TIMESTAMP_TZ_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": ""},
      {"name": "DATE_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD"},
      {"name": "TIME_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "HH24:MI:SS"},
      {"name": "BINARY_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "HEX"},
      {"name": "CLIENT_TELEMETRY_ENABLED", "value": true},
      {"name": "PYTHON_CONNECTOR_QUERY_RESULT_FORMAT", "value": "arrow"}
    ],
    "sessionInfo": {
      "databaseName": "TWINDB",
      "schemaName": "PUBLIC",
      "warehouseName": "TWIN_WH",
      "roleName": "SYSADMIN"
    }
  },
  "code": null,
  "message": null,
  "success": true
}
```

**Critical notes:**
- `AUTOCOMMIT` in `parameters` is required or JDBC driver fails (issue #178)
- `sessionInfo` is required or .NET connector fails (issue #221)
- `PYTHON_CONNECTOR_QUERY_RESULT_FORMAT: "arrow"` tells Python connector to use Arrow for results

## POST /session (logout)

Parse query string for `delete=true`. Remove token from sessions map.
```json
{"data": null, "code": null, "message": null, "success": true}
```
Note: The URL may have additional query params (`requestId`, `request_guid`) — check only for `delete=true`, not equality (issue #176 in fakesnow).

## POST /session/token-request

Authorization uses the **master token** (not the session token).

Request body:
```json
{"oldSessionToken": "...", "requestType": "RENEW"}
```

Response: issue a new session token, keep the master token:
```json
{
  "data": {
    "sessionToken": "<new_session_token>",
    "validityInSecondsST": 3600,
    "masterToken": "<same_master_token>",
    "validityInSecondsMT": 14400,
    "sessionId": 12345678901234
  },
  "success": true
}
```

## POST /session/heartbeat

No body. Auth required.
```json
{"success": true}
```

## POST /queries/v1/abort-request

No auth required. Always succeed.
```json
{"success": true}
```

## GET /monitoring/queries/{sfqid}

Auth required. Check `results_cache` for the sfqid.
- Found: `{"data": {"queries": [{"status": "SUCCESS"}]}, "success": true}`
- Not found: `{"data": {"queries": []}, "success": true}`

## SnowflakeConfig + CLI

```
twinning snowflake --schema schema.sql   [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port 9876]
                                          [--run <cmd>] [--report <file>] [--json]
```

Add `twinning snowflake` subcommand to `src/cli.rs` gated by `#[cfg(feature = "snowflake")]`.

## Startup output (non-json mode)

```
Snowflake twin ready — 12 tables, 847 rows materialized
account: fakesnow  host: 127.0.0.1:9876
```

## Tests

- POST login with gzip body → valid login response with all required parameters fields
- POST login without gzip (Go connector path) → same valid response
- POST query without auth → 401 code 390104
- POST logout → session removed, subsequent query returns 401
- Token extraction: verify `Snowflake Token="abc123"` → "abc123"

## Files touched
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/Cargo.toml` (new)
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/lib.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/listener.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/session.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-snowflake/src/config.rs` (new)
- `Cargo.toml` (add twinning-snowflake workspace member + snowflake feature)
notes: Snowflake session skeleton and root wiring are present: crates/twinning-snowflake implements the Axum session lifecycle, gzip login body handling, token extraction/renewal/logout/heartbeat/abort/monitoring, and query 501 stub; root Cargo/CLI/config/lib dispatch for the snowflake feature is wired. Verification on 2026-05-18: cargo fmt --check; cargo test -p twinning-snowflake; cargo test --no-default-features --features snowflake; cargo clippy --no-default-features --features snowflake --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo run --no-default-features --features snowflake -- snowflake --schema tests/fixtures/bootstrap_cli/schema.sql --run 'true' --json.

title: REST POST /reminders.add rejects Slack string time from request schema
description:
Autonomous REST e2e campaign finding from the public Slack Web API OpenAPI 3.0.0 spec.

Spec URL:
https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/slack.com/1.7.0/openapi.json

Local repro setup:
```bash
curl -L --fail https://api.apis.guru/v2/specs/slack.com/1.7.0/openapi.json -o /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json
cargo run -- rest --spec /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json --serve --port 18199
python3 /tmp/slack_web_api_client.py http://127.0.0.1:18199 /tmp/slack_web_api_openapi.json
```

Exact curl repro:
```bash
curl -i -X POST "http://127.0.0.1:18199/reminders.add" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data "text=hello&time=tomorrow"
```

Actual response:
```http
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
content-type: application/json

{"code":"type_mismatch","detail":"field `time` expected integer but received string","expected":"integer","field":"time","received":"string"}
```

Expected response/output:
The Slack request body schema for `POST /reminders.add` declares `time` as a string and describes natural-language values such as `tomorrow` as valid. The twin should validate the request body against the request schema and either accept the mutation or refuse a genuinely unsupported shape. It should not reject a spec-valid request field because the target response resource models `time` as an integer.

Campaign client excerpt:
```text
FAIL POST /reminders.add unexpected status: status=400, body={"code":"type_mismatch","detail":"field `time` expected integer but received string",...}
FAIL POST /usergroups.users.update unexpected status: status=400, body={"code":"type_mismatch","detail":"field `users` expected string but received array",...}
```

Root cause hypothesis:
`src/protocol/rest/normalize.rs::mutation_fields_from_object()` appears to coerce overlapping request fields using the target resource column type rather than the request body schema type. For Slack, the request schema allows `time: string` while the response/target resource has an integer `time`, so `normalize_insert()` rejects a spec-valid form body before mutation. This is related to `bd-34j8` request-vs-target schema validation, but this case is an overlapping-field type conflict rather than a request-only field being skipped.

title: Attach target-side evidence identities to twin-pair proof reports
description: Close the target-side evidence gap from PLAN_TWINNING_FUTURES. Extend the proof report model so Twin B can reference raw verify/benchmark/assess artifact identities without twinning interpreting their policy meaning. Acceptance: schema/report fields for optional evidence identities, fixture-backed report test, docs clarifying ownership boundaries, and no pseudo-score aggregation.
close_reason: Completed: twin-pair proof reports now carry optional raw target verify/benchmark/assess evidence identities without scoring or interpreting them.

title: Implement minimal extended-query flow for parameterized canaries
description:
Context:
The extended-query lane is still too broad for one sitting if Parse/Bind state and Execute/Sync dispatch stay bundled. This issue is now the roll-up for those two execution seams only.

Deliverables:
- Parse/Bind state and refusal handling for the declared prepared-statement subset
- Describe/Execute/Sync dispatch over prepared state for the declared portal subset

acceptance_criteria:

2. Parameterized canaries complete the declared extended-query path without widening the subset.
3. This issue remains a roll-up only.
notes:

Reservation guidance: roll-up only; close via child beads.

title: bd-mcp.1: McpCatalog — catalog types and loading from live server or static manifest
description:
Create `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/catalog.rs` with the MCP catalog types and two loading paths. This is the foundation bead — everything else depends on having a well-typed McpCatalog.

## Types to define

```rust
pub struct McpTool {
    pub name: String,
    pub description: Option<String>,
    pub input_schema: JsonValue,      // the JSON Schema for tool arguments
    pub output_schema: Option<JsonValue>, // present on some tools; drives stub executability
}

pub struct McpResource {
    pub uri: Option<String>,
    pub uri_template: Option<String>,
    pub name: String,
    pub mime_type: Option<String>,
}

pub struct McpPromptArgument {
    pub name: String,
    pub description: Option<String>,
    pub required: Option<bool>,
}

pub struct McpPrompt {
    pub name: String,
    pub description: Option<String>,
    pub arguments: Vec<McpPromptArgument>,
}

pub struct McpServerInfo {
    pub name: String,
    pub version: String,
}

pub struct McpCatalog {
    pub server_info: McpServerInfo,
    pub tools: Vec<McpTool>,
    pub resources: Vec<McpResource>,
    pub prompts: Vec<McpPrompt>,
    pub catalog_hash: String,     // SHA-256 of serialized catalog for report/snapshot
    pub source: McpCatalogSource, // LiveServer(command) or Manifest(path)
}

pub enum McpCatalogSource {
    LiveServer { command: String },
    Manifest { path: String },
}
```

## Loading from a live MCP server

`pub fn load_mcp_catalog_from_server(command: &str) -> RefusalResult<McpCatalog>`

Spawn the command as a subprocess with stdin/stdout piped (same pattern as in listener.rs where it uses `Command::new` with `Stdio::piped`). Send the MCP initialization sequence over stdio as newline-delimited JSON-RPC 2.0:

1. Send `initialize`: `{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2024-11-05","capabilities":{},"clientInfo":{"name":"twinning","version":"0.1.0"}}}`
2. Send `initialized` notification (no response expected): `{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"notifications/initialized"}`
3. Send `tools/list`: `{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/list"}`
4. Send `resources/list`: `{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":3,"method":"resources/list"}`
5. Send `prompts/list`: `{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":4,"method":"prompts/list"}`
6. Read responses for ids 1-4, parse, build catalog
7. Kill the subprocess

On subprocess spawn failure or JSON parse failure: return RefusalEnvelope with kind `catalog_load_failed` and the underlying error message.

Set a 10-second read timeout per response — if the server doesn't respond in time, kill and refuse.

## Loading from a static manifest

`pub fn load_mcp_catalog_from_manifest(path: &Path) -> RefusalResult<McpCatalog>`

Parse a JSON file with the shape:
```json
{
  "serverInfo": { "name": "...", "version": "..." },
  "tools": [ { "name": "...", "description": "...", "inputSchema": {...} } ],
  "resources": [ { "uri": "...", "name": "...", "mimeType": "..." } ],
  "prompts": [ { "name": "...", "description": "...", "arguments": [...] } ]
}
```
This is the static alternative when no live server is available (useful for CI, offline testing).

## Catalog hash

SHA-256 of the stable JSON serialization of the catalog (tools + resources + prompts in sorted order). Same pattern as `spec_hash` in RestCatalog / spec.rs.

## Module wiring

Create `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/mod.rs` exporting `catalog`. Add `#[cfg(feature = "mcp")] pub mod mcp;` to `crates/twinning-rest/src/lib.rs`.

Add `mcp` feature to `crates/twinning-rest/Cargo.toml`:
```toml
[features]
mcp = []
```

No new crate dependencies needed — `serde_json` and `sha2` already present in twinning-rest.

## Tests

Unit test: parse a known manifest JSON → assert tool count, resource count, catalog_hash is stable across two calls with same input.

Integration test: if `which npx` is available, spawn a real MCP server (e.g., `npx @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /tmp`) and verify catalog loads with at least one tool.

## Files touched

- `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/mod.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-rest/src/mcp/catalog.rs` (new)
- `crates/twinning-rest/src/lib.rs` (add mcp mod)
- `crates/twinning-rest/Cargo.toml` (add mcp feature)
close_reason: Completed MCP catalog types and manifest/live-server loaders

title: twinning snowflake: E_RUNTIME_IO at snowflake_listener_ready — receiving on a closed channel
description:
# Finding: `twinning snowflake` exits immediately with `E_RUNTIME_IO: receiving on a closed channel`

**Discovered:** 2026-05-18  
**Version:** 0.3.1 (upgraded from 0.3.0 via `cmdrvl/homebrew-tap`)  
**Platform:** macOS x86_64-apple-darwin  
**snowflake-connector-python:** 3.17.4

---

## What happens

Every invocation of `twinning snowflake --schema <FILE>` exits immediately with
a REFUSAL at the `snowflake_listener_ready` stage:

```
REFUSAL [E_RUNTIME_IO]
Live runtime failed during `snowflake_listener_ready`.
detail: {"error":"receiving on a closed channel","stage":"snowflake_listener_ready"}
```

As JSON (`--json` flag):

```json
{
  "version": "twinning.v0",
  "outcome": "REFUSAL",
  "refusal": {
    "code": "E_RUNTIME_IO",
    "message": "Live runtime failed during `snowflake_listener_ready`.",
    "detail": {
      "error": "receiving on a closed channel",
      "stage": "snowflake_listener_ready"
    },
    "next_command": null
  }
}
```

All invocation forms hit this path: bare `--schema FILE`, `--json`, `--serve`,
`--report FILE`. Exit code 2.

---

## Reproduction

```bash
# Minimal schema
cat > /tmp/test.sql <<'EOF'
CREATE TABLE rca_sample_transactions (
    deal_id    TEXT,
    price      NUMERIC,
    status_dt  DATE
);
EOF

twinning snowflake --schema /tmp/test.sql
# → REFUSAL [E_RUNTIME_IO] receiving on a closed channel

twinning snowflake --schema /tmp/test.sql --serve
# → same

twinning snowflake --schema /tmp/test.sql --json
# → same (JSON envelope)
```

---

## The Snowflake wire protocol IS partially working

Despite the crash, the HTTP server starts and handles at least one request before
exiting. During the brief window (< 1 second) the process is alive, the login
endpoint responds correctly:

```bash
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:9876/session/v1/login-request \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"data":{"ACCOUNT_NAME":"fakesnow","LOGIN_NAME":"x","PASSWORD":"x",
       "CLIENT_APP_ID":"PythonConnector","CLIENT_APP_VERSION":"3.17.4"}}'
```

Returns a fully-formed Snowflake session response:

```json
{
  "code": null,
  "data": {
    "displayUserName": "X",
    "firstLogin": false,
    "healthCheckInterval": 3600,
    "masterToken": "3116e5b54ee5461397a6641195d1dca4",
    "masterValidityInSeconds": 14400,
    "parameters": [
      { "name": "AUTOCOMMIT", "value": true },
      { "name": "CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE_HEARTBEAT_FREQUENCY", "value": 3600 },
      { "name": "TIMEZONE", "value": "UTC" },
      { "name": "TIMESTAMP_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF3 TZHTZM" },
      { "name": "TIMESTAMP_NTZ_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF9" }
    ],
    "token": "b1a6f67017534e5e913a..."
  },
  "success": true
}
```

The `snowflake-connector-python` 3.17.4 session auth completes (`success: true`,
token returned). The connector then fails when it tries to execute a query because
the server has already exited by then.

Connector error on query attempt:

```
snowflake/connector/cursor.py", line 1195, in _init_result_and_meta
    ResultMetadataV2.from_column(col) for col in data["rowtype"]
KeyError: 'rowtype'
```

This is secondary — the connector is hitting a dead server or a partial response.
The root bug is the channel crash, not the `rowtype` field.

---

## Root cause hypothesis

`snowflake_listener_ready` sounds like a Tokio oneshot or mpsc channel used to
signal from the listener task back to the main thread that the server is bound
and accepting connections. The error "receiving on a closed channel" means the
receiver end was dropped before (or just as) the sender tried to send.

Likely cause: the main thread drops the receiver before awaiting it, or a race
between the listener startup task and the main runtime shutdown path. The fact
that the HTTP server does handle one request before crashing suggests the listener
binds successfully, but the readiness signaling path has a bug.

---

## Schema used during testing (MSCI RCA Transactions, derived from listing metadata)

```sql
-- MSCI Real Assets Transactions — derived from listing metadata (GZ1M6ZQE25B)
-- Sample tables: London, Dallas, Singapore. Schema is identical across regions.

CREATE TABLE rca_sample_transactions (
    propertykey_id               TEXT,
    deal_id                      TEXT,
    property_id                  TEXT,
    propertyname                 TEXT,
    address_tx                   TEXT,
    city_tx                      TEXT,
    price                        NUMERIC,
    sqmeters_nb                  NUMERIC,
    status_dt                    DATE,
    transtype_tx                 TEXT,
    maintype                     TEXT,
    buyer1_principal_entity_tx   TEXT,
    seller1_principal_entity_tx  TEXT,
    deal_update_dt               TIMESTAMP,
    property_update_dt           TIMESTAMP
);
```

Bootstrap report for this schema (from `twinning postgres`, which works):

```json
{
  "schema": {
    "hash": "sha256:4a7e0ae7346b7098677daa6fcc563ee0c14e1377b4200f30912ed5c0f0ab0c06",
    "table_count": 1,
    "column_count": 15
  }
}
```

---

## What `--describe` says is implemented

From `twinning --describe` (0.3.1):

> "Snowflake HTTP wire protocol twin: Arrow IPC rowsetBase64 encoding,
> SHOW/DESCRIBE meta-queries, DDL catalog introspection, session lifecycle"

The session lifecycle piece (login endpoint) is confirmed working. The query
dispatch and listener stability are not.

---

## Workaround

None. The `snowflake` subcommand is not usable in 0.3.1. `twinning postgres`
bootstraps fine against the same schema and can reach bootstrap READY, but
speaks pgwire not the Snowflake HTTP wire protocol.

comments.author: cairn
comments.text:
# Finding: `twinning snowflake` exits immediately with `E_RUNTIME_IO: receiving on a closed channel`

**Discovered:** 2026-05-18  
**Version:** 0.3.1 (upgraded from 0.3.0 via `cmdrvl/homebrew-tap`)  
**Platform:** macOS x86_64-apple-darwin  
**snowflake-connector-python:** 3.17.4

---

## What happens

Every invocation of `twinning snowflake --schema <FILE>` exits immediately with
a REFUSAL at the `snowflake_listener_ready` stage:

```
REFUSAL [E_RUNTIME_IO]
Live runtime failed during `snowflake_listener_ready`.
detail: {"error":"receiving on a closed channel","stage":"snowflake_listener_ready"}
```

As JSON (`--json` flag):

```json
{
  "version": "twinning.v0",
  "outcome": "REFUSAL",
  "refusal": {
    "code": "E_RUNTIME_IO",
    "message": "Live runtime failed during `snowflake_listener_ready`.",
    "detail": {
      "error": "receiving on a closed channel",
      "stage": "snowflake_listener_ready"
    },
    "next_command": null
  }
}
```

All invocation forms hit this path: bare `--schema FILE`, `--json`, `--serve`,
`--report FILE`. Exit code 2.

---

## Reproduction

```bash
# Minimal schema
cat > /tmp/test.sql <<'EOF'
CREATE TABLE rca_sample_transactions (
    deal_id    TEXT,
    price      NUMERIC,
    status_dt  DATE
);
EOF

twinning snowflake --schema /tmp/test.sql
# → REFUSAL [E_RUNTIME_IO] receiving on a closed channel

twinning snowflake --schema /tmp/test.sql --serve
# → same

twinning snowflake --schema /tmp/test.sql --json
# → same (JSON envelope)
```

---

## The Snowflake wire protocol IS partially working

Despite the crash, the HTTP server starts and handles at least one request before
exiting. During the brief window (< 1 second) the process is alive, the login
endpoint responds correctly:

```bash
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:9876/session/v1/login-request \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"data":{"ACCOUNT_NAME":"fakesnow","LOGIN_NAME":"x","PASSWORD":"x",
       "CLIENT_APP_ID":"PythonConnector","CLIENT_APP_VERSION":"3.17.4"}}'
```

Returns a fully-formed Snowflake session response:

```json
{
  "code": null,
  "data": {
    "displayUserName": "X",
    "firstLogin": false,
    "healthCheckInterval": 3600,
    "masterToken": "3116e5b54ee5461397a6641195d1dca4",
    "masterValidityInSeconds": 14400,
    "parameters": [
      { "name": "AUTOCOMMIT", "value": true },
      { "name": "CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE_HEARTBEAT_FREQUENCY", "value": 3600 },
      { "name": "TIMEZONE", "value": "UTC" },
      { "name": "TIMESTAMP_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF3 TZHTZM" },
      { "name": "TIMESTAMP_NTZ_OUTPUT_FORMAT", "value": "YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF9" }
    ],
    "token": "b1a6f67017534e5e913a..."
  },
  "success": true
}
```

The `snowflake-connector-python` 3.17.4 session auth completes (`success: true`,
token returned). The connector then fails when it tries to execute a query because
the server has already exited by then.

Connector error on query attempt:

```
snowflake/connector/cursor.py", line 1195, in _init_result_and_meta
    ResultMetadataV2.from_column(col) for col in data["rowtype"]
KeyError: 'rowtype'
```

This is secondary — the connector is hitting a dead server or a partial response.
The root bug is the channel crash, not the `rowtype` field.

---

## Root cause hypothesis

`snowflake_listener_ready` sounds like a Tokio oneshot or mpsc channel used to
signal from the listener task back to the main thread that the server is bound
and accepting connections. The error "receiving on a closed channel" means the
receiver end was dropped before (or just as) the sender tried to send.

Likely cause: the main thread drops the receiver before awaiting it, or a race
between the listener startup task and the main runtime shutdown path. The fact
that the HTTP server does handle one request before crashing suggests the listener
binds successfully, but the readiness signaling path has a bug.

---

## Schema used during testing (MSCI RCA Transactions, derived from listing metadata)

```sql
-- MSCI Real Assets Transactions — derived from listing metadata (GZ1M6ZQE25B)
-- Sample tables: London, Dallas, Singapore. Schema is identical across regions.

CREATE TABLE rca_sample_transactions (
    propertykey_id               TEXT,
    deal_id                      TEXT,
    property_id                  TEXT,
    propertyname                 TEXT,
    address_tx                   TEXT,
    city_tx                      TEXT,
    price                        NUMERIC,
    sqmeters_nb                  NUMERIC,
    status_dt                    DATE,
    transtype_tx                 TEXT,
    maintype                     TEXT,
    buyer1_principal_entity_tx   TEXT,
    seller1_principal_entity_tx  TEXT,
```

Bootstrap report for this schema (from `twinning postgres`, which works):

```json
{
  "schema": {
    "hash": "sha256:4a7e0ae7346b7098677daa6fcc563ee0c14e1377b4200f30912ed5c0f0ab0c06",
```
